title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
The lectures in this course introduce each concept conceptually: enough mechanism to configure, use, and debug it, without the full architectural detail. This page maps every simplified concept to the authoritative document where the complete story lives, so that a curious student — or a future project — can go one level deeper with a known-good source instead of a random blog.
Each lecture gets its own section. Entries are added as lectures are revised.

title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
If a link stops working
Links below point at the current official locations (verified 2026-07-17). If one rots:

ST documents (RM…, PM…, UM…, MB… numbers): search the document number at st.com; the number uniquely identifies the document and survives site reorganizations.
Arm documents (DDI… numbers): search at developer.arm.com.
Books: available through the university library or the cited publisher; Lee & Seshia is free and legal at leeseshia.org.

title: Academic References and Further Reading
section: Primary documents used throughout the course
text:
Primary documents used throughout the course



Short name
Full citation
What it is for




RM0368
STMicroelectronics, RM0368: STM32F401xB/C and STM32F401xD/E advanced Arm®-based 32-bit MCUs — Reference Manual (PDF)
The register-level truth for every peripheral on our chip: GPIO, SYSCFG, EXTI, timers, ADC, I2C, SPI, DMA


PM0214
STMicroelectronics, PM0214: STM32 Cortex®-M4 MCUs and MPUs programming manual (PDF)
The processor core seen from software: instruction set, exception model, NVIC, SysTick, core registers


UM1724
STMicroelectronics, UM1724: STM32 Nucleo-64 boards (MB1136) — User Manual (PDF)
The board itself: B1 and LD2 circuits, jumpers, ST-LINK, power, board revision differences


MB1136 schematic
STMicroelectronics, MB1136 schematic pack, rev C-04 (PDF)
The actual circuit diagram of the Nucleo-F401RE — the version used in lecture


UM1725
STMicroelectronics, UM1725: Description of STM32F4 HAL and low-layer drivers (PDF)
Signature and behavior of every HAL_… function the labs use


Yiu 2014
J. Yiu, The Definitive Guide to ARM® Cortex®-M3 and Cortex®-M4 Processors, 3rd ed., Newnes, 2014. ISBN 978-0-12-408082-9
The standard book-length treatment of the core: pipeline, exceptions, NVIC, low-power, debug


DDI 0403
Arm Ltd., ARMv7-M Architecture Reference Manual (Arm DDI 0403)
The formal architecture specification — for when even Yiu is not precise enough


Lee & Seshia
E. A. Lee and S. A. Seshia, Introduction to Embedded Systems: A Cyber-Physical Systems Approach, 2nd ed., MIT Press, 2017 — free PDF
The academic textbook view: interrupts, concurrency, real-time reasoning


White 2024
E. White, Making Embedded Systems, 2nd ed., O’Reilly, 2024
Practical engineering judgment: interrupt hygiene, debouncing, system design patterns


C11
ISO/IEC 9899:2011, Programming languages — C (link is N1570, the final public draft, PDF)
The C language standard; final authority on volatile, integer types, undefined behavior

title: Academic References and Further Reading
section: L01 — Embedded Systems, the C Toolchain, and Debugging
text:
L01 — Embedded Systems, the C Toolchain, and Debugging



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




What an embedded system is — dedicated function + constraints
Lee & Seshia, ch. 1 Introduction (free PDF); White 2024, ch. 1


The Nucleo-F401RE board — ST-LINK, LD2, B1, headers
UM1724 Rev 17, §6 Hardware layout — p. 13; MB1136 C-04 schematic


Processor / MCU / system model
PM0214 Rev 10, §2.1 Programmers model — p. 17; Yiu 2014, ch. 1–2


The toolchain — compile, link, flash, debug
Yiu 2014, ch. 2 (development flow and what CMSIS standardizes); the VS Code build terminal of any generated project shows each stage’s real output


First C statements — main(), declarations, if, while (1)
C11 (N1570), §6.8.4 Selection statements — p. 166; program startup is §5.1.2.2.1

title: Academic References and Further Reading
section: L02 — Values, Decisions, Loops, and Arrays
text:
L02 — Values, Decisions, Loops, and Arrays



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




if / else if / else
C11 (N1570), §6.8.4 Selection statements — p. 166


while and for loops
C11 (N1570), §6.8.5 Iteration statements — p. 168


Arrays and indexing from zero
C11 (N1570), §6.5.2.1 Array subscripting — p. 98 — out-of-range access is undefined behavior, which is why samples[8] is not “just the next value”


uint16_t and friends
C11 (N1570), §7.20 Integer types <stdint.h> — p. 307


Integer division — a / b truncates
C11 §6.5.5 (multiplicative operators): division truncates toward zero

title: Academic References and Further Reading
section: L03 — Functions, Arrays, Strings, and Multiple C Files
text:
L03 — Functions, Arrays, Strings, and Multiple C Files



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




Declarations, definitions, calls
C11 (N1570), §6.9.1 Function definitions — p. 174


Pass by value — parameters are copies
C11 (N1570), §6.5.2.2 Function calls — p. 99


Headers, multiple files, the linker
C11 (N1570), §6.2.2 Linkages of identifiers — p. 54; #include / #define are §6.10 Preprocessing directives — p. 184


Why array parameters need a count — arrays decay to pointers
C11 §6.7.6.3¶7; the L04 pointer material makes this concrete


C strings and \0; strcmp
C11 (N1570), §7.24 String handling <string.h> — p. 380

title: Academic References and Further Reading
section: L04 — Pointers, Structures, Bits, Registers, and GPIO
text:
L04 — Pointers, Structures, Bits, Registers, and GPIO
The lecture builds GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 5); from its parts: addresses, pointers, structures, masks, and the GPIO registers.



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




Addresses and memory — “memory is a numbered collection of storage locations”
PM0214 Rev 10, §2.2 Memory model — p. 28: the real Cortex-M4 memory map, including where peripherals live


Pointers: & and *
C11 (N1570), §6.5.3.2 Address and indirection operators — p. 106


Structures and . / ->
C11 (N1570), §6.7.2.1 Structure and union specifiers — p. 130


Bitwise operators, masks, the U suffix
C11 (N1570), §6.5.7 Bitwise shift operators — p. 112, then §6.5.10–6.5.12 for &, ^, |; §6.5.7 also explains why shifting signed values can be undefined behavior — the reason the U suffix matters


CMSIS register names — “the device header supplies the definition”
The actual stm32f401xe.h device header (GPIO_TypeDef, GPIOA_BASE); Yiu 2014, ch. 2 on what CMSIS standardizes


volatile register members
C11 (N1570), §6.7.3 — p. 140; M. Barr, How to Use C’s volatile Keyword


Peripheral clock gating — “enable the clock first”
RM0368 Rev 6, RCC_AHB1ENR — p. 118; the RCC chapter (§6) explains why clocks are gated: power


GPIO block, MODER, BSRR
RM0368 Rev 6, §8 GPIO — p. 146; registers: GPIOx_MODER — p. 158, GPIOx_BSRR — p. 161 — including what the lecture skips: OTYPER, OSPEEDR, PUPDR

title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
L05 — GPIO and Hardware Abstraction
The lecture connects every HAL name to the register behavior taught in L04: same hardware, second interface.



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




The HAL GPIO API — GPIO_InitTypeDef, HAL_GPIO_Init, WritePin / TogglePin / ReadPin
UM1725 Rev 8, GPIO functions — p. 447


What the HAL actually executes
The source of stm32f4xx_hal_gpio.c — HAL_GPIO_WritePin is a single BSRR write


HAL names → register bits — GPIO_MODE_OUTPUT_PP, GPIO_NOPULL, speed
RM0368 Rev 6, §8 GPIO — p. 146; registers: GPIOx_MODER — p. 158, GPIOx_PUPDR — p. 159, GPIOx_BSRR — p. 161


Floating inputs, pull-up / pull-down
RM0368 §8.3 (I/O port control); the electronics context in any circuits text — the lecture’s claim is only that an undriven CMOS input is undefined


B1 circuit — external pull-up, active-low
MB1136 schematic rev C-04; UM1724 Rev 17, push-buttons — p. 24; confirmed against the ST Nucleo BSP source


Why abstraction layers exist — and what they cost
White 2024, ch. 2 (creating system diagrams / layering); Yiu 2014, ch. 2 (what CMSIS standardizes and why)


Polling and its limits — “the loop asks again and again”
Forward pointer: the L06 row Polling vs. interrupts above

title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
L06 — GPIO Interrupts
The lecture walks one button press through five plain-language stages (PRESS → NOTICE → ASK → LOOK UP → RUN YOUR CODE). Each simplification below names the document that removes it.
Page-anchored links (#page=N) below open the PDF at the cited page in the browser viewer. Page numbers were verified against the document revisions named in each entry (2026-07-17); if ST publishes a new revision, the section numbers in the text remain the reliable pointer.



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




Polling vs. interrupts — “polling asks again and again”
Lee & Seshia, ch. 10 Input and Output (interrupt mechanisms, latency, and when polling is actually the better design); Yiu 2014, §4.5


What really happens between ASK and RUN YOUR CODE — register stacking, vector fetch, exception entry/return (we skipped this entirely)
PM0214 Rev 10, §2.3 Exception model — p. 37; Yiu 2014, ch. 8 (including the 12-cycle entry sequence and tail-chaining); DDI 0403, §B1.5 for the formal definition


EXTI — “a set of hardware edge detectors”
RM0368 Rev 6, §10 EXTI — p. 205: block diagram, IMR/EMR/RTSR/FTSR/SWIER/PR registers, the event-vs-interrupt distinction we did not cover


SYSCFG routing — “a four-bit field selects the port”
RM0368 Rev 6, SYSCFG_EXTICR4 — p. 143


Pending bit and write-1-to-clear
RM0368 Rev 6, EXTI_PR — p. 211; the same rc_w1 convention appears in many STM32 peripherals


NVIC — “enable, priority, deliver”
PM0214 Rev 10, §4.2 NVIC — p. 208; Yiu 2014, ch. 7 — including what we postponed: priority levels, preemption, sub-priority, nesting


Vector table and the startup file
PM0214 Rev 10, §2.3.4 Vector table — p. 40; Yiu 2014, §4.4 and §8.5; the generated startup_stm32f401retx.s in every CubeMX-generated project is the concrete instance


EXTI15_10_IRQHandler / HAL helper / callback chain
UM1725 Rev 8, GPIO functions — p. 447 (HAL_GPIO_EXTI_IRQHandler, HAL_GPIO_EXTI_Callback); the ~10-line source of the helper in stm32f4xx_hal_gpio.c is worth reading in the lab


volatile — “read the stored value each time”
C11 (N1570), §6.7.3 — p. 140; M. Barr, How to Use C’s volatile Keyword, Barr Group. Note what the lecture only hints at: volatile does not make read-modify-write atomic — see Lee & Seshia, ch. 11 on shared-variable concurrency


ISR ↔︎ main() shared-variable pattern (button_event)
Lee & Seshia, ch. 11 (race conditions, atomicity); White 2024, interrupt chapter (keep-ISRs-short discipline and its reasons)


Switch bounce and the 80 ms guard
J. Ganssle, A Guide to Debouncing, 2nd ed., The Ganssle Group, 2008: measured bounce data for real switches (the “settles within ~10 ms” claim comes from here), plus hardware and software debounce alternatives


B1 circuit — external pull-up, active-low
MB1136 schematic rev C-04; UM1724 Rev 17, push-buttons — p. 24 — check the board revision: later MB1136 revisions changed the B1 circuit, which affects the pull configuration the lab assumes

title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
L07 — Timers
The lecture builds one mental model — clock → PSC → CNT → ARR → update event — and consumes it by polling, by interrupt, and as a 1 ms tick.



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




The time base — prescaler, counter, auto-reload, update event
RM0368 Rev 6, §13 TIM2 to TIM5 — p. 316; registers (§13.4 — p. 353)


Where the 16 MHz comes from — clock tree, APB timer-clock ×2 rule
RM0368 Rev 6, §6.2 Clocks — p. 94


HAL TIM base API — handle, Base_Init, Base_Start_IT, callback
UM1725 Rev 8, TIM functions — p. 1065


The interrupt path — reused from L06
The L06 rows above; the only new element is the request name TIM2_IRQn


Tick-based scheduling and why blocking delays hurt
Lee & Seshia, ch. 10–11; White 2024, timer and main-loop chapters


SysTick — the Cortex-M core timer behind HAL_Delay (not covered)
PM0214 Rev 10, §4.5 SysTick timer — p. 246

title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
L08 — Pulse-Width Modulation
The lecture adds the capture/compare channel to L07’s time base and uses it in the compare direction: ARR owns the frequency, CCR owns the duty.



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




PWM mode — pin high while CNT < CCR
RM0368 Rev 6, §13.3.9 PWM mode — p. 336, including what the lecture skips: center-aligned mode, PWM mode 2


Channel registers
TIMx_CCMR1 — p. 362, TIMx_CCER — p. 366, TIMx_CCR1 — p. 367


Handing the pin to the timer — alternate function
RM0368 Rev 6, GPIOx_AFRL — p. 162; the pin-to-AF table is the STM32F401RE datasheet (DS10086), Alternate function mapping — search the DS number at st.com


HAL PWM API
UM1725 Rev 8, TIM functions — p. 1065


The servo pulse contract — 50 Hz frame, 1–2 ms pulse
Hobby-servo convention, not an ST document: any RC-servo datasheet (e.g., SG90, S3003) states the same 20 ms / 1–2 ms numbers


Why the average works — PWM into slow loads
White 2024, output/actuator chapters; the RC-filter demo linked from the deck shows it interactively

title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
L09 — Input Capture
The same channel, reversed: an edge on the pin captures the free-running counter into CCR — a hardware timestamp.



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




Input capture — edge copies CNT into CCR
RM0368 Rev 6, §13.3.5 Input capture mode — p. 333


Automated duty measurement
RM0368 Rev 6, §13.3.6 PWM input mode — p. 334 — the hardware version of the paired-channel trick, at the cost of the whole timer


Direct vs indirect inputs, the filter, overcapture
TIMx_CCMR1 — p. 362 (CCxS, ICF), TIMx_CCER — p. 366; overcapture flags in the TIMx_SR description


HAL capture API
UM1725 Rev 8, TIM functions — p. 1065; HAL_TIM_ReadCapturedValue — p. 1097


Ultrasonic ranging — echo width → distance
HC-SR04 datasheet (community-published; no ST equivalent): 10 µs trigger, echo width, the /58 cm constant

title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
L10 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion
The lecture builds counts↔︎volts fluency on one convention (mV = raw × 3300 / 4095), one clock (PCLK2 ÷ 2 = 8 MHz), and one converter (the SAR core).



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




The ADC itself — 12-bit SAR, one converter on the F401
RM0368 Rev 6, §11 ADC — p. 213


The ADC clock — PCLK2 ÷ 2 = 8 MHz, 36 MHz ceiling
§11.3.2 ADC clock — p. 215


Channels — 16 external doors + the internal sensors
§11.3.3 Channel selection — p. 215; §11.9 Temperature sensor — p. 226


Single vs continuous conversion
§11.3.4–11.3.5 — p. 216; what the lecture skips: injected channels, the analog watchdog, external triggers (§11.6 — p. 222)


Sampling time and source impedance
§11.5 Channel-wise programmable sampling time — p. 222; the hard electrical limits (max source resistance vs sampling time) are in the STM32F401RE datasheet (DS10086), ADC characteristics


ADC registers
§11.12 — p. 229: ADC_SR — p. 229, ADC_CR1 — p. 230, ADC_CR2 — p. 232, ADC_SMPR2 — p. 234, ADC_SQR3 — p. 237, ADC_DR — p. 239


The analog pin mode — MODER = 11
RM0368 Rev 6, GPIOx_MODER — p. 158


HAL ADC API
UM1725 Rev 8, ADC generic driver — p. 56: HAL_ADC_Start — p. 65, HAL_ADC_PollForConversion / HAL_ADC_Start_IT — p. 66, HAL_ADC_GetValue / HAL_ADC_ConvCpltCallback — p. 68, HAL_ADC_ConfigChannel — p. 69


Sampling theory and aliasing — stated, not proven
Lee & Seshia, sensors/actuators and sampling chapters; White 2024, analog-input chapters

title: Academic References and Further Reading
text:
L11 — The Bridge to Assembly
Every listing in the deck is real output of arm-none-eabi-gcc 14.3 — the toolchain inside STM32CubeCLT — compiled for Cortex-M4 at -O0 and -O2.



Concept as taught
Where the full story is




The register file — R0–R12, SP, LR, PC, xPSR
PM0214 Rev 10, §2.1.3 Core registers — p. 18


Thumb-2 — 16- and 32-bit encodings, the .w suffix
PM0214 Rev 10, §3.1 Instruction set summary — p. 50; §3.3.8 Instruction width selection — p. 68


Memory access — LDR/STR, offsets, PUSH/POP
§3.4 — p. 69; §3.4.2 immediate offset — p. 71; §3.4.7 PUSH and POP — p. 78


Data processing — arithmetic, logic, shifts, CMP, MOV
§3.5 — p. 81: ADD/SUB — p. 83, AND/ORR/EOR/BIC — p. 85, shifts — p. 62, CMP — p. 88, MOV/MVN — p. 89


Branches and conditions — B/BL/BX, condition codes, IT
B, BL, BX, BLX — p. 142; §3.3.7 Conditional execution — p. 65; the -O2 ITE surprise: IT — p. 145


Calling convention — args in r0–r3, result in r0, LR
The Arm AAPCS32 (Procedure Call Standard for the Arm Architecture) — search “AAPCS32” at developer.arm.com; the lecture teaches only the subset visible in the debugger


Interrupt entry stacking — same stack, hardware-driven
the L06 rows above (exception model); the deck only points back to them


What the optimizer may do — folding, inlining, volatile limits
GCC manual, Optimize Options; White 2024, debugging/optimization chapters



Sections for other lectures are added as each lecture is revised to the 2026–2027 pattern.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
section: Welcome to Lecture 14: Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
Welcome to Lecture 14: Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
Showcasing Your Embedded Systems Journey

This is the culmination of your hard work throughout the semester.
You will demonstrate your final projects, applying all the knowledge and skills gained.

Learning Objectives for Lecture 14

Effectively present and demonstrate your final embedded system project.
Articulate the design choices, challenges, and solutions of your project.
Review key concepts learned throughout the EE5203 course.
Explore future learning paths and opportunities in embedded systems.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
The Final Project: Your Capstone
Bringing It All Together

The final project is an opportunity to design, build, and troubleshoot a complete embedded system.
It integrates concepts from various weeks: C programming, GPIO, Timers, Interrupts, ADC, and communication protocols.

Visual Suggestion

Image: A collage of diverse embedded system projects (e.g., smart home device, robot, drone, wearable).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
Project Presentation Guidelines
What to Cover

Introduction: Project title, team members, problem statement.
System Overview: High-level block diagram, how it works.
Hardware Design: Components used, wiring, custom PCBs (if any).
Software Design: Key C modules, algorithms, state machines.
Demonstration: Live demo of the working project.
Challenges & Solutions: What went wrong, how you fixed it.
Future Work: Potential improvements or extensions.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
Effective Presentation Tips
Engaging Your Audience

Practice: Rehearse your presentation multiple times.
Clarity: Explain complex concepts simply.
Visuals: Use clear diagrams, photos, and videos.
Live Demo: Ensure your demo is robust and well-rehearsed.
Time Management: Stick to the allocated time.
Q&A: Be prepared to answer questions thoroughly.

Visual Suggestion

Image: A person giving a presentation with a clear, engaging slide on a screen.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
section: Course Wrap-up: Review of Key Concepts
text:
Course Wrap-up: Review of Key Concepts
A Journey Through Embedded Systems

Let’s revisit the major topics we’ve covered throughout the semester.
This review will consolidate your understanding and highlight the interconnectedness of concepts.

Visual Suggestion

Image: A winding road or path, with milestones representing each week/topic.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
section: Part 1: From Python to Embedded C (Weeks 1-3)
text:
Part 1: From Python to Embedded C (Weeks 1-3)
Foundations of Embedded Programming

Lecture 1: Intro to Microprocessors, Embedded Systems, ARM, C vs. Python.
Lecture 2: C Fundamentals: Data types, operators (especially bitwise), control flow.
Lecture 3: Advanced C: Functions, Pointers, Arrays, struct, typedef (crucial for registers).

Visual Suggestion

Icons: Python snake, C language logo, a CPU chip, a memory block.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
Part 2: ARM Architecture & Peripheral Control (Weeks 4-7)
Interacting with the Hardware

Lecture 4: ARM Cortex-M4 Architecture: Programmer’s model, registers (SP, LR, PC), memory-mapped I/O.
Lecture 5: GPIO & Hardware Abstraction: In-depth configuration, bare-metal vs. HAL.
Lecture 6: Interrupts & the NVIC: Concept, ISRs, priorities, EXTI.
Lecture 7: Timers (TIM): Prescalers, counters, ARR, periodic interrupts, non-blocking delays.

Visual Suggestion

Icons: A CPU core, GPIO pins, an interrupt symbol, a clock/timer icon.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
Part 3: Communication & System Integration (Weeks 9-13)
Connecting to the World

Lecture 9: Pulse Width Modulation (PWM): Duty cycle, frequency, LED fading, servo control.
Lecture 10: Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC): Principles, resolution, sampling, reading sensors.
Lecture 11: The Bridge to Assembly: RISC vs. CISC, ARM instruction set, C to assembly mapping.
Lecture 12: Serial Communication: UART: Asynchronous, baud rate, data frames, PC interfacing.
Lecture 13: Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI: Protocols, master/slave, applications.

Visual Suggestion

Icons: A PWM waveform, an ADC symbol, assembly code, UART/I2C/SPI symbols.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
Key Learning Outcomes Revisited
What You Can Now Do

Write, compile, and debug C programs for embedded microcontrollers.
Understand ARM Cortex-M4 architecture and memory organization.
Configure and control various microprocessor peripherals (GPIO, Timers, ADC, UART, I2C, SPI).
Implement interrupt-driven systems and manage real-time events.
Design, build, and troubleshoot a complete embedded system.
Read and interpret technical documentation (datasheets, reference manuals).

Where to Go From Here?

Advanced Microcontroller Topics: DMA, RTOS (Real-Time Operating Systems), advanced peripherals (USB, Ethernet).
Specific Microcontroller Families: Deeper dive into STM32, ESP32, PIC, AVR.
Embedded Linux: Developing on more powerful embedded processors (e.g., Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
Embedded Systems Specializations
Diverse Fields

Internet of Things (IoT): Smart devices, sensor networks, cloud integration.
Robotics: Motor control, sensor fusion, navigation.
Automotive Embedded Systems: ECU development, CAN bus, autonomous driving.
Medical Devices: Wearables, diagnostic equipment.
Industrial Automation: PLCs, control systems.
Firmware Development: Low-level programming for hardware.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Final Project Presentations & Course Wrap-up
text:
Continuing Your Journey
Resources for Self-Study

Online Courses: Coursera, Udemy, edX (e.g., ARM Mbed, Embedded C courses).
Books: “Embedded Systems Fundamentals with ARM Cortex-M Microcontrollers” (Joseph Yiu), “Make: Electronics” (Charles Platt).
Community Forums: EEVblog, Stack Overflow, manufacturer forums.
Open-Source Projects: Contribute to or learn from existing embedded projects.
Personal Projects: The best way to learn is by doing! Build something you’re passionate about.

The Power of Embedded Systems

Embedded systems are the invisible technology that powers our modern world.
The skills you’ve gained in this course are highly valuable and in demand.
Keep experimenting, keep learning, and keep building!

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
The Problem: CPU Bottlenecks



Scenario: Attempting to send 57,600 pixels to a screen over SPI.
Without DMA (CPU Polling):

CPU reads a byte from RAM.
CPU writes byte to SPI Data Register.
CPU waits for transmission to finish.
Repeat 115,200 times.

Result: The CPU is 100% busy just “moving boxes.”

It cannot process sensor data.
It cannot calculate control loops.






The CPU shouldn’t do manual labor.

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
The Solution: DMA Controller


Direct Memory Access (DMA) is a specialized hardware peripheral that acts like a “data moving co-processor.”

CPU Configures: “Move 100 bytes from Address A to Address B.”
CPU Action: “Start!”
DMA Action: Copies data in the background.
Completion: DMA interrupts the CPU (“Done!”).


Benefit: CPU load drops near 0% during the transfer.





Parallel Processing: DMA works while CPU thinks.

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Hardware View: STM32F4 Bus Matrix

The DMA controller is a Bus Master (just like the CPU).
It can take control of the bus to read/write memory and peripherals.
Arbitration: If CPU and DMA both want the bus, a hardware arbiter manages access (CPU usually gets priority, or they interleave).


STM32 DMA Implementation

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Key DMA Concepts


1. Direction

Memory-to-Peripheral (M2P): RAM → SPI/UART (Sending data)
Peripheral-to-Memory (P2M): ADC/UART → RAM (Receiving data)
Memory-to-Memory (M2M): RAM → RAM (Fast memcpy)


2. Increment Mode

Source Increment: Do we read the next address?

Yes for arrays/buffers.
No for fixed registers (e.g., locking to a GPIO).

Dest Increment: Do we write to the next address?

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
DMA Modes: Normal vs. Circular



Normal Mode:

Transfer N bytes, then stop.
DMA stream disables itself.
Use Case: Sending a screen update or a UART packet.

Circular Mode:

Transfer N bytes, then restart from 0 automatically.
Double Buffering Strategy:

Half-Transfer (HT): DMA fills 2nd half \(\rightarrow\) CPU processes 1st half.
Transfer-Complete (TC): DMA fills 1st half \(\rightarrow\) CPU processes 2nd half.







Circular Buffer Visualization

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Internal Architecture: Controllers & Streams
1. Controllers (The Managers)
The STM32F401 has 2 DMA Controllers:

DMA1: Peripherals on APB1 (slower).

No Memory-to-Memory support.

DMA2: Peripherals on APB2 (faster).

Supports Memory-to-Memory.


2. Streams (The Highway Lanes)

Concept: A Stream is a unidirectional data pipeline.
Capacity: 8 Streams per Controller (16 total).
Rule: A Stream can only handle one task at a time.

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Reference: DMA Mapping Table




DMA Mapping Table

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
DMA Channels & Multiplexing


3. Channels (The Inputs)

Concept: A Channel is a request line from a peripheral (SPI, UART, Timers).
Structure: Each Stream has 8 Channels (0-7) feeding into it.
Function: Channels act as inputs to a multiplexer for that Stream.

4. The Mapping Matrix (The Selector)

The Constraint: You cannot connect any peripheral to any Stream.
Selection: You must select ONE Channel per Stream.
Example: If SPI3_RX and I2C1_RX are on the same stream but different channels, you cannot use them simultaneously on that stream.





Channel Multiplexing

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
DMA Interrupts
Your code interacts with DMA primarily through interrupts (Callbacks):

Transfer Complete (TC): The entire buffer has been moved.

Action: Process the data or prepare the next buffer.

Half Transfer (HT): Halfway point reached.

Action: Useful in Circular Mode to process the first half while DMA fills the second.

Transfer Error (TE): Bus error or configuration mismatch.

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
TFT LCD Technology



TFT (Thin-Film Transistor): Active Matrix LCD.

Each pixel is controlled by its own transistor.
Result: Faster response time, better contrast than passive STN.

IPS (In-Plane Switching):

Excellent viewing angles (colors don’t invert when tilted).
Vibrant color reproduction.

Controller (ST7789): Built-in RAM stores the image.





TFT Structure

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Pixels & Color Format (RGB565)



Resolution: 240 x 240 Grid

Total Pixels: 57,600.

Color Depth: 16-bit (65,536 colors).
RGB565 Format:

Red: 5 bits (32 shades)
Green: 6 bits (64 shades)
Blue: 5 bits (32 shades)

Why 6 bits for Green? The human eye is most sensitive to green detail.





RGB565 Memory Layout

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Why SPI + DMA?


We use the ST7789 TFT Driver.

The Bandwidth Challenge:

Resolution: 240 x 240 pixels.
Color: 16-bit (2 bytes) per pixel.
Frame Size: \(240 \times 240 \times 2 = 115,200\) bytes.

To achieve 30 FPS:

We need to move: \(115,200 \times 30 \approx 3.5 \text{ MB/s}\).
Conclusion: This requires high-speed SPI (MHz range) and DMA!






240x240 TFT Module

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
ST7789 Pin Interface


Standard 4-Wire SPI Interface:

MOSI (SDA): Serial Data (Pixels/Commands).
SCK (SCL): Serial Clock.
RES (RST): Hardware Reset (Low active).
DC (WR): Data/Command Selection.

Low: Sending a Command (e.g., “Sleep Out”).
High: Sending Data (e.g., Pixel Colors).






Interfacing STM32F401 with ST7789

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
The Drawing Protocol


Drawing to a TFT is a state machine:

Set Column Address (0x2A): Define Start X and End X.
Set Page Address (0x2B): Define Start Y and End Y.
Write RAM (0x2C): Prepare to accept pixels.
Stream Pixels: Send purely color data (DC High) until the defined area is filled.





Window + Pixels Concept

text:

TFT Pin

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Stepper Motor Basics



Definition: An electromechanical device that rotates in discrete angular steps.
Key Features:

Open Loop: No encoder needed; you count the steps to know the position.
Holding Torque: Resists rotation when stopped (if powered).
Speed Control: Controlled perfectly by the frequency of pulses.






Stepper Motor





Stepper Coils

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Unipolar vs Bipolar Steppers


Unipolar (5-Wire)



Click to Watch: Unipolar Stepper


▶ Watch Video

Center tap allows current reversal.
Simple driver (ULN2003).


Bipolar (4-Wire)



Click to Watch: Bipolar Stepper


▶ Watch Video

Requires H-Bridge (L298N/A4988).
Higher torque per weight.

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Stepping Sequences


Modes of Operation:

Wave Drive: One phase on at a time. Low torque.
Full Step (Torque): Two phases on. Max torque.
Half Step: Alternates 1-phase / 2-phase. Smoother, double resolution.

▶ Watch Video




Stepping Sequences

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
The Hardware: 28BYJ-48 & ULN2003



28BYJ-48 Motor:

5V Unipolar: 4 coils with a common center tap.
Geared: Internal 1:64 reduction gear.
Resolution: ~4096 steps per output revolution (Half-step).

ULN2003 Driver:

Darlington Array: High-current transistor switches.

MCU GPIOs (3.3V) cannot drive coils (needs ~200mA).





28BJY-48 Overview






ULN2003 Overview

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Control Logic: Drive Sequences
How do we make it move? By energizing coils in a specific order.



Sequence
Description
Pros/Cons




Wave
1 coil at a time
Low power, Low torque.


Full
2 coils at a time
Max torque, High power.


Half
1-2-1 sequence
Smoothest, Doubles resolution.



Half-Step Sequence (8 steps): 1000 -> 1100 -> 0100 -> 0110 -> 0010 -> 0011 -> 0001 -> 1001

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Stepper: Drive Logic
Logic: Wave Drive
In this example, we use Wave Drive (Single-Phase):

Method: Only 1 Coil is energized at a time.
Sequence: 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1…
Why? Simplest heat management, distinct steps.

The Interrupt State Machine

Timer triggers: Every X milliseconds (defines speed).
step_index: Tracks position (0-3).
Modulo (% 4): Wraps around automatically.

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Timing is Everything

Core Concept: Stepper motors require Time-Critical output generation.
Don’t: Never use HAL_Delay() between steps (Blocking CPU is bad).
Do: Use a hardware Timer Interrupt.

Example: Timer triggers every 1ms.
ISR: Move to next step in the sequence array.
Speed Control: Change the Timer Period (ARR).

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Acceleration (Ramping)
Motors have inertia. They cannot jump from 0 to 1000 RPM instantly.

Ramping:

Physics Constraint: Motors have inertia. They cannot jump from 0 to 1000 RPM instantly.
Solution:

Start with a slow timer frequency.
Gradually increase frequency (decrease period).
Same for stopping (decelerate).

Risk: Skipping this leads to “Stalling” (Motor buzzes but doesn’t move).



Velocity Profile

title: Lecture 13: DMA, Displays & Motors
text:
Final Lab Integration
This week’s lab combines all three concepts:

ADC + DMA: Read potentiometer (User Input).
SPI + DMA: Update TFT Display (User Interface).
Timer Interrupt: Drive Stepper Motor (Actuator).

The Result: A responsive system where motor speed and display updates happen “simultaneously” without blocking each other.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 13 - Lab 13: TFT Display & SPI with DMA

text:
Welcome to Lecture 13 Lab: TFT Display & SPI with DMA
High-Speed Graphics on Embedded Systems

This lab focuses on interfacing a 240x240 TFT display with an ST7789 driver.
You will use SPI for communication and DMA for efficient data transfer.
A pre-built driver library is provided — you’ll focus on using it, not writing it.

Learning Objectives for Lab 13

Configure SPI with DMA in CubeMX.
Use an existing TFT driver library to display graphics.
Read ADC values and visualize them on the display.

Appendix: Font Mechanics (11x18)
How is a Character Stored?





Grid: 11 \(\times\) 18 pixels (mapped to uint16_t array).

Logic: if ((row_data << col) & 0x8000) { DrawPixel(); }

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
section: Welcome to Lecture 12 Lab: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Welcome to Lecture 12 Lab: Analog Sensors & LCDs
Interfacing with the Physical World

This lab focuses on reading analog signals and displaying information on visual interfaces.
You will read analog voltage data from a Potentiometer using the ADC.
You will display this data on a 16x2 LCD connected via I2C.
You will also establish a control loop to manage the LCD from your PC via UART.

Learning Objectives for Lab 12

Configure and use the ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) to read sensor data.
Interface with a 16x2 LCD using an I2C Backpack.
Implement UART communication for both data logging and device control.
Create a system that integrates sensing, processing, and multiple display outputs.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
Required Resources
Hardware

Nucleo-F401RE Board: Our core development board.
Solderless Breadboard: For prototyping circuits.
Potentiometer: 10kΩ or similar.
16x2 LCD with I2C Backpack: Character display.
Jumper Wires: For connections.

Software

VS Code + STM32CubeMX: Development environment.
Serial Terminal Software: PuTTY, Tera Term, etc.

Documents

STM32F401RE Datasheet & Reference Manual.
LCD I2C Library / Datasheet.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)

Wiring Diagram: Potentiometer & LCD



Connecting the Potentiometer

VCC: Connect to 3.3V.
GND: Connect to GND.
Wiper (Middle Pin): Connect to an Analog Input Pin (e.g., PA0 / ADC1_IN0).

Connecting the LCD (I2C)

GND: Connect to GND.
VCC: Connect to 5V.
SDA: Connect to I2C SDA (e.g., PB9 or PB7).
SCL: Connect to I2C SCL (e.g., PB8 or PB6).







Potentiometer and LCD Wiring

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
Code Blueprint: Key HAL Functions
ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)

HAL_ADC_Start(): Starts the ADC conversion.
HAL_ADC_PollForConversion(): Waits for conversion to complete.
HAL_ADC_GetValue(): Reads the converted digital value.

I2C (For LCD)

HAL_I2C_Master_Transmit(): Sends commands/data to the LCD backpack.

UART (Serial Communication)

HAL_UART_Transmit(): Sends data to PC.
HAL_UART_Receive() / HAL_UART_Receive_IT(): Receives commands from PC.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
Step-by-Step: Project Setup & Configuration
1. CubeMX Configuration

ADC1: Enable IN0 (or your chosen pin).
I2C1: Enable in I2C mode. Check pins (e.g., PB8/PB9).
USART2: Enable Asynchronous mode (115200 baud).
Clock: Set HCLK to 84 MHz.
NVIC Settings: Enable USART2 global interrupt in the NVIC Settings tab.
Generate Code.

2. LCD Library Integration

You will need a simple I2C LCD library (functions like lcd_init(), lcd_send_string(), lcd_clear()).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
Understanding the LCD I2C Protocol

How the Code Works

I2C Expander (PCF8574): The LCD is connected to an 8-bit I/O expander.
4-Bit Mode: We send data in two 4-bit chunks (nibbles) because the expander only has 8 pins (4 for data, 4 for control).

Upper Nibble (D4-D7): Sent first.
Lower Nibble (D0-D3): Sent second.


Bit Mapping (Byte sent to I2C)













Bit 7
Bit 6
Bit 5
Bit 4
Bit 3
Bit 2
Bit 1
Bit 0




D7
D6
D5
D4
Backlight
EN
RW
RS




RS (Register Select): 0 = Command, 1 = Data.
RW (Read/Write): 0 = Write.
EN (Enable): Pulse High \(\to\) Low to latch data.
Backlight: 1 = On.

Transmission Sequence (Per Nibble)

Set Data & Control: Data on D4-D7, EN=1, Backlight=1.
Latch: Change EN to 0 (Data is captured by LCD).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
Task 2: UART Control Center

Objective
Control the LCD parameters from the PC via UART.
Commands to Implement

Display Message: Typing a string should show it on the LCD.
Clear Screen: Sending a specific command (e.g., #CLEAR) should clear the LCD.

Implementation Logic

Use UART Interrupts (HAL_UART_Receive_IT) to receive data without blocking the main loop.
Callback (HAL_UART_RxCpltCallback):

Store received character in a buffer.
If ‘Enter’ (\n or \r) is received, process the buffer.
If buffer == “#CLEAR”: Call lcd_clear().
Else: Call lcd_clear(), then lcd_send_string(buffer).

Restart reception: HAL_UART_Receive_IT(...).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
How to Send Commands (Task 2)
PC Side Instructions (VS Code)

Open the Serial Monitor: use VS Code’s SERIAL MONITOR tab (bottom panel).
New Connection: Click the “Open Console” icon (monitor with a plus sign) and select Command Shell Console.
Connection Type: Select Serial Port as the connection type.
Settings:

Connection Name: Give it a name (e.g., “Nucleo”).
Serial Port: Select the COM port connected to your board.
Baud Rate: Set to 115200.
Data/Parity/Stop: 8 Bits, None, 1 Stop Bit.

Usage: Type commands in the input field and press Enter.

Type text to display on LCD.
Type #CLEAR to clear the screen.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
Debugging & Common Pitfalls
ADC Issues

Unstable Readings: Use a capacitor (e.g., 100nF) between Vout and GND. Use multiple samples and average them.
Wrong Voltage: Ensure you use 3.3 in your calculation if VREF+ is 3.3V.

I2C LCD Issues

No Text: Check contrast potentiometer on the back of the I2C backpack.
Wrong Address: Default is often 0x27 or 0x3F. Scan for address if unsure.
Pull-ups: Ensure SDA/SCL have pull-up resistors (often built-in to backpack).

UART Issues

Garbage Data: Check Baud Rate (115200).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
Bonus Challenges (Optional)
1. UART Echo (Bidirectional)

Goal: Verify the Nucleo received the correct message.
Task: Modify the code to send the received string back to the PC Terminal immediately after displaying it.
Hint: Use HAL_UART_Transmit inside the if(lcd_needs_update) block.

2. LED Command Control

Goal: Control hardware via text commands.
Task: Add support for #ON and #OFF commands to turn the User LED (PA5) High or Low.
Hint: Add else if statements with strcmp to check rx_buffer and use HAL_GPIO_WritePin.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs (Potentiometer Version)
text:
Deliverables
Demonstration

Pot Monitor: Show real-time voltage updates on LCD and Serial Terminal. Turn the potentiometer to see changes.
Remote Control: Type a message on your PC and show it appearing on the LCD. Type #CLEAR to wipe it.

Submission

main.c: Fully commented.
lcd_i2c.c/.h: Your LCD driver files.
Short Video: Demonstrating both tasks.
