Submit your deliverables by the deadline.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The code you never wrote

You wrote counter++;. The chip has never heard of it. This is what it actually runs—and your debugger will show it to you.

080004a4:  4b04     ldr   r3, [pc, #16]    ; address of counter
080004a6:  681b     ldr   r3, [r3, #0]     ; fetch its value
080004a8:  3301     adds  r3, #1           ; add one
080004aa:  4a03     ldr   r2, [pc, #12]    ; the address again
080004ac:  6013     str   r3, [r2, #0]     ; write it back
By the end, you will read listings like this fluently—and use them to see exactly what the compiler did to every line of C you have written this term.

Every listing in this deck is real output from arm-none-eabi-gcc 14.3 — the exact toolchain inside STM32CubeCLT that VS Code drives. Nothing is invented.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The story so far


01 WEEKS 1-5: you wrote C—variables, loops, functions, pointers, registers.


02 WEEKS 6-10: the peripherals came alive—interrupts, timers, PWM, capture, ADC.


03 ALL ALONG: the debugger showed peripheral registers in the SFR view.


04 TODAY: one level down—the CPU’s own registers, and the instructions that move them.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Today’s objectives


01 Name the CPU registers and their jobs: R0–R12, SP, LR, PC, the flags.


02 Read the core Thumb-2 instructions: LDR/STR, MOV, arithmetic, logic, shifts.


03 Follow control flow: CMP, the flags, conditional branches, BL/LR.


04 Map real C—your C—to the compiler’s real output, at -O0 and -O2.


05 Step single instructions in the Disassembly view and watch registers change.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
L04–L06 recap: one level up
Thirty-second check on things you already know:


1 What does GPIOA->ODR |= (1u << 5); do, bit by bit?


2 When an interrupt fires, what does the hardware push, and where?


3 Where does a local variable live?



Read-modify-write with a mask; registers onto the stack; and locals live on the stack or in CPU registers—today you finally watch all three happen.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Why read assembly


01 THE DEBUGGER: the Disassembly view is always there—optimized code, library code, code with no source.


02 THE FAULT: when the program crashes, the evidence is a PC value and a stack of registers.


03 THE COST: you can finally see what a line of C costs—one instruction or forty.


04 THE OPTIMIZER: -O2 rewrites your code; assembly is the only place the truth lives.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The ladder


Three levels of the same operation: the C statement counter plus plus, compiled to four Thumb assembly instructions, assembled to four 16-bit machine-code halfwords that the processor fetches

    One statement, three languages  C — WHAT YOU WRITE counter++; one line   compiler  ASSEMBLY — WHAT YOU READ ldr r3, [pc, #16] ldr r3, [r3, #0] adds r3, #1 str r3, [r2, #0] four instructions    assembler  MACHINE CODE — FETCHED 4b04 681b 3301 6013 16 bits each, here   Thumb-2: most instructions are 16 bits; some take 32 — like f043 0320 (orr.w r3, r3, #32) Each assembly line is one machine instruction. That is the whole trick of reading it. 


Deep dive: PM0214 Rev 10, §3.1 Instruction set summary — p. 50 · the full pipeline around this ladder: From C to Silicon, §3 From C to ELF.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The CPU’s workspace


The Cortex-M4 register file: R0 to R3 carry arguments and results, R4 to R11 hold locals, R12 is scratch; R13 is the stack pointer, R14 the link register holding return addresses, R15 the program counter; the flags N Z C V record the last comparison

 Sixteen registers — every value passes through here  R0 – R3 arguments in, result out first argument in r0 — result out in r0   R4 – R11 workhorses for locals callee-saved: push/pop guard them    R12 scratch — the compiler’s spare hand    R13 = SP — STACK POINTER where push and pop happen the stack Week 6’s interrupt entry used    R14 = LR — LINK REGISTER BL writes the return address here bx lr = return    R15 = PC — PROGRAM COUNTER the machine’s finger on the page advances by 2 or 4; branches rewrite it    xPSR — THE FLAGS Nnegative result Zzero result Ccarry / no borrow Vsigned overflow written by CMP and by S-suffixed instructions read by conditional branches 


Deep dive: PM0214 Rev 10, §2.1.3 Core registers — p. 18.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Load/store architecture


01 Only two instruction families touch memory: LDR (memory → register) and STR (register → memory).


02 Everything else—math, logic, compares—works register to register.


03 So every C statement becomes the same rhythm: load, compute, store.



You saw the rhythm on the opener: load counter, add one, store counter. Once you hear it, every listing gets easier.


Deep dive: PM0214 Rev 10, §3.4 Memory access instructions — p. 69.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Anatomy of an instruction


The instruction adds r3, comma, hash 1, labeled part by part: ADD is the operation, the S suffix also updates the flags, r3 is both destination and first operand, hash 1 is an immediate constant

 adds r3, #1  ADD — the operation  S — also update the flags  r3 — destination and operand  # — an immediate constant

Read any instruction the same way: operation, destination, operands — and an s means the flags learn about the result.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
MOV and constants
movs r3, #1              ; r3 = 1  (small constants fit inside)
movs r0, #20             ; argument setup, as we will see
ldr  r3, [pc, #16]       ; big constants live in a literal pool
                         ;   -> loads 0x40020014 (GPIOA ODR!)


01 Small numbers travel inside the instruction—an immediate.


02 A 32-bit address cannot fit in a 16-bit instruction—the compiler parks it after the function and loads it PC-relative.



Deep dive: PM0214 Rev 10, §3.5.6 MOV and MVN — p. 89.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
LDR and STR
ldr r3, [r7, #4]     ; r3 = memory[r7 + 4]     (a local variable!)
str r0, [r7, #4]     ; memory[r7 + 4] = r0
ldr r3, [r3, #0]     ; r3 = memory[r3]         (follow a pointer)
str r3, [r2, #0]     ; memory[r2] = r3


01 Brackets mean memory at this address—r3 and [r3] are entirely different things.


02 At -O0, [r7, #4] is your local variable: r7 frames the stack, #4 is its slot.


03 L03’s pointers, made visible: dereferencing is exactly one ldr.



Deep dive: PM0214 Rev 10, §3.4.2 LDR and STR, immediate offset — p. 71.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
counter++, for real
The compiler’s actual output (-O0), prologue trimmed:
ldr  r3, [pc, #16]     ; r3 = &counter   (from the literal pool)
ldr  r3, [r3, #0]      ; r3 = counter
adds r3, #1            ; r3 = r3 + 1
ldr  r2, [pc, #12]     ; r2 = &counter   (again)
str  r3, [r2, #0]      ; counter = r3

LOAD, COMPUTE, STORE. Four instructions for one ++—and because counter is volatile, the compiler is forbidden to cache it in a register between uses. L04’s keyword, explained at last from below.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Arithmetic and logic
add  r3, r2            ; r3 = r3 + r2
lsls r3, r3, #1        ; r3 = r3 << 1     (the compiler's *2)
orr.w r3, r3, #32      ; r3 = r3 | 32     -- there it is: |= (1u << 5)
bic  r3, r3, #32       ; r3 = r3 & ~32    -- and &= ~(1u << 5)


01 The L04 masking idioms are single instructions: ORR sets, BIC clears, EOR toggles.


02 Multiplication by powers of two compiles to shifts—2*x became lsls.


03 orr.w is a 32-bit Thumb-2 encoding—the .w just means wide.



Deep dive: PM0214 Rev 10, §3.5.1 ADD, SUB… — p. 83, §3.5.2 AND, ORR, EOR, BIC… — p. 85, §3.3.4 Shift operations — p. 62.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Practice: run three instructions
Registers before: r3 = 0.


1 movs r3, #5 — now r3 = ?


2 lsls r3, r3, #2 — now r3 = ?


3 adds r3, #1 — now r3 = ? And which C expression did you just run?



5 → 20 → 21. You just executed 4*x + 1 the way the chip does—and all three, ending in s, updated the flags.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
CMP and the flags
cmp  r3, #10       ; compute r3 - 10, keep only the flags
                   ;   result thrown away, N Z C V remembered


01 CMP is a subtraction that publishes only its verdict: the four flags.


02 Branches then read the verdict: beq/bne for equality, bgt/blt for signed, bhi/bls for unsigned.


03 Our uint32_t value > 10 compiled to bls—the compiler chose the unsigned family because you chose the type.



Deep dive: PM0214 Rev 10, §3.5.5 CMP and CMN — p. 88, §3.3.7 Conditional execution — p. 65.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The if, for real
if (value > 10) { result = 1; } else { result = 0; }
The actual -O0 output (value in [r7,#4], result in [r7,#12]):
ldr  r3, [r7, #4]      ; load value
cmp  r3, #10           ; compare with 10
bls  else_part         ; if value <= 10 (unsigned), jump
movs r3, #1            ; then-branch: result = 1
str  r3, [r7, #12]
b    after_if          ; skip the else
else_part:  movs r3, #0
            str  r3, [r7, #12]

Every if is this shape: compare, branch around the code you skip. The condition is inverted—the branch jumps when the if is false.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The loop, for real
for (uint32_t i = 1; i <= n; i++) { total += i; }
      b    check          ; jump straight to the test
body: ldr  r2, [r7, #12]  ; total
      ldr  r3, [r7, #8]   ; i
      add  r3, r2
      str  r3, [r7, #12]  ; total += i   (then i++, same rhythm)
check: ldr  r2, [r7, #8]   ; i
      ldr  r3, [r7, #4]   ; n
      cmp  r2, r3         ; i <= n ?
      bls  body           ; yes: go around again

A loop is just a backward branch. Spot a branch to a lower address in any listing and you have found the loop.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
BL and the way home
uint32_t use_scale(void) { return scale(20); }
push {r7, lr}          ; save the old way home
movs r0, #20           ; argument goes in r0
bl   scale             ; call: LR = return address, PC = scale
mov  r3, r0            ; result comes back in r0
pop  {r7, pc}          ; popping into PC *is* the return


01 The calling convention: arguments in r0–r3, result in r0. Now L03’s “return value” has an address.


02 pop {…, pc} restores the saved lr straight into pc—return and cleanup in one instruction.



Deep dive: PM0214 Rev 10, B, BL, BX — p. 142, §3.4.7 PUSH and POP — p. 78.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Practice: trace the call
Step through use_scale() above, one instruction at a time:


1 Just after bl scale — what is in r0, and what is in lr?


2 scale runs lsls r0, r0, #1 then adds r0, #1 — what comes back?


3 What exactly does pop {r7, pc} do?



r0 = 20, lr = the address after the bl; the result 41 returns in r0; and the pop restores r7, then loads the saved return address into pc—execution is home.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
What the optimizer did
The same use_scale() at -O2—this is the entire function:
movs r0, #41           ; 2*20 + 1, computed at compile time
bx   lr


01 The call, the argument, the multiply—gone. The compiler proved the answer is 41.


02 Our if became ite ls / movls / movhi—conditional execution, no branch at all.


03 This is why debugging -O2 feels haunted: lines vanish, steps jump. Debug builds use -O0 on purpose.



volatile is the exception the optimizer must respect—your counter++ stayed a real load-add-store even at -O2. Peripheral registers survive optimization because of that keyword.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The Disassembly view


01 In a debug session: right-click the source → Open Disassembly View—your C interleaved with its instructions.


02 The i→ toolbar button—Instruction Stepping Mode—makes Step Into advance one instruction, not one line.


03 The Registers view shows r0–r12, sp, lr, pc, xpsr—live, changed values highlighted.


04 Works on any code—yours, HAL’s, code compiled without source.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Register evidence


01 Step one adds r3, #1: r3 increments and the flags in xpsr react.


02 Watch pc advance by 2—then by 4 across a .w instruction. Thumb-2, live.


03 Step a push {r7, lr}: sp drops by 8; find your return address in the Memory view at the new sp.


04 Step the bl: pc teleports, lr catches the way home.


A successful build proves none of this. The Registers view does.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Common misreadings


01 BRACKETS IGNORED: r3 is a value; [r3] is memory at that address.


02 FLAGS ASSUMED: only cmp/tst and s-suffixes write flags—add no, adds yes.


03 WRONG SIGN FAMILY: bgt after an unsigned compare—silently wrong for half the values.


04 BRANCH LOGIC INVERTED: the compiler branches when your if is false.


05 -O2 PANIC: vanishing lines and jumping steps are the optimizer, not a broken debugger.


06 ONE LINE ≠ ONE INSTRUCTION: counter++ was four; scale(20) was zero.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Mastery checklist


✓ Name every register’s job—r0–r3, r4–r11, sp, lr, pc, flags.


✓ Read load-compute-store rhythms in any -O0 listing.


✓ Follow cmp → flags → conditional branch, signed and unsigned.


✓ Trace a bl call: arguments, lr, result, pop {…, pc}.


✓ Step instructions and predict register changes before they happen.


Next: synchronous serial—I2C and SPI, talking to other chips.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Exit ticket
movs r3, #5
lsls r3, r3, #2
adds r3, #1


01 VALUE: What is in r3 at the end?


02 FLAGS: Which of the three instructions updated the flags?


03 CALL: Your function’s result must be handed to the caller—in which register?


04 MEMORY: Which two instruction families are the only ones that touch memory?



Every concept in this lecture, with page-linked sources: academic references.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
section: Welcome to Lecture 11 Lab: The Disassembly View
text:
Welcome to Lecture 11 Lab: The Disassembly View
Peeking Under the Hood

This lab is your opportunity to see how your high-level C code translates into low-level machine instructions.
We will use the debugger’s disassembly window to observe the CPU’s actual operations.

Learning Objectives for Lab 10

Navigate and interpret the debugger’s disassembly view.
Map C source code lines to corresponding ARM assembly instructions.
Observe the execution flow at the assembly level.
Understand how C constructs (variables, loops, functions) are implemented in assembly.
Utilize the disassembly view for advanced debugging and optimization insights.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Conceptual Background: Why Disassembly?
The Deeper Understanding

Debugging Complex Issues: When C code behaves unexpectedly, assembly can reveal the true execution path.
Performance Optimization: Identify bottlenecks and inefficient code sequences.
Understanding Compiler Behavior: See how the compiler translates your C code, including optimizations.
Hardware Interaction: Gain a clearer picture of how the CPU directly manipulates registers and memory.

Visual Suggestion

Diagram: A magnifying glass over a CPU chip, with C code on one side and assembly code on the other, showing the translation.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Recap: C to Assembly Mapping
The Compiler’s Job

The C compiler translates your human-readable C code into machine-executable assembly instructions.
Each C statement can translate into multiple assembly instructions.
Variables become memory locations or registers.
Functions become sequences of instructions with specific calling conventions.

Visual Suggestion

Simplified diagram showing a C function block transforming into a series of assembly instructions.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Required Resources
Hardware

Nucleo-F401RE Board: Our core development board.

Software

STM32CubeIDE: Our development environment.
A simple C project (e.g., a blinking LED, or a project with some arithmetic and control flow).

Documents

STM32F4xx Reference Manual: For understanding peripheral registers.
ARM Cortex-M4 Technical Reference Manual: For detailed instruction set information.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
STM32CubeIDE Debug Perspective
Your Window to the CPU

The Debug perspective in STM32CubeIDE provides various views to analyze program execution.
Key Views:

Source Code View: Shows your C code.
Disassembly View: Shows the corresponding assembly code.
Registers View: Displays the current values of CPU registers.
Variables View: Shows the values of C variables.
Memory View: Allows direct inspection of memory contents.


Visual Suggestion

Screenshot: A composite image of the STM32CubeIDE Debug perspective, highlighting the Source, Disassembly, Registers, and Variables views.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Step-by-Step Procedure: Project Setup
1. Create a Simple C Project

Open STM32CubeIDE.
Create a new project for Nucleo-F401RE.
Keep it simple: a basic blinking LED project is sufficient, or a project with a small function that performs some calculations.
Ensure the project compiles successfully.

Visual Suggestion

Screenshot: STM32CubeIDE project explorer with a simple project open.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Step-by-Step Procedure: Set a Breakpoint
Pausing Execution

Open your main.c file.
Choose a line of C code where you want to pause execution.

Good candidates: inside a loop, before/after a function call, or an arithmetic operation.

Double-click on the left margin next to the line number to set a breakpoint (a blue dot will appear).

Visual Suggestion

Screenshot: C code editor in STM32CubeIDE with a breakpoint set on a line.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Step-by-Step Procedure: Start Debug Session
Entering Debug Mode

Click the Debug button (bug icon) in the toolbar, or go to Run -> Debug.
STM32CubeIDE will compile (if necessary), flash the code to the board, and then switch to the Debug perspective.
Execution will pause at your first breakpoint.

Visual Suggestion

Screenshot: STM32CubeIDE toolbar with the debug button highlighted.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Step-by-Step Procedure: Open Disassembly View
Revealing the Machine Code

Once in the Debug perspective, go to Window -> Show View -> Disassembly.
The Disassembly view will open, showing the assembly instructions corresponding to your C code.
You will see the C source code interleaved with the assembly instructions.

Visual Suggestion

Screenshot: STM32CubeIDE Debug perspective with the Disassembly view prominently displayed.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Interpreting the Disassembly View
Columns Explained

Address: Memory address of the instruction.
Machine Code: The raw hexadecimal representation of the instruction.
Assembly Instruction: The mnemonic for the instruction (e.g., MOV, LDR, ADD).
Operands: The registers, memory addresses, or immediate values the instruction operates on.
Source Code: The corresponding line of C code (if available).

Visual Suggestion

Screenshot: A zoomed-in section of the Disassembly view with each column labeled and explained.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
section: Stepping Through Code: C vs. Assembly
text:
Stepping Through Code: C vs. Assembly
Observing Execution Flow

Use the debugger’s step controls:

Step Over (F6): Executes the current C line (or assembly instruction) and moves to the next.
Step Into (F5): Enters a function call (both C and assembly).
Step Out (F7): Executes until the current function returns.


Exercise

Step through a few lines of your C code.
Observe how the program counter (PC) moves in the Disassembly view.
Notice how a single C line might correspond to multiple assembly instructions.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Observing Register Changes
The CPU’s Internal State

Open the Registers view (Window -> Show View -> Registers).
As you step through assembly instructions, observe how the values in the general-purpose registers (R0-R12) and special registers (SP, LR, PC) change.
This is crucial for understanding data flow within the CPU.

Visual Suggestion

Screenshot: STM32CubeIDE Registers view, highlighting changing register values.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Observing Memory Changes
Data in RAM

Open the Memory view (Window -> Show View -> Memory).
Enter the address of a variable (you can find this in the Variables view).
Step through code that modifies this variable.
Observe how the memory contents change at the assembly level.

Visual Suggestion

Screenshot: STM32CubeIDE Memory view, showing a memory address and its changing contents.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Analyzing C Constructs: Arithmetic Operations
C Code
result = val1 * val2 + offset;
Disassembly Focus

Identify MUL (Multiply) and ADD (Add) instructions.
Notice how intermediate results are held in registers.
Observe the order of operations as translated by the compiler.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Common ARM Assembly Instructions (Review)
Data Movement

MOV: Move data between registers or immediate to register.
LDR: Load data from memory to register.
STR: Store data from register to memory.

Arithmetic

ADD: Addition.
SUB: Subtraction.
MUL: Multiplication.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Common ARM Assembly Instructions (Review Cont.)
Logical & Bitwise

AND, ORR, EOR: Bitwise logical operations.
LSL, LSR, ASR: Shift operations.

Control Flow

CMP: Compare values, sets flags.
B: Unconditional branch.
BL: Branch with Link (for function calls).
BEQ, BNE, BGT, BLT: Conditional branches.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Debugging Tips with Disassembly
Advanced Troubleshooting

Compiler Optimizations: When optimizations are enabled, C code lines might not map directly to assembly. The debugger might jump around.

Temporarily disable optimizations for easier debugging if needed.

Stack Analysis: Use the Registers view (SP) and Memory view to understand stack usage.
Interrupt Context: When an interrupt occurs, observe how registers are pushed onto the stack.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Identifying Issues with Disassembly
Real-World Scenarios

Infinite Loops: Identify the unconditional branch that keeps the PC stuck in a loop.
Incorrect Pointer Usage: See if LDR/STR instructions are accessing unexpected memory addresses.
Function Call Errors: Verify that BL and BX LR are used correctly for function entry/exit.
Unexpected Register Values: Track how data flows through registers.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Deliverables
What to Submit/Demonstrate

Demonstrate Disassembly Usage: Show your TA how to:

Set a breakpoint in C code.
Step through C code and observe the corresponding assembly.
Inspect register values as you step.
Explain how a simple C construct (e.g., a for loop or if statement) is implemented in assembly.

Discuss Insights: Share any interesting observations about compiler behavior or code optimization.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Questions & Discussion
Your Turn

Open floor for any questions or clarifications.

Visual Suggestion

Image: A question mark icon or a group of people discussing.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - Lab 10: The Disassembly View
text:
Thank You!
End of Lab 10

You’ve gained a powerful tool for understanding and debugging embedded systems!
The ability to read assembly is a hallmark of an advanced embedded developer.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
One number from a knob

The pin was neither HIGH nor LOW. The hardware answered with a number anyway—while your program did something else.

uint16_t raw = HAL_ADC_GetValue(&hadc1);   // 2867 ?!
By the end, you will know what 2867 means, how the hardware found it in twelve guesses, and how to turn a knob, a light level, or a temperature into brightness and motion.

Live demo if hardware is ready: pot on A0, raw value live in the Watch panel, turn the knob. 2867 × 3300 / 4095 ≈ 2311 mV — the same 2.31 V the SAR figure later converts.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The story so far


01 WEEKS 4-5: pins wrote and read digital levels—high or low, nothing between.


02 WEEKS 6-7: events interrupt the program; hardware counters keep time.


03 L08-L09: timer channels generated PWM and timestamped edges—still all digital.


04 TODAY: the last face of the pin—voltages between the levels.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Today’s objectives


01 Explain sampling, quantization, and resolution—and read the 0–4095 scale fluently.


02 Trace the SAR conversion and its timing from the 16 MHz clock.


03 Configure ADC1: channel, sampling time, single vs continuous conversion.


04 Convert counts to millivolts with integer math, and counts to control (PWM).


05 Verify conversions in the SFR view: DR, EOC, CONT.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
L08–L09 recap: the channel
Thirty-second check on the timer lectures:


1 Which register turned a number into LED brightness?


2 How did we measure an unknown PWM signal?


3 What can none of that hardware tell us about a voltage?



CCR owned the duty; capture channels timestamped edges into CCR; and every input so far reported only high or low—never how much.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Beyond high and low


01 THE KNOB: a potentiometer’s position is a voltage, 0 to 3.3 V.


02 THE LIGHT: an LDR in a divider turns brightness into volts.


03 THE HEAT: thermistors—and a sensor inside the chip—speak in millivolts.


04 THE BATTERY: monitoring a supply means reading its voltage.



All of these are voltages. One peripheral reads them all: the analog-to-digital converter.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The bridge: sampling





Three verbs, every conversion: sample (freeze the voltage), quantize (pick the nearest level), encode (store the number).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Resolution and the LSB





Our ADC has 12 bits: 4096 levels across 0–3.3 V. The staircase is finer than the noise—for us, effectively smooth.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Counts to millivolts
mV = raw × 3300 / 4095

raw =    0  →     0 mV        raw = 2048  →  1650 mV
raw = 4095  →  3300 mV        raw = 2867  →  2311 mV


01 Integer math first: (raw * 3300u) / 4095u fits comfortably in uint32_t.


02 Full scale (4095) maps to full voltage (3300 mV)—the endpoint convention.


03 Millivolts keep everything integer; convert to float only at the display edge.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Practice: read the counts


1 What raw value corresponds to 0.825 V?


2 Raw reads 3100—how many millivolts?


3 Why compute in millivolts instead of volts-as-float?



0.825 V is a quarter of full scale → ≈ 1024. 3100 × 3300 / 4095 = 2498 mV. And integers are exact, fast, and safe anywhere—including inside interrupt callbacks.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Sampling rate and aliasing





Sample slower than twice the fastest content and the samples lie—an alias. For knobs and light sensors we are safely slow; for audio and waveforms this rule bites.

In-lecture demo: aliasing, interactively.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The SAR guessing game





Binary search in silicon: one comparator decision per bit, MSB first. Our 12-bit ADC: twelve decisions per conversion.

In-lecture demo: step through a conversion.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The ADC in hardware


Simplified hardware diagram of the ADC: an analog pin feeds a sample-and-hold switch and capacitor, then the successive approximation core with comparator and internal DAC; the result lands in the data register and raises the end-of-conversion flag, which can be polled or raise an interrupt

     ADC1 — one conversion, start to finish  THE PIN PA0 · A0 analog mode    SAMPLE & HOLD switch + capacitor 3…480 cycles to charge     SAR CORE comparator vs. DAC 12 decisions, MSB first 12.5 cycles     THE RESULT ADC1->DR 0 … 4095     EOC sets in SR — ready!  poll it, or interrupt (Week 6 path)  Sample, decide bit by bit, publish the number. Hardware does all three. in: volts 0 – 3.3 V out: a number and a flag

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Conversion takes time







01 The ADC clock: PCLK2 16 MHz ÷ 2 = 8 MHz (divider in CCR; max 36 MHz).


02 High source impedance → the hold capacitor charges slowly → choose longer sampling. Our default: 84 cycles → ≈ 12 µs.



Deep dive: RM0368 Rev 6, §11.3.2 ADC clock — p. 215, §11.5 Channel-wise programmable sampling time — p. 222.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
One ADC, sixteen doors


01 The STM32F401RE has one ADC (ADC1) with 16 external channels—one converter, many pins.


02 PA0 is channel IN0—and the Arduino header’s A0. Our knob lives there.


03 Three internal channels need no wiring at all: a temperature sensor, V_REFINT, and V_BAT.



Channels share the one SAR core. Reading two sensors means choosing which door opens for each conversion.


Deep dive: RM0368 Rev 6, §11.3.3 Channel selection — p. 215, §11.9 Temperature sensor — p. 226.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The pin goes analog
The pin’s fourth personality, after input, output, and alternate function:


01 MODER bits = 11 — analog mode: the digital input path is disconnected entirely.


02 No pull-ups, no ReadPin, no AF—the pin wires straight to the ADC’s multiplexer.


03 The full GPIO story, complete: 00 input · 01 output · 10 alternate · 11 analog.



L04 promised the MODER table had four rows. Today the last row earns its keep.


Deep dive: RM0368 Rev 6, GPIOx_MODER — p. 158.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Single or continuous


01 SINGLE: software says go (SWSTART), the ADC converts once and stops. One question, one answer.


02 CONTINUOUS: the ADC restarts itself forever—DR always holds the freshest value.


03 SCAN: convert a whole sequence of channels in turn (with DMA—a later lecture).



We use single mode and ask on our schedule—the Week 7 tick decides when. Continuous returns tonight as SFR-view evidence.


Deep dive: RM0368 Rev 6, §11.3.4–11.3.5 Single / continuous conversion — p. 216.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
CubeMX configuration


01 Click PA0 → ADC1_IN0 (the pin turns analog—check MODER later).


02 In Analog → ADC1: enable IN0.


03 Parameters: Resolution 12 bits, Continuous Disabled, End Of Conversion single conversion.


04 Rank 1 → Channel 0, Sampling Time 84 Cycles. Generate; read the init before writing code.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Start, wait, read
HAL_ADC_Start(&hadc1);                          // SWSTART: go
HAL_ADC_PollForConversion(&hadc1, 2);           // wait for EOC (~12 us)
uint16_t raw = HAL_ADC_GetValue(&hadc1);        // read DR (clears EOC)

uint16_t mv  = (uint16_t)((raw * 3300u) / 4095u);


01 The third flag family: UIF (L07), CC1IF (L09), now EOC in ADC1->SR.


02 Reading DR clears EOC—the same read-to-acknowledge pattern.


03 12 µs is nothing on a human timescale—polling is honest here, not lazy.



Deep dive: UM1725 Rev 8, HAL_ADC_Start — p. 65, HAL_ADC_PollForConversion — p. 66, HAL_ADC_GetValue — p. 68.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
From counts to control
The whole course in four lines—analog in, analog out:
if (tick_10ms_flag) {                 // Week 7 schedules the reads
    tick_10ms_flag = 0;
    uint16_t raw = adc_read_in0();    // today: 0..4095

    uint32_t duty = (raw * 999u) / 4095u;          // map to L08's ARR
    __HAL_TIM_SET_COMPARE(&htim2, TIM_CHANNEL_1, duty);  // LD2 brightness
}

Knob voltage → number → compare register → PWM duty → average voltage → brightness. Two converters facing each other, your code in between.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Practice: the knob at midpoint
The pot sits exactly at center. With the L08 numbers (ARR = 999 LED, servo 1000–2000 µs):


1 What does raw read, and how many millivolts is that?


2 What LED duty does (raw * 999) / 4095 command?


3 What servo pulse does 1000 + (raw * 1000) / 4095 command—and what angle?



≈ 2048 → 1650 mV. Duty ≈ 499 — half brightness. Pulse ≈ 1500 µs → 90°, dead center. The knob is the servo now.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Reading nature: the LDR


A voltage divider: a fixed 10 kiloohm resistor from 3.3 volts to a middle node, and a light-dependent resistor from the node to ground; the node connects to analog pin PA1; in darkness the LDR resistance is large and the node voltage is high, in bright light it is small and the voltage is low

 3V3    10 kΩ fixed    → PA1 · ADC1_IN1   LDR dark ≈ 300 kΩ · bright ≈ 5 kΩ   GND V(PA1) = 3.3 V × R_LDR / (R_LDR + 10 kΩ)  DARK: R_LDR huge → V ≈ 3.2 V → raw ≈ 3970   BRIGHT: R_LDR small → V ≈ 1.1 V → raw ≈ 1365   dark → big number → bright LED: a night-light in one map()

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The ADC registers







Register
Role




ADC1->SR
EOC — result ready · OVR — you read too slowly


ADC1->CR1
RES resolution · scan mode


ADC1->CR2
ADON power · CONT continuous · SWSTART go · alignment


ADC1->SMPR2
sampling time per channel, IN0–IN9


ADC1->SQR3
which channel converts (rank 1)


ADC1->DR
the result, 0…4095 — reading it clears EOC




Deep dive: RM0368 Rev 6, §11.12 ADC registers — p. 229: ADC_CR2 — p. 232, ADC_SMPR2 — p. 234, ADC_DR — p. 239.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Register evidence


01 Pause after a read: ADC1->DR holds your raw value; turn the knob, step, read again.


02 Set CONT in CR2 and SWSTART from the SFR view: DR now streams by itself—no code running.


03 CR1 bits 25:24 read 00 — that is RES saying 12-bit.


04 GPIOA->MODER bits 1:0 read 11 — PA0 belongs to the analog world.


A successful build proves none of this. The SFR view does.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Common ADC mistakes


01 PIN LEFT DIGITAL: MODER not 11—you sample a pin the GPIO block still drives.


02 THE 5 V HABIT: Arduino formulas assume 5 V and 1023. Here: 3.3 V, 4095—and 5 V on an analog pin is damage, not data.


03 OFF-BY-ONE SCALE: divide by 4096 or 1023 and full scale never reads 100 %.


04 IMPEDANCE IGNORED: big resistances with 3-cycle sampling—the hold capacitor never charges; readings sag.


05 FLOAT IN THE CALLBACK: convert to millivolts with integers; pretty-print floats elsewhere.


06 POLLING BLIND: PollForConversion with no conversion started, or EOCSelection left unset—waits forever or returns stale data.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Mastery checklist


✓ Explain sample → quantize → encode with the bridge figure.


✓ Convert counts ↔︎ millivolts, in your head, with integers.


✓ Trace a SAR conversion and budget its time from 8 MHz.


✓ Configure a channel and read it by polling—and by interrupt.


✓ Verify DR, EOC, CONT, and the analog MODER in the SFR view.


Next: the bridge to assembly—what the compiler made of everything you have written.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Exit ticket
The knob reads raw = 1241 on the 12-bit scale.


01 VOLTAGE: How many millivolts is that?


02 CONTROL: What LED duty does the L08 map command?


03 REGISTER: Which register held 1241, and what cleared the ready flag?


04 HARDWARE: How many comparator decisions produced this number?



Every concept in this lecture, with page-linked sources: academic references.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
section: Welcome to Lecture 10 Lab: Reading the Analog World
text:
Welcome to Lecture 10 Lab: Reading the Analog World
Two Practical Tasks

This lab focuses on using the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) for real-world applications.
Task 1: Control a servo motor’s position using a potentiometer.
Task 2: Control LED brightness based on ambient light using an LDR (Light Dependent Resistor).

Learning Objectives for Lab 10

Configure the ADC peripheral for analog input conversion.
Read analog input values from a potentiometer and an LDR.
Map ADC values to control servo motor position using PWM.
Map ADC values to control LED brightness using PWM.
Understand practical applications of ADC in embedded systems.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Conceptual Background: Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
Bridging the Gap

Analog Signals: Continuous, real-world phenomena (e.g., temperature, light intensity).
Digital Signals: Discrete values (0s and 1s) understood by microcontrollers.
ADC Role: Converts analog voltage levels into digital numbers.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Required Resources


Hardware

Nucleo-F401RE Board: Our core development board.
Solderless Breadboard: For prototyping circuits.
Task 1 Components:

Servo Motor (e.g., SG90): For position control.
Potentiometer (e.g., 10kΩ): For variable analog input.

Task 2 Components:

LED & 220Ω Resistor: For visual output (controlled by PWM).
Light Dependent Resistor (LDR): For light intensity sensing.
10kΩ Resistor: To form a voltage divider with the LDR.

Jumper Wires: For connections.


Software

STM32CubeIDE: Our development environment.

Documents

STM32F401RE Datasheet: Pinout information.
STM32F4xx Reference Manual: Crucial! Details ADC and Timer registers.

Section 11: Analog-to-digital converter (ADC)
Section 13: General-purpose timers (TIM2 to TIM5)

STM32CubeF4 HAL User Manual: Details HAL functions for ADC and PWM.
Servo Motor Datasheet: Pulse width specifications (typically 1-2ms for 0-180°).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 1: Wiring Diagram


Potentiometer (Analog Input)

Outer pins → 3.3V and GND
Middle (wiper) → PA0 (ADC1_IN0)

Servo Motor (PWM Output)

Red wire → 5V
Brown/Black → GND
Orange/Yellow → PA6 (TIM3_CH1)

Key Points

Servo requires 50Hz PWM (20ms period)
Pulse width: 0.5ms = 0°, 1.5ms = 90°, 2.5ms = 180°
Potentiometer provides 0-3.3V analog signal





Task 1 Wiring: Potentiometer and Servo Motor

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
section: Task 1: Code Blueprint - Key HAL Functions
text:
Task 1: Code Blueprint - Key HAL Functions
Servo Motor Control Functions

ADC (Polled Mode):

HAL_ADC_Init(): Initialize ADC peripheral.
HAL_ADC_ConfigChannel(): Configure ADC channel for potentiometer.
HAL_ADC_Start(): Start ADC conversion.
HAL_ADC_PollForConversion(): Wait for conversion completion.
HAL_ADC_GetValue(): Read converted digital value.

PWM for Servo:

HAL_TIM_PWM_Init(): Initialize timer for PWM (50Hz for servo).
HAL_TIM_PWM_Start(): Start PWM generation.
__HAL_TIM_SET_COMPARE(): Set pulse width (0.5-2.5ms for servo position).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 1: Project Setup & Configuration
Steps 1-2: Create Project & Configure Clock

Create new project: ADC_Servo_Lab, select Nucleo-F401RE
Configure clock: HSI (Internal RC Oscillator) → HCLK: 16 MHz

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 1: CubeMX Configuration
Steps 3-5: Configure Peripherals
TIM3 (Servo PWM - 50Hz): * Channel 1 → PWM Generation, PSC: 15, ARR: 19999, Pulse: 1500
ADC1 (Potentiometer): * PA0 → ADC1_IN0, Continuous mode, 12-bit, Right aligned, Rank 1, set End of Conversion Selection to EOC flag at the end of each regular conversion (needed so HAL_ADC_PollForConversion fires every sample).
USART2 (Serial Communication): * Connectivity -> USART2 -> Mode: Asynchronous, Baud Rate: 115200 Bits/s
Generate Code: Save .ioc and generate

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 1: Test & Deliverables
Build, Flash, and Demonstrate

Build and flash to Nucleo board
Test: Rotate potentiometer → servo moves 0° to 180°
Demonstrate: Smooth servo control to TA
Explain: ADC config, 50Hz PWM, mapping logic (ADC→pulse width)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 1: Student Modifications
Suggested Enhancements to Implement

Reverse Servo Direction: Modify the mapping so that turning the potentiometer clockwise moves the servo counterclockwise.
Limited Range: Restrict the servo movement to only 45° to 135° instead of the full 0° to 180° range.
Center Deadzone: Implement a “deadzone” where the servo stays at 90° when the potentiometer is near the center position (e.g., ADC values 1800-2295).
Multi-Position Control: Divide the potentiometer range into 5 discrete positions (0°, 45°, 90°, 135°, 180°) instead of continuous control.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 2: Conceptual Background
Interrupt-Driven ADC Conversion

Task 1 Approach: Polled ADC - CPU waits for each conversion to complete.
Task 2 Approach: Interrupt-driven ADC - CPU is notified when conversion is complete.
Advantage: CPU can perform other tasks while ADC conversion is in progress.
Use Case: More efficient for applications with multiple concurrent operations.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 2: Wiring & Key Functions


Additional Wiring
LDR (Voltage Divider - Dark Sensor Config): * 10kΩ Resistor → 3.3V * LDR → GND * Junction (between Resistor and LDR) → PA1 (ADC1_IN1)
LED (PWM Output): * LED anode → PA7 (TIM3_CH2) via 220Ω * LED cathode → GND
Key HAL Functions (Interrupt Mode)

HAL_ADC_Start_IT(): Start ADC with interrupt
HAL_ADC_ConvCpltCallback(): Auto-called when done
HAL_NVIC_EnableIRQ(): Enable interrupt
__HAL_TIM_SET_COMPARE(): Set LED brightness





Task 2 Wiring: LDR and LED

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 2: Modify Existing Project
Steps 1-3: Add Task 2 Components to Same Project
In CubeMX (reopen your .ioc file):

TIM3 Channel 2: PWM Generation, PSC: 15, ARR: 999, Pulse: 0
PA1: Configure as ADC1_IN1
ADC1: Add Rank 2 (Channel 1), same settings as before
NVIC: Enable “ADC global interrupt”
USART2: Connectivity -> USART2 -> Mode: Asynchronous, Baud Rate: 115200 Bits/s
Regenerate code

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 2: Test & Deliverables
Build, Flash, and Demonstrate

Build and flash updated code
Test: Cover LDR → LED brightens, shine light → LED dims
Demonstrate: Both tasks working simultaneously!
Explain: Interrupt config, callback function, polled vs interrupt ADC

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Task 2: Student Modifications
Suggested Enhancements to Implement

Direct Mapping: Change the mapping so brighter light = brighter LED (remove the inverse relationship).
Threshold Control: Implement an automatic night light - LED only turns on when LDR value drops below a certain threshold (e.g., 2000).
Multi-Level Brightness: Instead of continuous brightness, implement 4 discrete brightness levels (0%, 33%, 66%, 100%) based on LDR ranges.
Hysteresis: Add hysteresis to prevent LED flickering when LDR value is near a threshold (implement a “buffer zone”).
Average Filtering: Store the last 5 ADC readings and use their average to control LED brightness (reduces noise/flickering).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Debugging & Common Pitfalls
Task 1 & Task 2 Troubleshooting

Task 1 - Servo Not Moving: Check 50Hz PWM configuration, verify pulse width range (500-2500).
Task 1 - Jittery Servo: Add a small delay between updates, check power supply stability.
Task 2 - Interrupt Not Firing: Verify NVIC interrupt is enabled in CubeMX.
Task 2 - LED Not Responding: Check callback function is properly defined, verify ADC channel configuration.
Both Tasks - ADC Reads Zero: Ensure GPIO pin is set to Analog mode, check voltage divider connections.
Both Tasks - PWM Not Working: Verify timer clock is enabled, check prescaler and ARR values.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 10 - Lab 10: Reading the Analog World
text:
Final Deliverables
What to Submit

Task 1 Code: main.c for servo control.
Task 2 Code: main.c for LDR LED control.
At least ONE modification from either task implemented and demonstrated.
Lab Report (Optional): Brief explanation of your modifications and results.

text:
Questions & Discussion
Your Turn
