title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Today’s target statement

This statement writes one bit to a GPIO register and turns the on-board LED on.

GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 5);
By the end, you will explain GPIOA, ->, BSRR, 1U << 5, and =—then verify the result on the board.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The story so far


01 WEEK 1: read statements, build, flash, and debug.


02 WEEK 2: store values, calculate, decide, loop, and process arrays.


03 WEEK 3: move jobs into functions and separate C files.


04 TODAY: use addresses and bits to control a hardware register.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Today’s objectives


01 Distinguish a stored value from the address where it is stored.


02 Read *, &, ., and -> in small C examples.


03 Create masks that set, clear, toggle, and test selected bits.


04 Explain why hardware register definitions use volatile.


05 Enable GPIOA, configure PA5, and control LD2 through CMSIS registers.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
L03 exit ticket - the answers


01 DESIGN — one clean contract: void acquire_samples(uint16_t out[], int count); (any correct types/name accepted).


02 ARRAY INPUT — arrays in C do not carry their length; the parameter cannot reveal it, so the caller must pass count.


03 EVIDENCE — a breakpoint inside the loop hits exactly 8 times with i covering 0…7, and the sum reads 18330 before the division.


Most common slip in your submissions: declarations with a body attached — a declaration ends at the ;.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Memory addresses



Address
Example stored value




0x20000000
24


0x20000004
1


0x20000008
4095



The value is the data. The address identifies where that data is stored.

Addresses are commonly written in hexadecimal because it is shorter than writing all the address bits.


Where these addresses live—flash, SRAM, and peripherals on one 32-bit map: From C to Silicon, §2 The memory your program lives in.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Taking an address: &
int temperature = 24;
int *address = &temperature;


01 temperature produces the stored value: 24.


02 &temperature produces the address of that variable.


03 int *address declares a pointer that can hold the address of an int.


Read the declaration aloud: “address is a pointer to an int.”

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Reading GPIOA->MODER
GPIOA->MODER


01 GPIOA is a CMSIS-provided pointer to the GPIOA register structure.


02 -> selects a member through that pointer.


03 MODER is the 32-bit mode register inside GPIOA.


The device header supplies this definition; your program uses the readable name.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The 32-bit register


A 32-bit register drawn as 32 cells, bit 31 on the left down to bit 0 on the right; every bit is 0 except bit 5, so the value is 0x00000020

 31 0  30 0  29 0  28 0  27 0  26 0  25 0  24 0  23 0  22 0  21 0  20 0  19 0  18 0  17 0  16 0  15 0  14 0  13 0  12 0  11 0  10 0  9 0  8 0  7 0  6 0  5 1  4 0  3 0  2 0  1 0  0 0        value = 0x0000 0020 — only bit 5 is 1

Each bit can be 0 or 1. The reference manual assigns a meaning to each bit or group of bits.
uint32_t register_value = 0U;
uint32_t matches the width of an STM32 peripheral register.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Hexadecimal



Hex
Binary
Decimal




0
0000
0


5
0101
5


A
1010
10


F
1111
15



0x00000020 = 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0010 0000
A 32-bit register can be written with eight hexadecimal digits.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Bitwise operators



Operator
Direct meaning




&
keep a bit only when both input bits are 1


|
make a bit 1 when either input bit is 1


^
make the result 1 when the input bits differ


~
reverse every bit


<<
shift bits left


>>
shift bits right



These are different from the logical operators &&, ||, and ! used in conditions.

Deep dive: C11 draft N1570, §6.5.7 Bitwise shift operators — p. 112 (then §6.5.10–6.5.12 for &, ^, |).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Building a mask
uint32_t mask = (1U << 5);
1U          0000 0001
1U << 5     0010 0000
The result has only bit 5 set. This is called a mask because it selects a specific bit position.
The U suffix marks the number as unsigned.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Set a bit: OR
register_value |= (1U << 5);
old value     0000 0001
mask          0010 0000
OR result     0010 0001
Bit 5 becomes 1; the other bits keep their previous values.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Clear a bit: AND + NOT
register_value &= ~(1U << 5);


01 1U << 5 selects bit 5.


02 ~ changes that mask so bit 5 is 0 and the other bits are 1.


03 &= clears bit 5 and preserves the other bits.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Replacing a bit field
Suppose bits 11:10 must become 01.
register_value &= ~(3U << 10);  // clear bits 11:10
register_value |=  (1U << 10);  // write 01

Clear the whole field first. Then set only the bits required by the new pattern.

3U is binary 11, so 3U << 10 selects both field bits.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
CMSIS register names


REFERENCE MANUAL



Peripheral
Register
Purpose




RCC
AHB1ENR
enable GPIO clocks


GPIOA
MODER
choose each pin’s mode


GPIOA
BSRR
set or reset output pins




C CODE
RCC->AHB1ENR
GPIOA->MODER
GPIOA->BSRR
The selected STM32 device header supplies these names and their addresses.


This is direct register access: CMSIS provides the names, but your C statement still reads or writes the hardware register.


The definitions live in stm32f401xe.h — every generated STM32 project includes this file.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The GPIOA clock
RCC->AHB1ENR |= (1U << 0);
Bit 0 of AHB1ENR enables the clock for GPIOA.


01 RCC selects the reset-and-clock-control peripheral.


02 AHB1ENR selects its AHB1 peripheral clock-enable register.


03 |= (1U << 0) sets the GPIOA enable bit without changing other clock bits.


Enable the clock first—an unclocked peripheral ignores register writes.

Deep dive: RM0368 Rev 6, RCC_AHB1ENR — p. 118.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
PA5’s mode field
Every GPIO pin uses two mode bits. Pin 5 therefore begins at bit 5 × 2 = 10.



Bits 11:10
PA5 mode




00
input


01
general-purpose output


10
alternate function


11
analog





MODER bits 15 down to 0 drawn as eight two-bit fields, one per pin; the pin 5 field at bits 11 and 10 is highlighted and contains 01, the output pattern

 15  14 pin 7  13  12 pin 6  11 0  10 1 pin 5  9  8 pin 4  7  6 pin 3  5  4 pin 2  3  2 pin 1  1  0 pin 0 pin n uses bits 2n+1 : 2n — pin 5 → bits 11:10 = 01 (output). Pins 15–8 continue in bits 31:16.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
PA5 as output
GPIOA->MODER &= ~(3U << 10);
GPIOA->MODER |=  (1U << 10);


01 3U << 10 selects bits 11 and 10.


02 The first statement clears both bits to 00.


03 The second statement sets bit 10, producing 01.


Do not assume the old field value; clear before setting the new pattern.

Deep dive: RM0368 Rev 6, GPIOx_MODER — p. 158.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Driving PA5 with BSRR
GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 5);       // PA5 high: LD2 on
GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 21);      // PA5 low:  LD2 off


BSRR drawn as 32 cells split into a reset half, bits 31 to 16, and a set half, bits 15 to 0; writing 1 to bit 21 drives PA5 low and writing 1 to bit 5 drives PA5 high

RESET half (31–16): write 1 → pin low SET half (15–0): write 1 → pin high  31           21 1      16  15           5 1      0  bit 21 = 5 + 16 → PA5 low bit 5 → PA5 high The two highlighted bits belong to two separate write statements.

Direct assignment is correct because each BSRR write is a command, not a stored configuration field.

Deep dive: RM0368 Rev 6, GPIOx_BSRR — p. 161.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Debugging register writes


01 PREDICT: calculate which bit or field should change.


02 STEP: stop immediately before and after the statement.


03 INSPECT: read the register in the SFR/peripheral view.


04 COMPARE: match the observed hexadecimal value to the mask.


05 CONFIRM: check that the physical LED agrees with the register state.



Register maps for the SFR view: RM0368 Rev 6, §8 GPIO — p. 146.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Common register mistakes


01 WRONG DEVICE: the project targets a different STM32 model.


02 CLOCK OFF: GPIOA is used before its clock-enable bit is set.


03 WRONG SHIFT: the mask selects a different pin or field.


04 FIELD NOT CLEARED: old mode bits remain in the two-bit field.


05 WRONG EVIDENCE: a successful build is treated as proof that the hardware changed.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Mastery checklist


✓ Distinguish a value, an address, and a pointer.


✓ Read structure members with . and pointer members with ->.


✓ Set, clear, toggle, and test selected bits.


✓ Explain CMSIS register names and volatile.


✓ Configure PA5 and control LD2 through register writes.


Next: compare direct register control with the STM32 HAL interface.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Exit ticket


GPIOA->MODER &= ~(3U << 10);
GPIOA->MODER |=  (1U << 10);


01 POINTER MEMBER: What does GPIOA->MODER select?


02 MASK: Which bits does 3U << 10 select?


03 RESULT: What pattern remains in bits 11:10?


04 HARDWARE: What register observation would prove the statements executed?




Scan before you leave — ungraded; your answers open the next lecture.


Every concept in this lecture, with page-linked sources: academic references.

title: EE5203 L04 Study Sheet
text:
Pointers, Structures, Bits, Registers, and GPIO - Basri Erdogan

Essential question: How does GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 5); turn on a physical LED — and how do you prove, bit by bit, that it did?




Distinguish a value, its address, and a pointer holding that address.
Read &, *, ., and -> in small C fragments.
Convert between binary and hexadecimal for 32-bit registers.
Build masks that set, clear, toggle, test, and field-replace bits.
Decode RCC->AHB1ENR, GPIOA->MODER, GPIOA->BSRR and explain volatile.
Enable the GPIOA clock, configure PA5 as output, and drive LD2 — then match the SFR view to the physical LED.




int x = 5;
int *p = &x;    /* p holds the ADDRESS of x        */
*p = 9;         /* write 9 THROUGH the pointer      */
                /* x is now 9; *p and x agree       */



Expression
Produces




x
the stored value


&x
the address of x


*p
the value at the address stored in p


s.member
member of a structure value


p->member
member reached through a pointer — (*p).member






GPIOA is a CMSIS pointer to the GPIOA register structure (from the device header stm32f401xe.h); -> selects a member through it; MODER is one 32-bit volatile register. volatile tells the compiler every read and write must really happen — hardware can change registers outside program flow.



value |=  (1U << n);          /* set bit n                    */
value &= ~(1U << n);          /* clear bit n                  */
value ^=  (1U << n);          /* toggle bit n                 */
if (value & (1U << n)) ...    /* test bit n                   */

value &= ~(3U << k);          /* clear a 2-bit field at k+1:k */
value |=  (1U << k);          /* then write pattern 01        */
Clear the whole field before setting the new pattern — never assume the old bits. U marks the constant unsigned.






Expression
Hex
Selects




1U << 0
0x00000001
GPIOA clock enable (AHB1ENR bit 0)


3U << 10
0x00000C00
PA5 mode field, bits 11:10


1U << 10
0x00000400
output pattern 01 for PA5


1U << 5
0x00000020
BSRR set half — PA5 high


1U << 21
0x00200000
BSRR reset half — PA5 low




MODER math: pin n owns bits 2n+1 : 2n; PA5 → bits 11:10; 01 = output.
BSRR halves: bits 15…0 set pins; bits 31…16 reset them; reset bit = pin + 16. Each write is a command, so plain = is correct for BSRR — while MODER needs read-modify-write (&=, |=).




RCC->AHB1ENR |= (1U << 0);      /* 1. clock FIRST - unclocked GPIO ignores writes */

GPIOA->MODER &= ~(3U << 10);    /* 2. clear PA5 mode field                        */
GPIOA->MODER |=  (1U << 10);    /* 3. write 01 = output                           */

while (1) {
    GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 5);    /* PA5 high - LD2 on  */
    HAL_Delay(250);
    GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 21);   /* PA5 low  - LD2 off */
    HAL_Delay(250);
}



Predict the bit → step the statement → read the register in the SFR view → compare hex to your mask → confirm the physical LED agrees. A successful build proves none of this.




Using GPIOA before its clock-enable bit is set.
Setting the new mode pattern without clearing the field first.
A shift that selects the wrong pin (1U << 5 vs. 1U << 10 confusion — BSRR uses the pin number, MODER uses 2n).
Confusing bitwise & | ^ with logical && || !.
Writing = to MODER (destroys the other pins’ fields).
Treating a clean build as hardware evidence.





I can trace int *p = &x; *p = 9; and give the final value of x.
I can read p->member as (*p).member.
I can compute the hex of any 1U << n and 3U << k without running.
I can write the two-statement field replace for any pin’s mode.
I can state why the RCC clock write comes first.
I can explain the two halves of BSRR and why plain = is safe there.
I can name the SFR observation that proves PA5 is an output.





After int a = 3; int *q = &a; *q = *q * 4; what are a and *q?
Which bits does ~(3U << 6) leave as 1, and what is the mask’s purpose?
A student writes GPIOA->MODER |= (1U << 10); without the clear first. When does this still work, and what starting field value breaks it?
Why does driving PB0 (homework) use 1U << 0 in BSRR but bits 1:0 in MODER?
volatile is removed from the register definition and the LED stops blinking at -O2. Explain what the optimizer did.

See the L04 worksheet for extended practice. Worked solutions are released after the practice window.

I can trace int *p = &x; *p = 9; and give the final value of x.
I can read p->member as (*p).member.
I can compute the hex of any 1U << n and 3U << k without running.
I can write the two-statement field replace for any pin’s mode.
I can state why the RCC clock write comes first.
I can explain the two halves of BSRR and why plain = is safe there.
I can name the SFR observation that proves PA5 is an output.

title: EE5203 L04 Study Sheet
text:
After int a = 3; int *q = &a; *q = *q * 4; what are a and *q?
Which bits does ~(3U << 6) leave as 1, and what is the mask’s purpose?
A student writes GPIOA->MODER |= (1U << 10); without the clear first. When does this still work, and what starting field value breaks it?
Why does driving PB0 (homework) use 1U << 0 in BSRR but bits 1:0 in MODER?
volatile is removed from the register definition and the LED stops blinking at -O2. Explain what the optimizer did.

See the L04 worksheet for extended practice. Worked solutions are released after the practice window.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
One job per function

Last week, one main() processed eight sensor values. Today we will give each job its own function.



1 ACQUIRE
Collect samples


→


2 PROCESS
Compute a result


→


3 REPORT
Build a message


By the end of today, main() will call these functions in order. Each function will have a clear name, inputs, and result.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Why small functions


01 READ The function name says what the code does.


02 TEST Stop inside one function and inspect its inputs and result.


03 REUSE Call the same function wherever that job is needed.


04 CHANGE Edit one job without rewriting the whole program.



For Week 3, use one clear job per function.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Today’s objectives


01 Read int clamp(...) and say what goes in and what comes back.


02 Write and call functions that clamp, average, and label sensor values.


03 Pass an array and its element count to a function.


04 Store "LOW", "OK", or "HIGH" in a C string ending with \0.


05 Put function declarations in .h files and function code in .c files.


You should be able to explain every function call and every file you add.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The function declaration


RETURN TYPE
What the caller receives


NAME
The responsibility


PARAMETERS
What the function needs


int clamp(int value, int low, int high);

Read the declaration aloud: “clamp receives three integers and returns one integer.”


Deep dive: C11 draft N1570, §6.9.1 Function definitions — p. 174.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Declare, define, call



1 · DECLARE

Tell other files the name and types

sensor_math.h

int clamp(int value,
          int low,
          int high);

Has no function body
Lets callers know the required types



→



2 · DEFINE

Write the function body

sensor_math.c

int clamp(int value,
          int low, int high)
{
    if (value < low)  return low;
    if (value > high) return high;
    return value;
}

Supplies the executable body
Normally exists once in the program



→



3 · CALL

Use the function

main.c

int safe = clamp(raw, 0, 4095);

Arguments must match the declaration
The return value replaces the call





COMPILE · The declaration lets the compiler check argument and return types.


LINK · The linker must find one matching definition of clamp.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Anatomy of a call


1 · ARGUMENTS COPIED
clamp(4170, 0, 4095)


→


2 · BODY EXECUTES
4170 > 4095


→


3 · RETURN REPLACES CALL
safe = 4095



Debugger plan: break at entry → inspect parameters → step → inspect return destination.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Common function mistakes


01 MISMATCH The declaration and definition use different types.


02 MISSING RETURN A non-void function reaches the end without returning a value.


03 WRONG ARGUMENTS A call uses the wrong type, order, or number of arguments.


Compiler warnings are evidence. Read them before debugging logic.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
#define constants


#define SAMPLE_COUNT 8

uint16_t samples[SAMPLE_COUNT];



First: the preprocessor replaces every SAMPLE_COUNT with 8.


Then: the compiler sees uint16_t samples[8];.


Important: SAMPLE_COUNT is not a variable and does not use memory.




Use one readable name so the array size and loop count cannot accidentally use different numbers.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Arrays as parameters


uint16_t average_samples(
    uint16_t values[], int count);

uint16_t mean =
    average_samples(samples, 8);



values[] receives access to the first array element.


count receives 8, the number of valid elements.


Return value replaces the call and is stored in mean.









Warning


The function cannot discover the array length from values[]; the caller must also pass the count.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
C strings
char status[8] = "READY";


0R


1E


2A


3D


4Y


5'\0'


6—


7—


The array capacity is 8; the text length is 5; the terminator occupies one element.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The \0 terminator


VALID STRING
{'O', 'K', '\0'}
Library functions know where to stop.


NOT A C STRING
{'O', 'K'}
No terminator is available.



The array size limits where we may write; \0 tells string code where the text stops.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Strings as outputs
bool make_status(char destination[],
                 int capacity,
                 uint16_t mean);


mean
Choose "LOW", "OK", or "HIGH"


capacity
Number of available char elements


bool RESULT
true if the complete string fit


bool stores true or false and is provided by <stdbool.h>.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Laboratory mission


A REFACTOR
Move arithmetic into functions


B PROCESS
Traverse a sample array safely


C REPORT
Write a status string that fits in its array


D DEFEND
Change one condition and explain the evidence


Checkpoint evidence: build result, watched variables, boundary test, and individual modification.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Mastery checklist


✓ Read a function declaration and explain each part


✓ Trace arguments, parameters, and return values


✓ Separate declarations from definitions


✓ Pass an array and its valid element count to a function


✓ Explain the role of \0 in a C string


✓ Pass the output array size to a string function


Next: pointers, structures, bits, and your first hardware register.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Exit ticket




01 DESIGN Write one function declaration for acquiring eight ADC samples.


02 ARRAY INPUT Why must an array function also receive count?


03 EVIDENCE What debugger observation would prove your average function is correct?




Scan and answer before you leave — sign in with your Google account. Ungraded; your answers open the next lecture.


Every concept in this lecture, with page-linked sources: academic references.

title: EE5203 L03 Study Sheet
text:
Functions, Arrays, Strings, and Multiple C Files - Basri Erdogan

Essential question: How do you split one long main() into named functions across several files — and still prove, with compiler and debugger evidence, that the pipeline computes the same results?




Read a declaration and state the return type, name, and parameters.
Explain declare (.h), define (.c), and call — and which build stage checks each.
Predict pass-by-value behavior: parameters are local copies.
Pass an array and its element count to a function.
Choose integer widths: uint16_t per sample, uint32_t for the sum.
Build a valid C string: capacity, text length, and the \0 terminator.
Use strcmp(a, b) == 0 for equality — never == on strings.




int clamp(int value, int low, int high);
Read aloud: “clamp receives three integers and returns one integer.”









Step
File
Contains
Checked by




Declare
sensor_math.h
signature, no body
compiler (types at each call)


Define
sensor_math.c
the body, exactly once
linker (finds one definition)


Call
main.c
clamp(raw, 0, 4095)
return value replaces the call



#include "sensor_math.h" is preprocessor text substitution — both .c files then see the same declaration. undefined reference is a linker message: a declaration exists but no definition was found.



void raise_to_minimum(int value) { if (value < 100) value = 100; }

int raw = 42;
raise_to_minimum(raw);   /* raw is still 42 */
Parameters are copies. To send one result back, return it.



uint16_t average_samples(uint16_t values[], int count);

values[] receives access to the first element — not a copy, and the function cannot discover the length: the caller must pass count.
Loop with i < count; return 0U when count == 0.
Sum into uint32_t: a uint16_t sum can overflow from the 17th maximal sample (17 x 4095 > 65535).




#define SAMPLE_COUNT 8
The preprocessor replaces the name with 8 before compilation. It is not a variable and occupies no memory — and the array size and loop bound can no longer disagree.



char status[8] = "READY";   /* capacity 8, length 5, '\0' at index 5 */







Term
Meaning




Capacity
Array size — where you may write (includes the \0)


Length
Characters before the \0 ("HIGH" has length 4, needs 5 bytes)


\0
Tells string code where the text stops




{'O','K'} is not a C string — no terminator.
Compare with strcmp(command, "START") == 0 (<string.h>).
A string-writing function receives the destination capacity, checks it before writing, writes \0 explicitly, and reports success (bool).




acquire_samples(samples, SAMPLE_COUNT);          /* hardware -> array  */
mean = average_samples(samples, SAMPLE_COUNT);   /* array    -> value  */
make_status(message, MESSAGE_CAPACITY, mean);    /* value    -> string */
main() keeps the short list of steps; the detail lives in the functions.




Declaration and definition types that do not match.
A non-void function that can reach its end without return.
Assuming an array parameter knows its own length.
uint16_t accumulator for a sum of many uint16_t values.
Writing text that fills the array but leaves no room for \0.
Comparing strings with ==.
Reading only the last build message instead of the first.





I can label return type, name, and parameters in any declaration.
I can say which build stage catches a bad argument type vs. a missing definition.
I can explain why raise_to_minimum(raw) leaves raw unchanged.
I can state the valid indices when count == 8.
I can justify uint32_t for the sum with a concrete overflow count.
I can draw "OK" in memory, byte by byte, inside char status[8].
I can write a capacity check for make_status before it writes.





uint16_t average_samples(uint16_t values[], int count); — what does the caller receive, and what two things does the caller have to supply?
The build fails with undefined reference to make_status. Which file is missing what, and why did compilation succeed?
After int x = 7; scale(x, 4); the student expects x == 28. What actually happened, and what one-line change fixes the caller?
How many bytes does "HIGH" occupy as a C string, and what is stored in the final byte?
make_status(text, 4, 3500U) must select "HIGH". What should the function do, and what should it return?

See the L03 worksheet for extended practice. Worked solutions are released after the practice window.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Today’s objectives


01 Store and change sensor values with variables.


02 Calculate with arithmetic operators.


03 Choose a result with if, else if, and else.


04 Repeat code with for and while.


05 Process all eight values in one array.


The laboratory keeps everything in one main.c file.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Anatomy of a variable
int temperature = 24;


01 int stores a whole number.


02 temperature is the name used by later statements.


03 24 is the first value stored in the variable.


04 ; ends the statement.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Assignment
int temperature = 24;

temperature = 18;
The second statement does not ask a question. It stores 18 in temperature.


BEFORE
temperature is 24

AFTER
temperature is 18

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Arithmetic operators
int a = 10;
int b = 3;

int sum        = a + b;   // 13
int difference = a - b;   // 7
int product    = a * b;   // 30
int quotient   = a / b;   // 3
int remainder  = a % b;   // 1

Both operands of a / b are integers, so the result is the whole-number quotient.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
++ and +=
int count = 0;
int sum = 10;

count++;     // same result as count = count + 1
sum += 5;    // same result as sum = sum + 5
Use the longer form first when it makes the operation easier to understand.






Note


After these statements, count is 1 and sum is 15.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Comparison operators



Expression
Question




a == b
Are the values equal?


a != b
Are the values different?


a < b
Is a smaller?


a <= b
Is a smaller or equal?


a > b
Is a larger?


a >= b
Is a larger or equal?



A comparison does not change either variable.

Deep dive: C11 draft N1570, §6.8.4 Selection statements — p. 166 covers if; comparisons are §6.5.8–6.5.9.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
= vs ==


ASSIGNMENT
status = 1;
Store 1 in status.

COMPARISON
status == 1
Ask whether status contains 1.


Using = when you mean == is a common beginner error.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Logical operators








Operator
Meaning
Example




&&
both conditions must be true
sample >= 0 && sample <= 4095


||
at least one condition is true
mode == 1 || mode == 2


!
reverse true and false
!ready



Use parentheses when they make the intended grouping clearer.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Loop trace table



Repetition
i before body
Is i < 4 true?




1
0
yes


2
1
yes


3
2
yes


4
3
yes


stop
4
no



The loop body runs four times, not five.

Deep dive: C11 draft N1570, §6.8.5 Iteration statements — p. 168.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Which statement when?


01 Use if when code may run once or may be skipped.


02 Use while when repetition depends on a condition.


03 Use for when a counter visits a known range.


04 Use while (1) for the main loop of firmware that must keep running.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Array indexing



Expression
Selected element




samples[0]
first


samples[1]
second


samples[7]
eighth


samples[8]
invalid





An eight-element array drawn as cells with indices 0 through 7; a ninth dashed cell labeled index 8 is crossed out because it is outside the array

 [0] 850  [1] 1250  [2] 4095  [3] 4200  [4] 2050  [5] 900  [6] 3100  [7] 2800  [8] ✗ valid indices 0…7 — samples[8] is outside the array

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Predict, then verify


01 Calculate the expected final array, sum, average, and high count.


02 Build with zero errors and read the first warning, if any.


03 Break after the loop and inspect the variables.


04 Change one input value, predict again, and repeat the test.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Common Week 2 mistakes


01 Using = instead of == in a condition.


02 Forgetting to give an accumulator its starting value.


03 Writing i <= 8 and accessing samples[8].


04 Forgetting to update the counter in a while loop.

Mastery checklist


✓ Trace assignment and arithmetic statements in order.


✓ Explain the difference between = and ==.

✓ Process indices 0 through 7 without leaving the array.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 L02 Study Sheet
text:
Values, Decisions, Loops, and Arrays - Basri Erdogan

Essential question: How does one C loop turn eight raw sensor values into a validated average, a count, and a status decision — and how do you prove each intermediate value in the debugger?




Trace assignment and arithmetic statements in execution order.
Predict the result of integer division and remainder.
Distinguish = (store) from == (compare).
Write and trace if, else if, else decision chains.
Trace while and for loops, including first and last counter values.
Index an array safely and explain why samples[8] is invalid for [8].
Use the accumulator pattern to compute a sum and average.











Statement
Question it answers




x = 5;
Nothing — it stores 5 in x


x == 5
Does x currently contain 5?


if (c) { ... }
Run the block once if c is true


while (c) { ... }
Repeat while c stays true (test first)


for (init; test; update)
Repeat with a counter over a known range


while (1) { ... }
Firmware main loop — repeat forever














Group
Operators
Watch out




Arithmetic
+ - * / %
10 / 3 is 3 (integer); 10 % 3 is 1


Shorthand
x++, x += 5
Same as x = x + 1, x = x + 5


Comparison
== != < <= > >=
Comparing changes neither operand


Logical
&& || !
&& both true; || at least one true






for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) { /* body */ }



Repetition
i before body
i < 4?




1
0
yes


4
3
yes


stop
4
no



START creates i = 0, TEST runs before every repetition, UPDATE runs after every body. The body runs four times — i never reaches 4 inside the body.



#include <stdint.h>

uint16_t samples[8] = { 850, 1250, 4095, 4200,
                        2050, 900, 3100, 2800 };

uint16_t: unsigned, 16-bit, fixed-width integer — no negative values.
Valid indices are 0 through 7. samples[8] is outside the array.
Loop condition is i < 8, never i <= 8.




int sum = 0;                      // must start at zero

for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
    if (samples[i] > 4095) {      // clamp invalid input
        samples[i] = 4095;
    }
    sum += samples[i];            // accumulate
    if (samples[i] > 2500) {
        high_count++;             // count while visiting
    }
}

int average = sum / 8;            // integer division
One loop can clamp, sum, and count in a single pass. Every result variable needs a correct starting value before the loop.



if (average < 1500)       status_code = -1;   // LOW
else if (average > 3000)  status_code = 1;    // HIGH
else                      status_code = 0;    // OK
Conditions are tested top to bottom; exactly one branch runs; testing stops at the first true condition.




Writing = where == is meant — the condition becomes an assignment.
Forgetting to initialize an accumulator (sum starts with garbage).
i <= 8 on an eight-element array — reads/writes outside the array.
Forgetting the update in a while loop — the loop never ends.
Expecting 19140 / 8 to have a fraction — integer division discards it.
Trusting a clean build as proof the computed values are correct.





I can trace x = x + 3; y *= 5; x++; and give both final values.
I can explain why if (status = 1) is a bug, not a comparison.
I can state how many times for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) runs its body.
I can list the valid indices of uint16_t samples[8].
I can explain why sum must start at zero.
I can predict sum / 8 when sum is 19140.
I can place a breakpoint inside a loop and read i and samples[i].





What are the final values of a and b after int a = 6, b = 4; a = a - b; b++; a *= 2;?
int x = 7; — what is the value of the expression x % 3, and what is x afterwards?
A while loop’s body never executes. What must be true of its condition, and when was it tested?
In one pass over an array, which of these need a starting value before the loop: the loop counter, an accumulator, a count of high samples?
The lab program clamps 4200 to 4095 before adding it to sum. What would the average be wrong by if it clamped after summing?

See the L02 worksheet for extended practice. Worked solutions are released after the practice window.

Trace while and for loops, including first and last counter values.
Index an array safely and explain why samples[8] is invalid for [8].
Use the accumulator pattern to compute a sum and average.

In one pass over an array, which of these need a starting value before the loop: the loop counter, an accumulator, a count of high samples?
The lab program clamps 4200 to 4095 before adding it to sum. What would the average be wrong by if it clamped after summing?

See the L02 worksheet for extended practice. Worked solutions are released after the practice window.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Opening challenge

How does a line of C code become a physical change on a circuit board?

By the end of today, you will be able to trace the complete path from source code to physical evidence.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
section: A system is more than its processor

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Which systems are embedded?


Washing-machine controller
Dedicated function? Constraints?


Laptop
Dedicated function? Constraints?


Digital thermostat
Dedicated function? Constraints?


Car braking controller
Dedicated function? Constraints?









Note


The boundary is not always absolute. Your justification matters more than the label.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Processor, MCU, embedded system


PROCESSOR
Executes instructions
a computing core


MICROCONTROLLER
Processor + memory + peripherals
integrated on one chip


EMBEDDED SYSTEM
Hardware + software + purpose
the complete product



Our Nucleo board carries an STM32F401RE microcontroller, whose processor is an Arm Cortex-M4 core.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The course mission


01 Write & debug Embedded C


02 Model Cortex-M hardware


03 Control peripherals


04 Design responsive applications


05 Integrate & defend a system


The goal is not register-name memorization. The goal is evidence-based engineering.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Your destination: a complete project
A successful project has a testable chain:


Environmental monitor · Reaction-time game · Smart controller · Motion alarm · Sensor logger

L01

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Why C for embedded
int led_on = 0;
led_on = 1;


01 C requires us to state what kind of value a variable stores.


02 C statements end with a semicolon.


03 The compiler translates these statements into machine instructions.


We will build these foundations during the first four lectures.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Declaring a variable
int temperature = 24;


01 int means the variable stores a whole number.


02 temperature is the name used by later statements.


03 24 is the first stored value.


04 ; ends the declaration.

Source code is not firmware

Each stage produces different kinds of errors and evidence.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
The embedded toolchain








Tool
Job
Evidence




Editor
Create source files
Readable C source


Compiler
Translate C to object code
Errors, warnings, .o files


Linker
Combine objects and libraries
Memory map, .elf file


Programmer
Write firmware to flash
Successful download


Debugger
Control and inspect execution
Breakpoints, variables, registers



Our toolchain packages these tools: STM32CubeMX configures and generates, VS Code edits and debugs, and the STM32CubeCLT toolbox supplies the compiler, linker, and programmer underneath.

The full story behind this table—what each tool consumes and produces, from keypress to blinking LED: From C to Silicon, the toolchain companion.

USB

user button

STM32F401RE
target MCU

Register level and HAL: our policy

Clocks · bit fields · flags · cause and effect


HAL helps build systems
Readable operations · generated setup · manageable complexity



Our sequence: working HAL → C foundations → direct GPIO → informed abstraction choices

title: EE5203 Microprocessors

section: Build, flash, run: three different claims
text:
Build, flash, run: three different claims








Claim
What it proves
What it does not prove




Build succeeds
Source compiles and links
Hardware behaves correctly


Flash succeeds
Firmware reached the MCU
The logic is correct


LED blinks
One observable requirement works
Every requirement works



Engineering conclusions must match the evidence available.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Three error families


1 · COMPILE
Can each source file be translated?
Syntax · types · names · warnings


2 · LINK
Can all pieces form one program?
Missing symbols · duplicates · memory


3 · RUN / HARDWARE
Does the real system behave correctly?
Logic · clocks · pins · wiring · power

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Debugging is controlled observation


Change one thing, then repeat the cycle with new evidence.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Useful debugging statements


1 EXPECTED
What should happen?


2 OBSERVED
What actually happened?


3 EVIDENCE
What did you measure?


4 NEXT TEST
What is the smallest check?



“It does not work” contains none of these.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
Breakpoints and variables


Pause → inspect state → compare with prediction → step → observe again

title: EE5203 Microprocessors
text:
A controlled experiment


BASELINE
HAL_Delay(500);
Observe the blink period.


→
one change


MODIFIED
HAL_Delay(100);
Predict before flashing.



What stays fixed? What changes? Which physical observation would confirm it?

Symptom

First error, not the last; file saved; braces and semicolons


Flash fails
ST-LINK connected; no other debug session holding it

Correct board, PA5/LD2 configuration, code in user region


Change has no effect
Rebuild and flash the new binary; confirm the edited project



Collect evidence before changing multiple settings.

title: EE5203 L01 Study Sheet
text:
Embedded Systems, the C Toolchain, and Debugging - Basri Erdogan

Essential question: How does a line of C code become an observable change on an STM32 board, and what evidence verifies each step?




Distinguish a processor, microcontroller, and embedded system.
Trace source code through compile, link, flash, and execution.
Identify the compiler, linker, programmer, and debugger roles.
Explain why a successful build, successful flash, and correct behavior are different claims.
Use a breakpoint and variable inspection as engineering evidence.
Describe the course policy: register literacy, HAL fluency.




Input -> STM32 microcontroller -> Output
              |
       software + memory
       + peripherals + timing







Term
Meaning in this course




Processor
The CPU core that executes instructions


Microcontroller
Processor, memory, and peripherals on one chip


Embedded system
Purpose-built hardware and software performing a dedicated function


Peripheral
Hardware block such as GPIO, timer, ADC, UART, I2C, or SPI






C source -> compiler -> object files -> linker -> .elf firmware
                                                |
                                         flash/program
                                                v
                                          STM32 memory








Stage
Evidence
Typical failure




Compile
Source translated; errors/warnings reported
Syntax, type, or undeclared name


Link
Objects/libraries combined into .elf
Missing definition or memory problem


Flash
Firmware written to the board
Board/debug connection problem


Run/test
Observable requirement checked
Logic, configuration, or hardware problem






while (1)
{
    HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(LD2_GPIO_Port, LD2_Pin);
    HAL_Delay(500);
}

while (1) repeats indefinitely.
HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(...) changes the physical LD2 state.
HAL_Delay(500) blocks for approximately 500 ms.
We use HAL here to learn the toolchain. Direct GPIO registers follow after the four-lecture Embedded C Foundation.











Item
Nucleo-F401RE fact




MCU
STM32F401RE


Processor core
Arm Cortex-M4


Programmer/debugger
On-board ST-LINK


User LED
LD2 on PA5


User button
B1 on PC13


Development environment
VS Code + STM32CubeMX (STM32CubeCLT toolchain)






Predict -> Run -> Observe -> Compare -> Change one thing -> Repeat
Before asking for implementation help, state:

expected behavior;
observed behavior;
evidence collected;
next smallest test.





Treating “build succeeded” as proof that the hardware works.
Reading the last compiler error instead of the first relevant error.
Editing one project but building/flashing a different project.
Writing application code outside protected USER CODE regions.
Changing several settings at once and losing the cause-effect relationship.
Using a power-only USB cable rather than a data cable.





I can explain compiler versus linker.
I can identify the .elf file’s role.
I can distinguish build, flash, and behavior evidence.
I can create a breakpoint and predict where execution pauses.
I can explain why HAL_Delay(100) blinks faster than HAL_Delay(500).
I can name the board, MCU, core, programmer, LED pin, and button pin.
I can describe when this course uses registers and when it uses HAL.





What does a microcontroller contain that a processor alone may not?
What is the linker’s job?
What does a successful flash prove?
Why is a breakpoint useful in a working program?
What single observation would confirm that a delay modification reached the board?

See the L01 worksheet for extended practice. Worked solutions are released after the practice window.

title: EE5203 L01 Lab Reference
text:
The toolchain, by name
You use two applications and one invisible toolbox:







Tool
Job




STM32CubeMX
select the board, configure pins, generate the C project


VS Code (STM32 profile)
edit, build, flash, and debug that project


STM32CubeCLT (behind the scenes)
the compiler, CMake/Ninja build tools, ST-LINK debug server that VS Code drives



On laboratory PCs, open VS Code through the EE5203 desktop shortcut — it selects the course profile with the correct extensions.

title: EE5203 L01 Lab Reference
text:
Workflow reference








Stage
Where / action
Expected evidence




Create
STM32CubeMX → Board Selector → NUCLEO-F401RE; accept default initialization
board name in the project view


Configure
Pinout & Configuration tab
PA5 labeled LD2 / GPIO output


Generate
Project Manager: name, location (your C:\Work folder), Toolchain/IDE = CMake → Generate Code
project folder with Core/Inc, Core/Src, main.c, CMakeLists.txt


Open
VS Code → File → Open Folder → the generated folder
STM32/CMake extension picks up the project (accept the default preset if asked)


Edit
Core/Src/main.c, inside protected USER CODE regions
application statements preserved on regeneration


Build
Build button in the status bar (or Terminal → Run Build Task)
Build finished with 0 errors in the terminal; .elf in build/


Flash + run
press F5 (Run → Start Debugging)
firmware downloads over ST-LINK; execution stops at main


Debug
debug toolbar + side panels
execution marker, breakpoints, variables



F5 is both the flash and the debug step — there is no separate “program the board” click in the normal loop. Press F5, then Continue to let the program run.

title: EE5203 L01 Lab Reference
text:
Debug controls (toolbar, left to right)







Control
Meaning




Continue (F5)
run until the next breakpoint


Pause
suspend a running program


Step over (F10)
execute the current line without entering called functions


Step into (F11)
enter the called function when debug info permits


Step out (Shift+F11)
run until the current function returns


Restart
reset and run again from the beginning


Stop (Shift+F5)
end the debug session

title: EE5203 L01 Lab Reference
text:
Evidence panels (debug side bar)








Panel
What it shows
Course name




VARIABLES / WATCH
C variables; add expressions to Watch
variable inspection


PERIPHERALS
live peripheral register bits (from the chip’s SVD description)
the SFR view — register evidence lives here


MEMORY
raw memory bytes at an address
Memory view


Disassembly View (right-click → Open Disassembly View)
the machine instructions behind your C
Disassembly view



When a lab guide says “inspect X in the SFR view”, it means the PERIPHERALS panel.

title: EE5203 L01 Lab Reference
EE5203 L01 Lab Reference
text:
First-day troubleshooting

Board not detected

Confirm the board’s power LED.
Use the ST-LINK USB connector and a known data cable and port.
Close other software that may hold ST-LINK (CubeMX programmer window, a second debug session).
Reconnect the board, then press F5 again.



Build fails

Save the edited file (unsaved dot on the tab?).
Read the first relevant error in the terminal and its file/line.
Check braces, parentheses, semicolons, and names.
Confirm VS Code has the intended folder open (title bar).



F5 flashes but behavior is unchanged

Confirm the build actually ran before the flash (watch the terminal).
Set a breakpoint in the changed code — is it hit?
Inspect toggle_count to determine whether the loop executes.



Code disappears after regeneration

Move application changes into protected USER CODE regions.
Treat the .ioc as the generated-configuration source of truth: pin changes happen in CubeMX, code changes in VS Code.



Working on lab PCs
Work in your local C:\Work\<studentnumber> folder, never directly on the network drive. At the end of the session run the desktop Save to H: shortcut — it copies your project (without build/) to your student folder.

title: EE5203 L01 Lab Reference
text:
Help request format
Use this template:
Expected:
Observed:
Evidence collected:
Smallest next test:

title: EE5203 L03 Starter Files
text:
EE5203 L03 Starter Files
Copy these files into a working copy of the completed L02 Nucleo-F401RE project:

Core/Inc/sensor_math.h -> project Core/Inc
Core/Inc/status_text.h -> project Core/Inc
Core/Src/sensor_math.c -> project Core/Src
Core/Src/status_text.c -> project Core/Src

The starter implementations compile but intentionally return placeholder results. Complete every TODO using the L03 lab guide. Add the fixed arrays, debugger-visible result variables, and pipeline calls to generated main.c only inside its USER CODE regions.
Before copying the main.c declarations, be able to explain them:

uint16_t is an unsigned 16-bit whole-number type; it can hold 0 through 65,535 and comes from <stdint.h>.
ordinary int is used for the lab’s small counts, indices, and capacities.
bool stores true or false and comes from <stdbool.h>.
#define SAMPLE_COUNT 8 gives the preprocessor a readable name to replace before C compilation; it is not a variable.

Do not add these files to a different project without checking its include paths and source folders.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — Syllabus
text:
EE5203 develops embedded-system competence through a C-first STM32 pathway. You first build reliable Embedded C foundations, then learn enough register-level programming to understand the machine, and finally use the STM32 HAL to implement increasingly complex peripherals and an integrated project of your own.
Four ideas shape every week:

Embedded C comes first. Four full lectures build the C you need; everything later assumes it.
Register literacy, HAL fluency. You implement GPIO directly on registers once, so that when you use HAL afterwards you know what it does.
Labs fade their scaffolding. Early labs walk you through the workflow; later labs hand you a specification and expect a working, explained result.
The project starts early. You see the destination in Lecture 1, form teams mid-term, and demonstrate working subsystems as milestones — not one big demo at the end.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — Syllabus
text:
EE5203 develops embedded-system competence through a C-first STM32 pathway. You first build reliable Embedded C foundations, then learn enough register-level programming to understand the machine, and finally use the STM32 HAL to implement increasingly complex peripherals and an integrated project of your own.
Four ideas shape every week:

Embedded C comes first. Four full lectures build the C you need; everything later assumes it.
Register literacy, HAL fluency. You implement GPIO directly on registers once, so that when you use HAL afterwards you know what it does.
Labs fade their scaffolding. Early labs walk you through the workflow; later labs hand you a specification and expect a working, explained result.
The project starts early. You see the destination in Lecture 1, form teams mid-term, and demonstrate working subsystems as milestones — not one big demo at the end.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — Syllabus
text:
Learning outcomes
By the end of the course, a successful student can:

Write, compile, debug, and test Embedded C programs.
Explain the Cortex-M memory, register, and exception model.
Configure GPIO, timers, PWM, ADC, UART, and a synchronous serial peripheral using an appropriate combination of CMSIS register access and STM32 HAL.
Design interrupt-driven, non-blocking embedded applications.
Interpret datasheets, reference manuals, timing diagrams, compiler messages, debugger state, and peripheral registers as engineering evidence.
Integrate, test, document, demonstrate, and individually explain an STM32-based embedded system.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — Syllabus
text:
Tools and hardware



Item
What we use




Board
ST Nucleo-F401RE (provided in the laboratory)


Configuration
STM32CubeMX (pin configuration and project generation)


Development
VS Code with the course STM32 profile (edit, build, flash, debug)


Where things run
Laboratory PCs are fully set up; personal installation instructions are on the L01 lab reference page

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — Syllabus
text:
Weekly rhythm
Each lecture package on this site has the same parts, used in the same order:

Prepare — a short study task and the pre-lab quiz (taken in CATS, two attempts, highest score counts).
Learn — the theory session and its study sheet.
Practise — a 30-45 minute worksheet; worked solutions are released in CATS after the corresponding session.
Lab — the lab guide, with an individual checkoff and a short oral check. Every lab follows the same cycle: warm-up, foundation checkpoint, independent core task, individual modification, oral check.
Review — a mastery checklist and exam-style questions inside the study sheet.

Graded pre-lab quizzes and homework are only in CATS — they are not published on this site. Announcements, submissions, and the weekly calendar schedule also live in CATS.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — Syllabus
text:
Course sequence
Lecture numbers describe instructional order, not calendar weeks.









ID
Topic
Central performance
Project step




L01
Embedded systems, C toolchain, and debugging
Build, flash, debug, and modify Blinky
See example projects


L02
Values, decisions, loops, and arrays
Process a sensor-data array in one C file
Identify project inputs


L03
Functions, arrays, strings, and modular C
Decompose the sensor program into functions and files
Identify processing functions


L04
Pointers, structures, bits, registers, and GPIO
Control PA5 through CMSIS registers
Individual idea canvas


L05
GPIO and hardware abstraction
Rebuild GPIO with HAL and compare the two interfaces
Teams form; project launch


L06
Interrupts and the NVIC
Implement and explain an event-driven application
Proposal and block diagram


L07
Timers and non-blocking time
Generate periodic events without blocking delays
Design review


L08
PWM and motor control
Generate and control timed output signals
First peripheral milestone


L09
Input capture
Measure signals you generate
Measurement milestone


L10
ADC and sensor acquisition
Acquire, scale, and validate analog data
Data-acquisition milestone


L11
The bridge to assembly
Read the disassembly behind your C
—


L12
I2C and SPI
Interface a synchronous serial device
Sensor/display integration


L13
DMA, displays, and integration
Integrate peripherals and diagnose faults
Working prototype


L14
Demonstration and defense
Demonstrate, test, and individually explain the system
Final assessment

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — Syllabus
text:
Assessment
Assessment combines a midterm (including an individual toolchain practical), laboratory performance (individual checkoffs plus pre-lab quizzes and homework), the term project (proposal, milestones, integration, demo, report, and oral defense), and a final examination. Official weights follow the ECTS course record; any clarifications are announced in CATS.
Laboratory credit reflects directly observed performance: checkoffs are individual, and every lab ends with a short oral check where you explain your own work.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — Syllabus

Milestone

title: EE5203 L01 Lab Guide
text:
Build, flash, debug, and modify Blinky - Basri Erdogan

Mission: Create a Nucleo-F401RE project, make LD2 blink, prove that the program executes in the debugger, and independently change its timing.




Complete the five-question pre-lab quiz.
Bring the Question 5 entry ticket.
Confirm VS Code + STM32CubeMX are installed (see the Reference page’s toolchain table) or identify that you need a laboratory PC.
Bring a USB data cable if using your own equipment.




By checkoff time, you can:

build, flash, run, and debug a Nucleo-F401RE project;
distinguish build evidence from hardware evidence;
set a breakpoint and inspect a variable;
make and explain one controlled code modification.











Item
Required value




Board
Nucleo-F401RE


Programmer/debugger
On-board ST-LINK USB connection


Output
LD2, connected to PA5


Tools
STM32CubeMX (configure/generate) + VS Code (edit/build/debug)


Project name
EE5203_L01_<studentnumber>






The session begins with one live demonstration of the complete workflow:
create -> configure -> generate -> edit -> build -> flash -> debug -> verify
Observe which tool owns each stage: the .ioc configuration lives in STM32CubeMX; main.c, the build terminal, debug controls, and the variable panels live in VS Code. Record questions; do not copy menu clicks without understanding the stage they belong to.




Connect the board through the ST-LINK USB connector.
In STM32CubeMX: create a project for NUCLEO-F401RE (Board Selector) and accept the board’s default peripheral initialization.
Confirm that PA5/LD2 is configured as a GPIO output.
In Project Manager set the name, the location, and Toolchain/IDE = CMake; generate code.
Open the generated folder in VS Code and locate the protected USER CODE regions in main.c.
Build the unchanged project with zero errors.

Evidence for Checkpoint A: Show the selected board, PA5 configuration, and a successful build message.



Inside the existing generated while (1) user-code region, add these statements. Do not create a second infinite loop.
HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(LD2_GPIO_Port, LD2_Pin);
HAL_Delay(500);
Build, flash, and verify a stable blink. Explain why the physical observation is stronger evidence than the build message alone.



Add before the loop:
int toggle_count = 0;
Increment it after every toggle:
toggle_count = toggle_count + 1;

Place a breakpoint on or immediately after the increment.
Start a debug session.
Predict the value before pressing Resume.
Resume twice and record the observed values.




Observation
Predicted
Observed




First pause




Second pause




Third pause





Evidence for Checkpoint C: Show the breakpoint, execution marker, and changing toggle_count value.



Each student must make one assigned change and demonstrate it individually.



Variant
Requirement




A
Change the delay to 100 ms


B
Change the delay to 250 ms


C
Change the delay to 750 ms


D
Blink three times quickly, then pause for 1 s



Before flashing, state the predicted behavior. Change only what is necessary, then collect physical or debugger evidence.




Correct Nucleo-F401RE project selected.
Project builds without errors.
LD2 exhibits the required core behavior.
Breakpoint is reached and toggle_count changes as predicted.
Individual timing variant is demonstrated.
Student explains build versus flash versus run evidence.
Code remains inside protected user-code regions.




Be prepared for one question and one live change:

What does a successful build prove?
What does ST-LINK do?
Why does the breakpoint pause before or after a particular statement?
Where should application code be placed, and why?
What evidence proves your modified firmware is running?




Submit the requested source/project, one debugger screenshot, the completed prediction table, and a two-sentence debugging report if a failure occurred.



Report expected and observed behavior; read the first relevant error; confirm the project, board, ST-LINK, build, and flash; test whether a breakpoint reaches the loop; then show the TA your evidence and smallest next test.

text:
Write decimal 13 in binary.
Write binary 1010 in decimal.

value | (1U << 3)

title: EE5203 L01 Practice Worksheet
text:
Expected time: 30-45 minutes
Policy: Attempt every question before consulting the worked solutions.

title: EE5203 L01 Practice Worksheet
text:
Part A - Core concepts

For each item, classify it as a processor, microcontroller, peripheral, development board, or embedded system. Some descriptions may contain more than one relevant level; state the most useful classification.

Arm Cortex-M4

STM32F401RE

Nucleo-F401RE

TIM2

A thermostat containing an STM32 board, temperature sensor, and relay

Give one example of each constraint:

timing constraint;

memory constraint;

energy constraint;

safety or reliability constraint.

A project builds with zero errors, but the LED never changes. Explain why this is not a contradiction.

title: EE5203 L01 Practice Worksheet
text:
Part B - Toolchain sequencing

Put these actions in a valid order:

flash firmware;
link object files;
edit main.c;
compile source files;
observe the LED;
inspect a variable at a breakpoint.

Match each symptom to the most likely initial investigation.




Symptom
Investigation choices




expected ';' before '}'
A. Debug connection/USB


undefined reference to process_sample
B. Compile-time syntax


No ST-LINK detected
C. Link-time definition


Program pauses but variable is wrong
D. Run-time logic/state




What evidence would you record for each claim?

The source compiled.

The firmware was written to the board.

The loop executed repeatedly.

The physical output met the timing requirement.

title: EE5203 L01 Practice Worksheet
EE5203 L01 Practice Worksheet
EE5203 L01 Practice Worksheet
text:
Part E - Project connection

Choose one potential project and complete this chain:

input -> acquisition -> processing -> output -> communication

Define the minimum viable version in no more than three sentences. Then name one optional extension that should only begin after the minimum system works.

Unknown type at build

section: Part D - Arrays and the accumulator pattern
text:
Part D - Arrays and the accumulator pattern

c     uint16_t values[4] = { 1200, 4300, 2600, 800 };

List the valid indices.
Which element does values[2] select?
What clamping (limit 4095) changes, if anything?

Trace the full pipeline on the four-element data of Question 12 — clamp above 4095, sum, count strictly above 2500, average over 4:



Result
Value




array after clamping



sum



high_count



average




Then apply the L02 status rule (< 1500 LOW, > 3000 HIGH, else OK) to the average.
One loop, one pass: write a single for loop over values[4] that finds the largest element. State what starting value your max variable needs and why 0 works here but is not a safe general choice.
In the lab program, the clamp (if (samples[i] > 4095) samples[i] = 4095;) is placed before sum += samples[i]; inside the same loop body. If the two statements were swapped, which final results change and by how much, for the lab’s data set {850, 1250, 4095, 4200, 2050, 900, 3100, 2800}?

title: EE5203 L02 Practice Worksheet
text:
Part E - Debugger evidence

You claim your loop visits all eight elements exactly once. Describe the breakpoint placement and the two variables you would watch to prove it, and what values you expect on the first and last pause.
A student’s average is 0 although the samples are clearly nonzero. Give three distinct explanations consistent with a clean build, and one debugger observation that separates each from the others.

text:

Ready for the L02 lab without additional review

title: EE5203 L03 Lab Guide
text:
Process eight sensor values with small C functions - Basri Erdogan

Mission: Copy your completed L02 project, add four C files, clamp eight sensor values, calculate their average, and write "LOW", "OK", or "HIGH". Prove the result with compiler output and debugger evidence.




Bring your working L02 Nucleo-F401RE project and USB data cable.
Review function declarations, definitions, calls, array bounds, and \0.
Download the L03 starter/ folder from the course materials.
Complete this entry ticket before the session warm-up:

uint16_t average_samples(uint16_t values[], int count);

Circle the return type, function name, and two parameters.
Explain why count is required.
State the valid indices when count == 8.




By checkoff time, you can:

read the types, names, and parameters in a C declaration;
connect a declaration, definition, and call across C files;
loop through an array without using an invalid index;
write a C string that fits in its array and ends with \0;
use debugger evidence to justify a result and one controlled change.







Item
Required value




Board
Nucleo-F401RE


Tools
STM32CubeMX + VS Code


Starting point
A working copy of the L02 project


Project name
EE5203_L03_<studentnumber>


Header files
sensor_math.h, status_text.h


Source files
sensor_math.c, status_text.c


Input
Eight fixed uint16_t samples; no ADC yet


Evidence
Build console, breakpoint, Watch/Expressions, Memory view



Keep all edits to generated main.c inside the appropriate USER CODE regions.




Copy the L02 project and rename the copy EE5203_L03_<studentnumber>.
Copy the starter headers into Core/Inc and sources into Core/Src; confirm each .c includes its own .h.
Add these includes inside USER CODE BEGIN Includes in main.c:

#include <stdbool.h>
#include <stdint.h>
#include "sensor_math.h"
#include "status_text.h"

Build the unmodified starter with zero errors, then identify where clamp_u16 is declared, defined, and called.

Evidence for Checkpoint A: Show the four module files, a successful build, and the declaration/definition/call locations for one function.



Inside USER CODE BEGIN PV in main.c:
#define SAMPLE_COUNT       8
#define STATUS_CAPACITY    8
#define SAMPLE_LIMIT       4095U
#define HIGH_THRESHOLD     2500U

uint16_t raw_samples[SAMPLE_COUNT] = {
    850U, 1250U, 4095U, 4200U,
    2050U, 90U, 3100U, 2800U
};

uint16_t bounded_samples[SAMPLE_COUNT];
char status_text[STATUS_CAPACITY];

uint16_t sample_average = 0U;
int samples_above = 0;
bool status_valid = false;
bool lab_done = false;
Inside USER CODE BEGIN 2, before the generated while (1):
for (int i = 0; i < SAMPLE_COUNT; ++i) {
    bounded_samples[i] =
        clamp_u16(raw_samples[i], 0U, SAMPLE_LIMIT);
}

sample_average = average_samples(bounded_samples, SAMPLE_COUNT);
samples_above = count_above(bounded_samples,
                            SAMPLE_COUNT,
                            HIGH_THRESHOLD);
status_valid = make_status(status_text,
                           STATUS_CAPACITY,
                           sample_average);
lab_done = true;
Do not add a second infinite loop. Leave the generated while (1) in place.



Complete these functions in sensor_math.c:
uint16_t clamp_u16(uint16_t value,
                   uint16_t low,
                   uint16_t high);

uint16_t average_samples(uint16_t values[],
                         int count);

int count_above(uint16_t values[],
                int count,
                uint16_t threshold);
Requirements:

clamp_u16 bounds every value into 0U…4095U.
Array loops use i < count; average_samples returns 0U when count == 0 and sums into a uint32_t so the total fits before division.
count_above is strict: values[i] > threshold with HIGH_THRESHOLD = 2500U.

Before debugging, calculate the expected results:



Quantity
Prediction
Observed




bounded_samples[3]




sample_average




samples_above





Evidence for Checkpoint B: Show a breakpoint inside average_samples, the changing index and accumulator, and the final three observed values.



Complete this function in status_text.c:
bool make_status(char destination[],
                 int capacity,
                 uint16_t average);
Requirements:

Thresholds: "LOW" below 1500U, "HIGH" above 3000U, otherwise "OK"; capacity is eight characters including the terminator.
Check the required capacity before writing; write the \0 explicitly.
Return true only after a complete string is written; on insufficient capacity write an empty string when possible and return false.

Inspect status_text in both the Watch panel and the Memory view, and record the character and hexadecimal byte at indices 0 through 4.
Evidence for Checkpoint C: Show the readable string, the terminator byte, and status_valid.



Use a breakpoint at or immediately before lab_done = true;.
Predict the final values first, then inspect every result variable and confirm lab_done flips to true on resume. Collect evidence for each claim:
no index exceeded 7; the 4200 sample became 4095; the average used all eight clamped samples; the status text is null-terminated.



Each student receives one variant. State the prediction before editing, change only what is necessary, then collect fresh evidence.







Variant
Required change




A
Change the 90U sample to 5000U; predict the clamped value and new average.


B
Change HIGH_THRESHOLD from 2500U to 2000U; predict samples_above.


C
Change the HIGH status boundary from 3000U to 2200U; predict the string.


D
Call the array functions with SAMPLE_COUNT - 1; predict which sample is excluded and the new results.



Evidence for Checkpoint E: Demonstrate the new result and explain how the changed input or function call produced the observed output.




Build is clean; declarations and definitions match exactly.
Array loops use explicit length and valid indices; the sum uses uint32_t.
The status string fits, ends with \0, and reports success.
Predictions, observations, and Checkpoint D evidence are recorded.
The assigned individual modification is demonstrated.
All main.c edits stay inside protected user-code regions.




Be prepared for one question and one live change:

What is the difference between declaring and defining a function?
Why can average_samples not discover the caller’s array length?
Why uint32_t for the sum, and where is the \0 byte in your status array?
What does a successful build prove, and what does the debugger prove?
If the linker reports undefined reference, which stage do you inspect?




Submit the four module files, the main.c user-code sections, one debugger screenshot of the final arrays and results, the completed tables, and a three-sentence explanation of your modification.



Read the first compiler or linker message and compare the declaration with its definition; test one small input while watching i, count, and the accumulator (or the string’s capacity and \0 byte); record expected result, observed result, and the smallest next test.

text:
Be prepared for one question and one live change:

What is the difference between declaring and defining a function?
Why can average_samples not discover the caller’s array length?
Why uint32_t for the sum, and where is the \0 byte in your status array?
What does a successful build prove, and what does the debugger prove?
If the linker reports undefined reference, which stage do you inspect?

Message
Stage (compiler/linker)
Likely fix

uint16_t average_samples(uint16_t values[], int count);   /* (i)   */

title: EE5203 L04 Lab Guide
text:
Control LD2 through CMSIS registers - Basri Erdogan

Mission: Configure PA5 and blink the Nucleo-F401RE on-board LED with direct CMSIS register writes. Calculate every mask, inspect the changed registers, and explain why the physical result agrees with the C statements.




Bring the board, USB data cable, and working L03 project.
Review pointers, ->, hexadecimal, and bit masks.
Complete the entry ticket:

int x = 5;
int *p = &x;
*p = 9;

What is stored in p? 2. What is the final value of x?
Which expression selects bit 5?




Use the Nucleo-F401RE, VS Code, and a working L03 project copied as EE5203_L04_<studentnumber>. GPIO ownership is direct CMSIS register access; only HAL_Delay remains. Evidence comes from the build, SFR view, and LD2.



CMSIS expression
Required field




RCC->AHB1ENR
bit 0 enables GPIOA


GPIOA->MODER
bits 11:10 select PA5 mode


GPIOA->BSRR
bit 5 sets PA5; bit 21 resets PA5



Verify the field names in the STM32F401 reference manual. CMSIS provides the C names and addresses through the device header.



Without running code, complete the values:



Purpose
Expression
Hex value




GPIOA clock bit
1U << 0



PA5 mode field
3U << 10



PA5 output pattern
1U << 10



Set PA5
1U << 5



Reset PA5
1U << 21




Explain why PA5 begins at mode bit 5 × 2 = 10.




Copy and rename the L03 project; build with zero errors.
Keep generated initialization calls intact.
Remove the old HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(...) call; keep HAL_Delay(...).
Confirm that main.h is included and the target is STM32F401RE.

Evidence: Show the project name, target device, and successful baseline build.



GPIOA->MODER
Explain: GPIOA is a device-header pointer; -> selects a member through that pointer; MODER is a 32-bit register; and the member is volatile.
After generated initialization, execute this statement and inspect bits 11:10:
GPIOA->MODER &= ~(3U << 10);   // temporary input-mode baseline
Confirm 00. This also establishes a known starting value for Checkpoint D.



Inside USER CODE BEGIN 2, before the generated while (1), add:
RCC->AHB1ENR |= (1U << 0);
Predict bit 0, step over the statement, and inspect RCC->AHB1ENR. OR leaves all other clock bits unchanged.
Evidence: Record the observed register value and mark bit 0.



GPIOA->MODER &= ~(3U << 10);
GPIOA->MODER |=  (1U << 10);
The first statement clears both PA5 mode bits; the second writes output pattern 01. Break after each statement and record:



Moment
Predicted bits 11:10
Observed bits 11:10




After clear
00



After set
01







Inside the existing generated while (1):
GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 5);
HAL_Delay(250);

GPIOA->BSRR = (1U << 21);
HAL_Delay(250);
Bits 0...15 set pins; bits 16...31 reset them. PA5 reset therefore uses bit 5 + 16 = 21. Do not use a HAL GPIO write or toggle call.
Evidence: Demonstrate a stable blink and show both mask calculations.



Break after the PA5 set write, inspect GPIOA in the SFR/peripheral view, and confirm LD2 is on. Continue to the reset write and confirm the opposite state. Do not step through HAL_Delay.
Record three claims: MODER bits 11:10 are 01; the set-state observation agrees with LD2; and the reset-state observation agrees with LD2.



Predict first, make the assigned change, and collect new evidence.







Variant
Required change




A
Change both delays to 100 ms; explain why masks do not change.


B
Keep LD2 on by removing only the reset phase.


C
Replace literal 5 with #define LED_PIN 5U in both BSRR masks.







Build succeeds; no HAL GPIO write/toggle call remains.
GPIOA clock bit 0 is set and PA5 mode bits are 01.
BSRR bit 5 sets LD2 and bit 21 resets it.
Prediction, register evidence, and physical evidence agree.
The assigned individual change is demonstrated.

Oral check: explain GPIOA->MODER, volatile, 3U << 10, BSRR assignment, or why a successful build does not prove LED behavior.



Submit the relevant main.c user-code sections, completed tables, one register-view screenshot, one LD2 photograph/video, and three sentences explaining the controlled change.
Do not submit the entire workspace unless specifically requested.

Part B - Binary and hexadecimal

binary 0000 0100 0001 0001 to hex.

Give both intermediate hex values.

title: EE5203 L05 Lab Guide
text:
GPIO with the HAL — same hardware, new interface - Basri Erdogan

Mission: Rebuild the Week 4 LED control with HAL functions, make B1 control LD2 by polling, and prove in the register view that HAL wrote the same bits you wrote by hand last week.




Re-read the theory slides up to “The polling loop.”
Know last week’s three registers: AHB1ENR, MODER, BSRR.
Know why B1 released reads GPIO_PIN_SET (external pull-up, active-low).




By checkoff time, you can:

configure PA5 (output) and PC13 (input, no pull) in CubeMX and read the generated MX_GPIO_Init();
drive and read pins with HAL_GPIO_WritePin / TogglePin / ReadPin;
verify a HAL configuration in the SFR view down to the register bits;
build a polled button behavior without any direct register writes.











Item
Required value




Board
Nucleo-F401RE (LD2 on PA5, B1 on PC13 — no wiring)


Input
B1, external pull-up: released = SET, pressed = RESET


Project
New CubeMX project L05_HAL_GPIO (Toolchain = CMake), opened in VS Code


Clock
Default HSI is sufficient







New STM32 project → Board Selector → NUCLEO-F401RE; accept default peripheral initialization.
PA5 → GPIO_Output (defaults: push-pull, no pull, low speed).
PC13 → GPIO_Input, no pull-up and no pull-down — the board’s external resistor already holds PC13 high.
Generate code. CubeMX already calls MX_GPIO_Init() in main() before the loop — verify it is there; do not add a second call.




Inside the while (1) user-code region:
HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5);
HAL_Delay(500);
Build, flash, and confirm the blink. Then pause in the debugger after MX_GPIO_Init() and check the SFR view:



Register
Expected




GPIOA->MODER bits 11:10
01 (output)


GPIOC->MODER bits 27:26
00 (input)


GPIOC->PUPDR bits 27:26
00 (no internal pull)



Evidence for Checkpoint A: the three register fields above, plus the blink. HAL wrote what you wrote by hand in Week 4.



Replace the loop body: blink while B1 is released, hold LD2 off while B1 is pressed.
GPIO_PinState button = HAL_GPIO_ReadPin(GPIOC, GPIO_PIN_13);

if (button == GPIO_PIN_SET) {          /* released */
    HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5);
    HAL_Delay(500);
} else {                               /* pressed  */
    HAL_GPIO_WritePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5, GPIO_PIN_RESET);
}
Evidence for Checkpoint B: LED dark while B1 is held, blinking resumes on release; watch GPIOC->IDR bit 13 change in the SFR view as you press.



Count presses and cycle four LED behaviors (off → steady → slow blink → double flash). Detect the press edge, not the level:
static uint32_t press_count = 0U;
static uint32_t prev = 1U;                     /* released */

uint32_t cur = (uint32_t)HAL_GPIO_ReadPin(GPIOC, GPIO_PIN_13);
if (prev == 1U && cur == 0U) {                 /* falling edge */
    ++press_count;
    HAL_Delay(10);                             /* debounce sample */
}
prev = cur;

switch (press_count & 0x3U) { /* four cases: your patterns here */ }
Evidence for Checkpoint C: four presses walk through all four patterns and return to the first.



Recreate Checkpoint B without HAL calls: clocks via RCC->AHB1ENR (GPIOA bit 0, GPIOC bit 2), PA5 output via MODER, read GPIOC->IDR bit 13, drive PA5 via BSRR. Leave PC13 with no internal pull — the external resistor is already there. Keep it in a separate source file or #ifdef block.




.ioc: PA5 output, PC13 input with no pull; generated code untouched outside USER CODE regions.
Register proof for all three fields in Checkpoint A.
Button override works and IDR bit 13 evidence shown.
Press counter cycles four patterns with edge detection.
Extension demonstrated in one interface only (no HAL/register mixing).





Which register bits did GPIO_MODE_OUTPUT_PP change, and to what?
Why must PC13 use GPIO_NOPULL on this board?
Why does the press counter compare prev and cur instead of testing cur == 0 alone?
What does a successful build not prove?




Submit main.c (and the bare-metal file if attempted) plus one SFR-view screenshot showing the three Checkpoint A register fields.



Check in order: MX_GPIO_Init() runs before the loop (once); the .ioc pin settings match this card; the SFR view shows the expected MODER/PUPDR fields; IDR bit 13 follows the physical button; only then suspect your loop logic. LED inverted? You swapped SET/RESET meaning. Count jumps by 2–3 per press? Your edge test or debounce delay is missing.

Part B - The two interfaces

Give the direct CMSIS equivalent of each HAL call (masks computed):

HAL_GPIO_WritePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5, GPIO_PIN_SET);
HAL_GPIO_WritePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5, GPIO_PIN_RESET);
reading B1: HAL_GPIO_ReadPin(GPIOC, GPIO_PIN_13) — write the IDR test that yields the same information.

A teammate mixes interfaces: CubeMX configures PA5, then their code does GPIOA->MODER = 0x400; “to be sure”. Name two distinct things this plain assignment breaks on this board.

resistor to ground, switch to 3.3 V;
no resistor, switch to ground.

Which case is a bug, and what would the program observe?
The board’s B1 has external R30 to 3.3 V. A student nevertheless configures GPIO_PULLUP. Explain what is now physically on the pin, and whether pressed/released readings change.

B1
ReadPin returns
branch taken
LD2




released





pressed
