title: EE5203 L06 Lab Guide
text:
Button interrupts — replace polling with EXTI - Basri Erdogan

Mission: Configure B1 (PC13) as a falling-edge interrupt so that one valid press toggles LD2 (PA5) — without reading the button anywhere in while (1).




Re-read the theory slides up to “The complete interrupt path.”
Bring your working Week 5 HAL GPIO project.
Know the answer to: why does pressing B1 produce a falling edge?




By checkoff time, you can:

configure an EXTI pin and enable its NVIC request in CubeMX;
prove with a breakpoint that the interrupt path reaches your callback;
pass an event from the callback to main() with a volatile flag;
debounce the button so one press produces exactly one event.











Item
Required value




Board
Nucleo-F401RE (no wiring — B1 and LD2 are on the board)


Input
B1 on PC13, external pull-up, low while pressed


Output
LD2 on PA5, high turns the LED on


Starting point
Copy of your Week 5 project, saved as L06_Button_Interrupt






Open the .ioc file. Keep PA5 as GPIO_Output (label LD2). Then:

Click PC13 and select GPIO_EXTI13.
In System Core → GPIO: falling-edge interrupt mode, no pull-up and no pull-down (the board’s external resistor already holds PC13 high).
In System Core → NVIC: enable EXTI line[15:10] interrupts (PC13 is line 13; lines 10–15 share this entry). Leave the priority at its default.
Generate code, then verify the generated setup in MX_GPIO_Init():

GPIO_InitStruct.Mode = GPIO_MODE_IT_FALLING;
GPIO_InitStruct.Pull = GPIO_NOPULL;
HAL_NVIC_EnableIRQ(EXTI15_10_IRQn);
Write code only inside USER CODE blocks.



Add below main() in the user-code section for functions:
void HAL_GPIO_EXTI_Callback(uint16_t GPIO_Pin)
{
    if (GPIO_Pin == GPIO_PIN_13) {
        HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5);
    }
}
Build, flash, press B1 — the LED responds without any button code in the loop.
Evidence for Checkpoint A: a breakpoint on the if line is hit when you press B1, and GPIO_Pin equals GPIO_PIN_13. If execution never stops, recheck the PC13 mode and the NVIC checkbox.



The LED action does not belong in the callback. In USER CODE BEGIN PV:
volatile uint8_t button_event = 0;
Change the callback body to only record the event (button_event = 1;), and let the main loop consume it:
if (button_event == 1) {
    button_event = 0;               /* consume before acting */
    HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5);
}



A real contact bounces — several falling edges per press:





Add uint32_t last_press_ms = 0; beside the flag and accept a press only when at least 80 ms has passed since the last accepted one:
uint32_t now = HAL_GetTick();
if ((now - last_press_ms) >= 80) {
    last_press_ms = now;
    button_event = 1;
}
The subtraction stays correct even when the millisecond counter wraps.






Test
Expected observation




press once slowly
LD2 toggles once


press ten times
LD2 toggles ten times


hold B1 down
no repeated toggling


release B1
no toggle



Record unexpected behavior before changing the code.



Toggle LD2 only after two valid presses (a counter in the main loop); the interrupt and debounce code stay unchanged.




.ioc shows PC13 = GPIO_EXTI13, falling edge, no pull, NVIC enabled.
Breakpoint proves HAL_GPIO_EXTI_Callback runs on a press.
button_event is declared volatile; the LED action lives in main().
Ten deliberate presses produce ten reliable state changes.
Individual extension demonstrated.




Be ready for one question and one live change:

Why does B1 use a falling edge and no internal pull?
What runs first: EXTI15_10_IRQHandler() or your callback?
Why must the callback stay short, and why is volatile required?
What would happen without the 80 ms guard?




Submit the project source and one debugger screenshot showing the breakpoint inside your callback.

title: EE5203 L06 Practice Worksheet
text: Expected time: 30-45 minutes Policy: Attempt every question before consulting the worked solutions. The graded homework (posted in CATS) contains the repair-and-extend work; this worksheet is your rehearsal for it.

title: EE5203 L06 Practice Worksheet
section: Part A - The path and its names
text:
Part A - The path and its names

Put these stations in the order a B1 press visits them, and give each one’s one-line job:
NVIC · HAL_GPIO_EXTI_Callback · falling edge on PC13 · EXTI15_10_IRQHandler · vector table · EXTI13 edge detector · HAL_GPIO_EXTI_IRQHandler · EXTI15_10_IRQn
Why do PC13, PB13, and PA13 all map to EXTI13 — and why can only one of them use it at a time? Name the register field that stores the choice and its value in our configuration.
Lines 10-15 share one IRQ. If a design used interrupts on both PC13 and PB11, describe what the handler would have to do that our single-button handler does not.

title: EE5203 L06 Practice Worksheet
text:
Part B - Configuration and registers

For each of the three CubeMX choices on the lab guide, name the register bit(s) the generated code writes as a result:

PC13 = GPIO_EXTI13, falling mode;
no pull-up / no pull-down;
NVIC checkbox for EXTI line[15:10].

EXTI->PR is write-1-to-clear. A student “clears” it with EXTI->PR = 0; and separately another writes EXTI->PR |= (1U << 13);. Explain what each statement actually does, and why the |= version is a subtle bug on a register with several pending lines.
Predict EXTI->PR bit 13 at each moment:



Moment
PR bit 13




before any press



paused at a breakpoint inside EXTI15_10_IRQHandler



paused at a breakpoint inside your callback



back in while (1) after the press




With the NVIC line disabled but everything else configured, a press occurs. State the value of IMR bit 13, FTSR bit 13, PR bit 13, and whether the handler runs.

title: EE5203 L06 Practice Worksheet
section: Part C - Callback discipline and volatile

title: EE5203 L06 Practice Worksheet
text:
Part D - Debounce

Falling edges arrive at t = 5000, 5003, 5008, 5012, 5240, 5310 ms. Trace the 80 ms guard: for each edge state accepted/rejected, the reason, and the value of last_press_ms afterwards. How many events does main() see?
Why is the guard’s subtraction (now - last_press_ms) >= 80 still correct when HAL_GetTick() wraps from 0xFFFFFFFF to 0? What property of unsigned arithmetic saves it?
Give one user-visible failure of choosing 5 ms for the guard, and one of choosing 500 ms — and say why 80 ms sits comfortably between them.

title: EE5203 L06 Practice Worksheet
section: Part E - Evidence and behavior tests
text:
Part E - Evidence and behavior tests

Your claim: “the LED action runs in main(), not in interrupt context.” Describe a two-breakpoint experiment that proves it.
Complete the expected-observation column, then add one test of your own that would expose a missing debounce:



Test
Expected observation




one slow press



hold B1 for 3 seconds



release B1



ten quick presses




A classmate’s LED toggles on release instead of press. Name the single .ioc setting that explains it and the register bit that would confirm your diagnosis.

text:

Need to review volatile and the consume pattern
Need to review the debounce arithmetic

title: EE5203 L07 Lab Guide

Timers → TIM2: Clock Source = Internal Clock; Prescaler 15 (16 MHz ÷ 16 = 1 MHz), Counter Period 999 (1 MHz ÷ 1000 → 1 ms).
System Core → NVIC: enable TIM2 global interrupt.

CNT frozen? Timer never started or clock not enabled. Callback never runs? NVIC checkbox or Start_IT missing. Pattern timing drifts? You reset t_phase to g_ms somewhere other than the phase change.

title: EE5203 L08 Lab Guide
text:
PWM — fading LEDs and controlling servos - Basri Erdogan

Mission: Breathe LD2 with TIM2 PWM, then position and sweep a hobby servo with TIM3—every PSC/ARR/CCR value chosen from the 16 MHz clock, every brightness change scheduled by the Week 7 tick, not by HAL_Delay().




Re-read the theory slides up to “Positioning the servo.”
Bring your working L07 timer project (TIM2 tick interrupt).
Know the answer to: with a 1 µs tick, what ARR gives 1 kHz? What gives 50 Hz?
Servo group kit: SG90-class servo + 3 jumpers (5V, GND, signal).




By checkoff time, you can:

configure a timer channel for PWM and justify PSC/ARR/CCR from 16 MHz;
show the alternate-function handover of a pin in the .ioc and the code;
change duty live—from code and from the SFR view;
command servo angles as pulse widths in microseconds, clamped to the contract.











Item
Required value




Board
Nucleo-F401RE — LD2 is TIM2_CH1 (PA5): zero wiring for Part 1


LED PWM
TIM2 CH1, PSC 15, ARR 999 → 1 kHz, µs units


Servo PWM
TIM3 CH1 on PA6 (D12), PSC 15, ARR 19999 → 50 Hz


Servo wiring
red → 5V, brown/black → GND, signal → PA6 (D12)






Starting from a copy of the L07 project, saved as L08_PWM:

Click PA5 → TIM2_CH1; Timers → TIM2: Channel 1 = PWM Generation CH1, PSC 15, ARR 999.
Click PA6 → TIM3_CH1; Timers → TIM3: Channel 1 = PWM Generation CH1, PSC 15, ARR 19999.
Generate; find both AF assignments in the generated GPIO init before writing any code of your own.




HAL_TIM_PWM_Start(&htim2, TIM_CHANNEL_1);
__HAL_TIM_SET_COMPARE(&htim2, TIM_CHANNEL_1, 350);   // 35 %
Evidence for Checkpoint A: LD2 visibly dimmer than full-on; in the SFR view, write 700 directly into TIM2->CCR1 while paused and watch the brightness step—no code changed, no reflash.



No HAL_Delay(): reuse the L07 tick. Every 10 ms flag from your timer interrupt, step the duty:
if (tick_10ms_flag) {
    tick_10ms_flag = 0;
    duty += dir * 5;
    if (duty >= 999) { duty = 999; dir = -1; }
    if (duty <= 0)   { duty = 0;   dir = +1; }
    __HAL_TIM_SET_COMPARE(&htim2, TIM_CHANNEL_1, duty);
}
Evidence for Checkpoint B: smooth breathing; predict-then-check: what does changing the step from 5 to 20 do to the breathing period?



At a 1 µs tick, CCR is the pulse width. Clamp to the contract:
uint16_t servo_us(uint8_t deg) {          // 0-180 deg -> 1000-2000 us
    if (deg > 180) deg = 180;
    return 1000 + (1000u * deg) / 180u;
}
HAL_TIM_PWM_Start(&htim3, TIM_CHANNEL_1);
__HAL_TIM_SET_COMPARE(&htim3, TIM_CHANNEL_1, servo_us(90));
Evidence for Checkpoint C: servo holds 0°, 90°, 180° on command; then a slow sweep stepped from the tick (not HAL_Delay(20) in a tight loop). Predict first: what CCR value is 45°?



Pick one, predict first:

Button-gated sweep: the servo only moves while B1 (PC13) is held— combine with Week 6’s EXTI or poll in the tick.
Gamma breathing: linear duty steps look uneven to the eye; try stepping through a small squared lookup table and compare.
Second channel: add TIM2 CH2 on PA1 with a different duty—two brightnesses, one timer, same frequency. Explain why the frequency must be shared.





.ioc: PA5 → TIM2_CH1 and PA6 → TIM3_CH1 (AF handover visible), PSC 15 both.
LED at 1 kHz / servo at 50 Hz, both derived from 16 MHz on paper.
Breathing scheduled by the timer tick — no HAL_Delay() in the loop.
Servo commands clamped to 1000–2000 µs.
One live duty change shown from the SFR view.





Why does the LED need ARR = 999 but the servo ARR = 19999?
What duty cycle is a 1.5 ms servo pulse—and why doesn’t the servo care?
Why can’t the servo share TIM2 with the 1 kHz LED?
CCR = 1000 on the LED timer: what does the pin do? And CCR = 0?
Where in the generated code did PA5 stop being a GPIO output?




Submit main.c and one SFR-view screenshot showing TIM2->CCR1 mid-breath, plus your frequency derivations (16 MHz → PSC → tick → ARR) for both timers.



Walk the chain: PWM_Start called for that channel? CCR actually nonzero? Pin really in AF mode in the .ioc? Then: LED full-on/off only — CCR outside 0…ARR, or you wrote to the wrong channel; servo buzzes at the end stops — commands outside 1000–2000 µs: clamp; servo jitters — power it from 5V (not 3V3) and share GND with the board; breathing is jerky — you are stepping from HAL_Delay() in a loop that also does other work; step from the tick flag instead.

title: EE5203 L09 Lab Guide
text:
Input capture — measure your own PWM - Basri Erdogan

Mission: Close the loop: TIM2 generates PWM on PA5 (your L08 project), one jumper wire carries it to PB6, and TIM4 captures it back. Measure the frequency and duty you configured—and prove they match.




Re-read the theory slides up to “The indirect trick.”
Bring your working L08 PWM project (TIM2 CH1 on PA5).
Bring one jumper wire. On the Arduino header: PA5 = D13, PB6 = D10.
Know the answer to: at a 1 µs tick, what period do ARR = 999 captures show?




By checkoff time, you can:

configure direct and indirect capture channels on TIM4;
turn hardware timestamps into period, frequency, and duty—wrap-safe;
verify a measurement against the generating side’s own registers;
observe CC1IF, a live polarity flip, and an overcapture in the SFR view.







Item
Required value




Board
Nucleo-F401RE


Wiring
one jumper: PA5 (D13) → PB6 (D10)


Generator
TIM2 CH1 PWM on PA5 — 1 kHz, from the L08 project


Analyzer
TIM4 CH1/CH2 capture on PB6, 1 µs tick, free-run






Starting from a copy of the L08 project, saved as L09_Capture:

PB6 → TIM4_CH1.
Timers → TIM4: Channel 1 = Input Capture direct mode, polarity rising; Channel 2 = Input Capture indirect mode, polarity falling.
Prescaler 15 (1 µs tick), Counter Period 65535 (free-run).
NVIC: enable TIM4 global interrupt. Generate; verify — do not add.




Start both sides:
HAL_TIM_PWM_Start(&htim2, TIM_CHANNEL_1);            // generator, 35 %
__HAL_TIM_SET_COMPARE(&htim2, TIM_CHANNEL_1, 350);
HAL_TIM_IC_Start_IT(&htim4, TIM_CHANNEL_1);          // analyzer
HAL_TIM_IC_Start_IT(&htim4, TIM_CHANNEL_2);
Evidence for Checkpoint A: a breakpoint in HAL_TIM_IC_CaptureCallback is hit; in the SFR view TIM4->CCR1 changes by itself between pauses—hardware is timestamping without you.



In the callback (CH1, rising edges):
uint16_t now = HAL_TIM_ReadCapturedValue(htim, TIM_CHANNEL_1);
period_us = (uint16_t)(now - last);    // wrap-safe on 16 bits
last = now;
Predict first: the generator runs PSC = 15, ARR = 999 → what should period_us read? Then check the live variable.
Evidence for Checkpoint B: period_us ~ 1000; change the generator’s ARR to 1999 and watch the measurement follow to ~ 2000 (and the LED frequency halve).



CH2 (falling, indirect) gives the high time relative to the last rising edge:
high_us = (uint16_t)(HAL_TIM_ReadCapturedValue(htim, TIM_CHANNEL_2) - last);
Duty = high_us / period_us. With the generator at CCR1 = 350, predict both numbers before looking.
Evidence for Checkpoint C: measured duty ~ 35 %; run the L08 breathing sweep on the generator and watch the measured duty track it live.



Pick one, predict first:

Overcapture: pause in the debugger through several PWM periods, resume, and find CC1OF set in TIM4->SR. Explain what was lost.
Polarity flip: clear/set CC1P in TIM4->CCER from the SFR view and explain what the period measurement does (and does not) change.
Ultrasonic (hardware permitting): HC-SR04 echo width on CH1/CH2; distance_cm = width_us / 58.





.ioc: TIM4 CH1 direct/rising, CH2 indirect/falling, PSC 15, free-run.
Callback checks instance and channel; subtractions are uint16_t.
Measured period and duty match the generator’s ARR/CCR numbers.
Measurement tracks a live change on the generating side.
One extension demonstrated with the SFR evidence.





Who writes TIM4->CCR1, and at what instant exactly?
Why must the subtraction be cast to uint16_t?
Why is CH2 configured indirect? What would direct listen to?
Your period reads 3036 after captures 64000 and 1500—explain.
What is the slowest signal this 16-bit setup can measure honestly?




Submit main.c and one SFR-view screenshot showing TIM4->CCR1, CCR2, and SR with a capture flag set—plus your predicted-vs-measured table for Checkpoints B and C.



Walk the chain: jumper seated (D13 → D10)? Generator actually running (LED dim/bright)? PB6 in AF2 in the .ioc? Start_IT called for both channels? Breakpoint in the callback reached? Then: period garbage — check the cast and the polarity; duty > 100 % — CH2 is probably direct, or the edges are swapped; CCR1 frozen — capture never started or the NVIC box is unticked.

text: Walk the chain: pins really analog in the .ioc? adc_read returns at all? Then: stuck at 0 or 4095 — wiring: wiper actually on PA0, divider junction actually on PA1; values sag or jitter — sampling time too short, or the pin is floating; PollForConversion times out — no conversion started (Start before Poll) or EOC selection left unset; servo buzzes at the end stops — commands outside 1000–2000 µs: clamp; night-light inverted — your divider has the LDR on top: swap the map or the resistor.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Welcome to Week 10: Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
Bridging the Analog and Digital Worlds

This week, we explore Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs).
ADCs are crucial for microcontrollers to interact with the real, analog world.

Learning Objectives for Week 10

Understand the principles of Analog-to-Digital Conversion.
Learn key ADC parameters: resolution, sampling rate, accuracy.
Configure the ADC for single-channel, continuous conversion.
Read and interpret analog sensor data.
Explore applications of ADCs in embedded systems.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
section: The Analog World vs. The Digital World
text:
The Analog World vs. The Digital World
Why ADC is Needed

Analog World: Continuous signals (e.g., temperature, light, pressure, sound).

Infinite number of values within a range.

Digital World: Discrete values (0s and 1s) that microcontrollers understand.



ADCs is a bridge, converting continuous analog signals into discrete digital values.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Principles of ADC: Sampling
Taking Snapshots

Sampling: The process of taking discrete measurements (samples) of an analog signal at regular intervals.
Sampling Rate: How often samples are taken (samples per second, Hz).
Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem: To accurately reconstruct an analog signal, the sampling rate must be at least twice the highest frequency component of the signal.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Principles of ADC: Quantization
Assigning Digital Values

Quantization: The process of converting the sampled analog voltage into a discrete digital value.
The range of analog input voltages is divided into a finite number of levels.
Each level is assigned a unique digital code.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Principles of ADC: Encoding
Representing as Binary

Encoding: The process of representing the quantized digital value as a binary code (0s and 1s).
The number of bits used for encoding determines the ADC’s resolution.

Example

A 12-bit ADC will encode the quantized value into a 12-bit binary number.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Key ADC Parameters: Resolution
How Fine-Grained the Measurement Is

Resolution: The number of discrete digital values an ADC can produce.
Expressed in bits (e.g., 8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit).
An N-bit ADC can represent 2^N distinct values.

Calculation

Step Size (LSB): The smallest change in analog voltage that the ADC can detect.

Step Size = V_REF / (2^N - 1) (for unipolar, N-bit ADC)
V_REF: Reference voltage.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Resolution in Pictures




Signal range: 0 to 3.3 V
Left plot: 8 levels (~3 bits) → LSB ≈ 0.47 V
Right plot: 64 levels (~8 bits) → LSB ≈ 0.052 V

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Key ADC Parameters: Sampling Rate
How Fast Measurements Are Taken

Sampling Rate: The number of conversions performed per second.
Measured in Samples Per Second (SPS) or Hertz (Hz).
Determined by the ADC clock frequency and the number of clock cycles per conversion.

Importance

Higher sampling rates are needed for faster-changing analog signals.
Too low a sampling rate can lead to aliasing (misrepresentation of the signal).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Key ADC Parameters: Accuracy
How Close to the True Value

Accuracy: How close the ADC’s digital output is to the true analog input value.
Affected by various factors:

Quantization Error: Inherent error due to discrete nature of digital values.
Non-linearity: Deviations from ideal linear response.

Noise: Electrical noise in the system.
Reference Voltage Stability: Fluctuations in V_REF directly impact accuracy.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Signal Conditioning for ADCs
Why Condition Signals?

Real-world sensors rarely output voltages that perfectly match the ADC’s range or bandwidth.
Signal conditioning prepares the signal so the ADC can sample it without clipping, aliasing, or excessive noise.
Typical blocks include filtering, level shifting, amplification/attenuation, and buffering.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Anti-Aliasing & Filtering
Limit Bandwidth Before Sampling

Use a low-pass filter (often simple RC) to remove frequency content above half the sampling rate (Nyquist).
Choose f_c = 1 / (2πRC) slightly below f_s / 2 to provide attenuation margin.
Higher-order (active) filters offer steeper roll-off when the signal spectrum is wide.
For noisy environments, add a small RC at the pin plus an op-amp active filter near the sensor.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text: Aliasing in Action

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Level Shifting & Scaling
Keep the ADC Within Its Input Range

Many sensors output ± signals or voltages greater than V_REF; design attenuation networks so the ADC never saturates.
Use resistor dividers for simple scaling, but account for source impedance loading the ADC’s sample-and-hold capacitor.
If you choose very large resistor values, the ADC’s sample-and-hold capacitor may not have enough time to charge to the correct voltage.
Differential or low-level signals benefit from instrumentation amplifiers that provide gain plus common-mode shift.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Buffering & Impedance Considerations
Present a Low-Source Impedance to the ADC

The STM32 ADC samples the input onto an internal capacitor; high source impedance causes droop and conversion errors.
Add an op-amp configured as a voltage follower (unity gain buffer) to isolate the sensor from the ADC pin.
When multiple channels share sensors, use dedicated buffers or sample-and-hold stages to avoid cross-coupling.
Keep traces short and guard the analog node with a ground shield to reduce parasitic capacitance pickup.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Practical Signal-Chain Workflow
From Sensor to Counts

Characterize sensor output range, impedance, and bandwidth from the datasheet.
Decide on required gain/attenuation so the conditioned signal spans most of the ADC span without clipping.
Select filter order/cutoff that suppresses out-of-band content but preserves the signal of interest.
Prototype the chain (e.g., in LTspice) before implementing on the PCB or breadboard, then validate with an oscilloscope.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Types of ADCs (Briefly)
Different Conversion Methods

Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC:

Most common type in microcontrollers.
Converts by successively approximating the input voltage.
Good balance of speed, resolution, and power consumption.

Delta-Sigma (ΔΣ) ADC:

High resolution, good for audio applications.
Slower conversion rates.

Flash ADC:

Very fast, but low resolution and high power consumption.
Used in high-speed applications (e.g., oscilloscopes).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
SAR ADC Block Diagram



Sample-and-hold captures the input voltage.
SAR sets MSB = 1 (others 0) and the DAC generates the trial voltage.
Comparator decides if V_trial is above/below V_in.
SAR keeps bit = 1 if V_trial <= V_in; otherwise clears it.
Repeat for each lower bit until all bits are decided.
Final SAR register word is the digital conversion result.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text: SAR Conversion Animation

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
ADC Configuration: Clocking
Powering the ADC

STM32F4 ADCs sit on the APB2 domain (PCLK2).
Analog domain clock derives from PCLK2 divided by ADCPRE (÷2, ÷4, ÷6, ÷8).
STM32F401RE has only ADC1.
Ensure the divided frequency stays within the ADC max spec (~36 MHz for F401).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
ADC Configuration: Channels
Multiple Analog Inputs

Microcontrollers often have multiple analog input channels.
Each channel corresponds to a specific GPIO pin configured for analog mode.
Single-Channel Conversion: Converting one analog input at a time.
Multi-Channel Conversion: Converting multiple analog inputs sequentially or simultaneously.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
ADC Configuration: Conversion Modes
How Conversions Are Triggered

Single Conversion Mode: ADC performs one conversion and then stops.
Continuous Conversion Mode: ADC continuously performs conversions without external triggers.

Useful for monitoring slowly changing analog signals.

Scan Mode: Converts multiple channels in a sequence.
Discontinuous Mode: Converts a subset of channels.

You still have the same scan sequence, but you tell the ADC to convert it in small groups.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
ADC Conversion Time
Why It Takes Multiple Clock Cycles

A conversion is not instantaneous because the SAR core must sample the input, then perform successive bit decisions.
T_conversion = T_sample (sample time) + T_successive (fixed number of ADC clock cycles necessary for conversion).

Example: T_successive = 12.5 cycles; choosing SamplingTime = 56 cycles gives T_conversion = (56 + 12.5) / f_ADC.

High source impedance requires longer sampling times to settle.
Available sample time options are 3, 15, 28, 56, 84, 12, 144 and 480 cycles

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
End-of-Conversion Behavior
What Happens When EOC Fires

When conversion finishes, the digital value is stored into Data Register (ADCx->DR).
The EOC (End of Conversion) flag in SR is set; firmware can poll this bit, handle it in HAL_ADC_ConvCpltCallback(), or let DMA move the value automatically.
EOC is cleared by reading ADC->DR (CPU or DMA), so ISRs must read it before returning.
In continuous mode, the ADC immediately starts the next sampling phase once DR is updated; in single mode it idles until a new trigger arrives.
Optional events: EOS (End of Sequence) asserts after the final channel in a scan, and OVR (Overrun flag) sets if a new result arrives before the previous DR read.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Reading Analog Sensor Data
Connecting a Sensor



Analog sensors (e.g., potentiometers, LDRs, thermistors) provide a varying voltage output.
This output voltage is connected to an ADC input pin on the microcontroller.
Analog sensors (e.g., potentiometers, LDRs, thermistors) provide a varying voltage output.
This output voltage is connected to an ADC input pin on the microcontroller.
Ensure the sensor voltage stays within 0–V_REF and that the source impedance is low enough for the ADC sample-and-hold.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Interpreting Analog Sensor Data
From Raw Value to Meaningful Units

The ADC provides a raw digital value (e.g., 0-4095 for a 12-bit ADC).
This raw value needs to be converted to a meaningful physical unit (e.g., Volts, degrees Celsius, Lux).

Conversion Formula

V_\text{analog} = \frac{\mathrm{ADC}_{\text{value}}}{2^N - 1} \times V_\text{REF}
Then, use the sensor’s datasheet to convert voltage to physical units.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
STM32 ADC Specifics (Conceptual)
ADC Peripheral Overview

STM32 microcontrollers typically have one or more independent ADC peripherals.
Each ADC has multiple input channels.
Configurable for various modes (single, continuous, scan, DMA).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text: STM32 ADC Simplified Block

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Key ADC Registers (Bare-Metal Conceptual)
Direct Control

CR1 (Control Register 1): Mode selection, scan mode, resolution.
CR2 (Control Register 2): Continuous conversion, data alignment, software start.
SQRx (Sequence Register): Defines the conversion sequence for multiple channels.
DR (Data Register): Holds the converted digital value.
SR (Status Register): Contains flags like End of Conversion (EOC).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
STM32 HAL Functions for ADC
Simplified ADC Control

HAL_ADC_Init(): Initializes the ADC peripheral.
HAL_ADC_ConfigChannel(): Configures individual ADC channels.
HAL_ADC_Start(): Starts ADC conversion (polling mode).
HAL_ADC_PollForConversion(): Waits for conversion to complete.
HAL_ADC_GetValue(): Reads the converted digital value.
HAL_ADC_Start_IT(): Starts ADC conversion with interrupt.
HAL_ADC_ConvCpltCallback(): Callback for conversion complete interrupt.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Applications of ADC
Interacting with the Real World

Temperature Sensors: NTC thermistors, LM35.
Light Sensors: Photoresistors (LDRs).
Potentiometers: User input, volume control.
Pressure Sensors: Barometers.
Humidity Sensors: Capacitive or resistive types.
Microphones: Sound input.
Battery Voltage Monitoring: Check power levels.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Week 10 - Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)
text:
Conclusion: Week 10 Theory
Key Takeaways

ADC: Converts analog signals to digital values.
Principles: Sampling, Quantization, Encoding.
Parameters: Resolution, Sampling Rate, Accuracy.
Configuration: Clocking, Channels, Conversion Modes, Data Alignment.
Reading Data: Convert raw ADC values to meaningful units.
Applications: Interfacing with a wide range of analog sensors.

Looking Ahead: Lab 9

Reading the Analog World: Read analog inputs from a potentiometer and a light sensor (LDR).
Use the ADC value to control the brightness of an LED.

Questions & Discussion
Your Turn

Open floor for any questions or clarifications.

Thank You!
Next Steps

title: EE5203 L11 Lab Guide
text:
The disassembly view — your C, as the machine sees it - Basri Erdogan

Mission: Run your own L10 project one instruction at a time: the load-compute-store of your ADC math, the branch of your night-light if, the bl into a call—every step predicted first, every prediction checked in the Registers view.




Re-read the theory slides up to “BL and the way home.”
Bring your working L10 project. No new hardware—today’s instrument is the debugger.
Know the answer to: after movs r3, #5 then lsls r3, r3, #2, what is r3?
Know which register carries a function’s first argument—and its result.




By checkoff time, you can:

drive the Disassembly view and single-instruction stepping;
map a C statement to its -O0 instructions, load-compute-store;
predict r0–r3, sp, lr, pc, and the flags before each step;
explain what -O2 did to one of your functions.











Item
Where




Project
your L10_ADC, debug build (-O0 — the default Debug config)


Views
Disassembly View (right-click → Open Disassembly View), PERIPHERALS/SFR, MEMORY


Stepping
debug toolbar: Instruction Stepping Mode (the i→ button)


Registers
expand Core registers; changed values highlight after each step



Start a debug session, let it pause at main, dock the Disassembly view beside the editor. With Instruction Stepping Mode on, Step Into moves one instruction; the highlighted line tracks pc.



Breakpoint on your millivolt line: mv_pot = (raw_pot * 3300u) / 4095u;. Step through it by instructions.
Evidence for Checkpoint A: you point at the load-compute-store shape in the listing; before each step you name the register about to change, then the Registers view confirms it; pc advances by 2—and by 4 across one .w instruction you point out.



Breakpoint on a comparison of yours (night-light threshold, or the servo clamp). Step to the cmp.
Evidence for Checkpoint B: xpsr before and after the cmp—which flags changed; the branch that follows (bls/bhi/bcs…) matched to your C condition, and the unsigned family explained from your uint32_t types; both paths demonstrated live (turn the knob / cover the LDR, run to the breakpoint again).



Breakpoint just before adc_read(ADC_CHANNEL_0). Step into it by instructions.
Evidence for Checkpoint C: the argument appears in r0 before the bl; the bl writes lr and teleports pc; the push {r7, lr} drops sp by 8—you find the saved return address at the new sp in the Memory view; the result rides home in r0; pop {…, pc} lands execution back at the call site.
Then the optimizer: in a copy of the project (or a second build config), set Properties → C/C++ Build → Settings → Optimization to -O2, rebuild, and find the same function in the Disassembly view.



Pick one, predict first:

The one-instruction HAL: step into __HAL_TIM_SET_COMPARE(...) at -O0—show that the “function” is a single str. Where did it go at -O2?
The literal pool: find the .word holding a peripheral address after one of your functions, and the ldr with [pc, #…] that fetches it.
The vanished call: write uint32_t twice(uint32_t x){return 2*x;}, call it with a constant, build at -O2—find what remains of it.





Instruction stepping shown; pc, sp, lr roles explained live.
One C statement mapped instruction-by-instruction, predictions first.
cmp + branch matched to your if; flag changes shown in xpsr.
A call traced end to end: r0 in, lr, sp, r0 out, pop {…, pc}.
The same function compared at -O0 vs -O2, differences explained.





What is the difference between r3 and [r3]?
add versus adds—what changes, and who reads it?
What exactly does pop {r7, pc} do?
Why did your uint32_t comparison compile to bls, not ble?
The program hard-faults at pc = 0x08001a32. What is your first move?




Submit two screenshots—Disassembly + Registers mid-step at Checkpoint A, and the -O0 vs -O2 listing of your compared function—plus a six-row trace table for your millivolt statement: instruction, register changed, predicted value, observed value.



Walk the chain: paused in a live debug session? Then: Disassembly view empty — the target is running; pause first; steps move whole C lines — Instruction Stepping Mode is off; registers greyed out — same cause; cannot find your function at -O2 — it was inlined or folded into a constant: look inside its caller; the highlighted source line jumps around at -O2 — that is the optimizer reordering, not a debugger bug; compare against the -O0 build.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
section: Welcome to Lecture 11: The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Welcome to Lecture 11: The Bridge to Assembly
Understanding the Machine’s Language

This week, we explore assembly language.
It’s the low-level language directly understood by the processor.
Understanding assembly provides deep insights into how your C code executes.

Learning Objectives for Lecture 11

Understand why it’s important to know assembly language.
Differentiate between RISC and CISC architectures.
Get a high-level overview of the ARM instruction set.
Learn about Load/Store, Data Processing, and Branch instructions.
Understand how C code maps to assembly language.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
What is Assembly Language?
The Next Step Down

Assembly Language: A low-level programming language.
Uses mnemonics (short codes) to represent machine code instructions.
Each assembly instruction typically corresponds to one machine instruction.
Specific to a particular processor architecture (e.g., ARM, x86).

Visual Suggestion

Diagram: A pyramid showing layers of abstraction: High-level (C) -> Assembly -> Machine Code.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Why Understand Assembly?
The Deeper Dive

Debugging: Essential for understanding complex bugs, especially in embedded systems.

When C code doesn’t behave as expected.
Analyzing crashes or unexpected behavior.

Optimization: Fine-tune code for performance or memory footprint.

Identify bottlenecks.
Write highly optimized routines (e.g., for critical timing).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
More Reasons to Learn Assembly
System-Level Understanding

Hardware Interaction: Directly manipulate CPU registers and peripherals.
Reverse Engineering: Analyze existing binaries.
Security: Understand vulnerabilities and exploits.
Boot-up Process: Initializing the microcontroller from reset.
Compiler Behavior: See how your C code is translated.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
RISC vs. CISC: An Overview
Two Philosophies of CPU Design

RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer):

Simple, fixed-length instructions.
Few addressing modes.
Emphasis on software complexity (compiler does more work).
ARM is a RISC architecture.

CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer):

Complex, variable-length instructions.
Many addressing modes.
Emphasis on hardware complexity (CPU does more work).
Example: Intel x86 processors.


Visual Suggestion

Infographic: Side-by-side comparison of RISC and CISC characteristics.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
RISC Characteristics (ARM)
Simplicity and Speed

Simple Instructions: Each instruction performs a single, basic operation.
Fixed-Length Instructions: Easier for the CPU to fetch and decode.
Load/Store Architecture: Only specific instructions access memory.
Many General-Purpose Registers: Reduces memory access, improves speed.
Pipelining: Instructions can be processed in stages, improving throughput.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
ARM Instruction Set: High-Level Look
What the Processor Can Do

The ARM instruction set is the complete list of commands the ARM processor understands.
We’ll focus on the main categories:

Data Transfer Instructions
Data Processing Instructions
Branch Instructions


Visual Suggestion

Simplified diagram of the ARM instruction set categories.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Data Transfer Instructions
Moving Data Around

These instructions move data between registers, or between registers and memory.
Load/Store Architecture: A key RISC principle.

Data must be loaded into registers before processing.
Results must be stored back to memory from registers.


Common Instructions

LDR (Load Register): Copies data from memory to a register.
STR (Store Register): Copies data from a register to memory.
MOV (Move): Copies data from one register to another, or an immediate value to a register.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
LDR and STR Examples
Memory Access
; LDR R0, [R1]   ; Load value from memory address in R1 into R0
; STR R0, [R2]   ; Store value from R0 into memory address in R2
; LDR R0, [R1, #4] ; Load value from address (R1 + 4) into R0
; STR R0, [R1, #8]! ; Store value from R0 into address (R1 + 8), then update R1
Visual Suggestion

Diagram: Illustrate LDR and STR operations between registers and memory locations.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
MOV Example
Register to Register / Immediate to Register
; MOV R0, R1     ; Copy value from R1 to R0
; MOV R0, #10    ; Move immediate value 10 into R0
; MOV R0, #0xFF  ; Move hexadecimal value 255 into R0

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Data Processing Instructions
Performing Calculations and Logic

These instructions perform arithmetic, logical, and shift operations.
They typically operate on values held in registers.
Results are usually stored back into a register.

Categories

Arithmetic Instructions
Logical Instructions
Shift Instructions

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Arithmetic Instructions
Basic Math Operations

ADD: Addition
SUB: Subtraction
MUL: Multiplication
SDIV: Signed Division
UDIV: Unsigned Division

Examples
; ADD R0, R1, R2  ; R0 = R1 + R2
; SUB R3, R4, #5  ; R3 = R4 - 5
; MUL R0, R1, R2  ; R0 = R1 * R2

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Logical Instructions
Bitwise Operations

AND: Bitwise AND
ORR: Bitwise OR
EOR: Bitwise XOR
BIC: Bit Clear (AND with NOT)
MVN: Move Not (Bitwise NOT)

Examples
; AND R0, R0, #0xFF ; Mask lower 8 bits of R0
; ORR R1, R1, #0x01 ; Set bit 0 of R1
; EOR R2, R2, #0x80 ; Toggle bit 7 of R2

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Shift Instructions
Manipulating Bits

LSL (Logical Shift Left): Shifts bits left, fills with zeros.
LSR (Logical Shift Right): Shifts bits right, fills with zeros.
ASR (Arithmetic Shift Right): Shifts bits right, preserves sign bit.
ROR (Rotate Right): Rotates bits, including carry flag.

Examples
; LSL R0, R0, #2  ; R0 = R0 * 4
; LSR R1, R1, #1  ; R1 = R1 / 2 (unsigned)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Branch Instructions
Changing the Flow of Execution

These instructions alter the Program Counter (PC) to jump to a different part of the code.

Types

Unconditional Branches: Always jump.
Conditional Branches: Jump only if certain conditions (flags in APSR) are met.
Branch with Link: Used for function calls.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Unconditional Branches
Always Jump

B (Branch): Jumps to a specified label or address.

Example
; Loop_Start:
;   ... code ...
;   B Loop_Start  ; Jump back to Loop_Start

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Conditional Branches
Jumping Based on Conditions

These instructions check the condition flags (N, Z, C, V) in the APSR.
Only branch if the condition is true.

Common Conditional Branches

BEQ: Branch if Equal (Z flag set)
BNE: Branch if Not Equal (Z flag clear)
BGT: Branch if Greater Than (signed)
BLT: Branch if Less Than (signed)
BCS / BHS: Branch if Carry Set / Higher or Same (unsigned)
BCC / BLO: Branch if Carry Clear / Lower (unsigned)

Example
; CMP R0, #0      ; Compare R0 with 0, sets Z flag if R0 == 0
; BEQ Label_Zero  ; Branch to Label_Zero if R0 was 0

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Branch with Link (BL)
Function Calls

BL (Branch with Link): Used to call subroutines (functions).
It performs a branch to the target address.
Crucially, it saves the address of the next instruction into the Link Register (LR).
The subroutine can then return by branching to the address in LR.

Example
; BL MyFunction   ; Call MyFunction, save return address in LR
; ...             ; Execution continues here after MyFunction returns

; MyFunction:
;   ... code ...
;   BX LR         ; Return to caller (branch to address in LR)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
section: How C Code Maps to Assembly
text:
How C Code Maps to Assembly
The Compiler’s Role

The C compiler is responsible for translating your high-level C code into low-level assembly instructions.
This translation is complex and involves many optimizations.
Understanding this mapping helps in debugging and optimizing embedded code.

Visual Suggestion

Diagram: A C code snippet on one side, with arrows pointing to corresponding assembly instructions on the other side.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
section: C to Assembly Example: Simple Addition

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
section: C to Assembly Example: if Statement

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
section: C to Assembly Example: Function Call

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Conclusion: Lecture 11 Theory
Key Takeaways

Assembly: Low-level language, crucial for debugging and optimization.
RISC vs. CISC: ARM is RISC (simple, fixed-length, load/store).
ARM Instruction Set: Load/Store, Data Processing, Branch instructions.
C to Assembly Mapping: Compiler translates C code into these instructions.

Looking Ahead: Lab 10

The Disassembly View: Use the debugger’s disassembly window.
Step through C code and observe corresponding assembly instructions.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Questions & Discussion
Your Turn

Open floor for any questions or clarifications.

Visual Suggestion

Image: A question mark icon or a group of people discussing.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 11 - The Bridge to Assembly
text:
Thank You!
Next Steps

Next Session: Lab 10 - The Disassembly View
Preparation: Review basic ARM assembly instructions.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
section: Welcome to Lecture 12 Lab: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Welcome to Lecture 12 Lab: Analog Sensors & LCDs
Interfacing with the Physical World

This lab focuses on reading analog signals and displaying information on visual interfaces.
You will read temperature data from an LM35 sensor using the ADC.
You will display this data on a 16x2 LCD connected via I2C.
You will also establish a control loop to manage the LCD from your PC via UART.

Learning Objectives for Lab 12

Configure and use the ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) to read sensor data.
Interface with a 16x2 LCD using an I2C Backpack.
Implement UART communication for both data logging and device control.
Create a system that integrates sensing, processing, and multiple display outputs.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Required Resources
Hardware

Nucleo-F401RE Board: Our core development board.
Solderless Breadboard: For prototyping circuits.
LM35 Temperature Sensor: Analog temperature sensor (10mV/°C).
16x2 LCD with I2C Backpack: Character display.
Jumper Wires: For connections.

Software

VS Code + STM32CubeMX: Development environment.
Serial Terminal Software: PuTTY, Tera Term, etc.

Documents

STM32F401RE Datasheet & Reference Manual.
LM35 Datasheet.
LCD I2C Library / Datasheet.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Wiring Diagram: LM35 & LCD



Connecting the LM35

VCC: Connect to 5V (or 3.3V if supported, 5V recommended for linearity).
GND: Connect to GND.
Vout: Connect to an Analog Input Pin (e.g., PA0 / ADC1_IN0).

Connecting the LCD (I2C)

GND: Connect to GND.
VCC: Connect to 5V.
SDA: Connect to I2C SDA (e.g., PB9 or PB7).
SCL: Connect to I2C SCL (e.g., PB8 or PB6).







LM35 and LCD Wiring

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
LM35 Datasheet

LM35 Datasheet

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Code Blueprint: Key HAL Functions
ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)

HAL_ADC_Start(): Starts the ADC conversion.
HAL_ADC_PollForConversion(): Waits for conversion to complete.
HAL_ADC_GetValue(): Reads the converted digital value.

I2C (For LCD)

HAL_I2C_Master_Transmit(): Sends commands/data to the LCD backpack.

UART (Serial Communication)

HAL_UART_Transmit(): Sends temperature data to PC.
HAL_UART_Receive() / HAL_UART_Receive_IT(): Receives commands from PC.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Step-by-Step: Project Setup & Configuration
1. CubeMX Configuration

ADC1: Enable IN0 (or your chosen pin).
I2C1: Enable in I2C mode. Check pins (e.g., PB8/PB9).
USART2: Enable Asynchronous mode (115200 baud).
Clock: Set HCLK to 84 MHz.
NVIC Settings: Enable USART2 global interrupt in the NVIC Settings tab.
Generate Code.

2. LCD Library Integration

You will need a simple I2C LCD library (functions like lcd_init(), lcd_send_string(), lcd_clear()).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Understanding the LCD I2C Protocol

How the Code Works

I2C Expander (PCF8574): The LCD is connected to an 8-bit I/O expander.
4-Bit Mode: We send data in two 4-bit chunks (nibbles) because the expander only has 8 pins (4 for data, 4 for control).

Upper Nibble (D4-D7): Sent first.
Lower Nibble (D0-D3): Sent second.


Bit Mapping (Byte sent to I2C)













Bit 7
Bit 6
Bit 5
Bit 4
Bit 3
Bit 2
Bit 1
Bit 0




D7
D6
D5
D4
Backlight
EN
RW
RS




RS (Register Select): 0 = Command, 1 = Data.
RW (Read/Write): 0 = Write.
EN (Enable): Pulse High \(\to\) Low to latch data.
Backlight: 1 = On.

Transmission Sequence (Per Nibble)

Set Data & Control: Data on D4-D7, EN=1, Backlight=1.
Latch: Change EN to 0 (Data is captured by LCD).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Task 2: UART Control Center

Objective
Control the LCD parameters from the PC via UART.
Commands to Implement

Display Message: Typing a string should show it on the LCD.
Clear Screen: Sending a specific command (e.g., #CLEAR) should clear the LCD.

Implementation Logic

Use UART Interrupts (HAL_UART_Receive_IT) to receive data without blocking the temperature loop.
Callback (HAL_UART_RxCpltCallback):

Store received character in a buffer.
If ‘Enter’ (\n or \r) is received, process the buffer.
If buffer == “#CLEAR”: Call lcd_clear().
Else: Call lcd_clear(), then lcd_send_string(buffer).

Restart reception: HAL_UART_Receive_IT(...).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
How to Send Commands (Task 2)
PC Side Instructions (VS Code)

Open the Serial Monitor: use VS Code’s SERIAL MONITOR tab (bottom panel).
New Connection: Click the “Open Console” icon (monitor with a plus sign) and select Command Shell Console.
Connection Type: Select Serial Port as the connection type.
Settings:

Connection Name: Give it a name (e.g., “Nucleo”).
Serial Port: Select the COM port connected to your board.
Baud Rate: Set to 115200.
Data/Parity/Stop: 8 Bits, None, 1 Stop Bit.

Usage: Type commands in the input field and press Enter.

Type text to display on LCD.
Type #CLEAR to clear the screen.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Debugging & Common Pitfalls
ADC Issues

Unstable Readings: Use a capacitor (e.g., 100nF) between Vout and GND of LM35. Use multiple samples and average them.
Wrong Voltage: Ensure you use 3.3 in your calculation if VREF+ is 3.3V.

I2C LCD Issues

No Text: Check contrast potentiometer on the back of the I2C backpack.
Wrong Address: Default is often 0x27 or 0x3F. Scan for address if unsure.
Pull-ups: Ensure SDA/SCL have pull-up resistors (often built-in to backpack).

UART Issues

Garbage Data: Check Baud Rate (115200).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Bonus Challenges (Optional)
1. UART Echo (Bidirectional)

Goal: Verify the Nucleo received the correct message.
Task: Modify the code to send the received string back to the PC Terminal immediately after displaying it.
Hint: Use HAL_UART_Transmit inside the if(lcd_needs_update) block.

2. LED Command Control

Goal: Control hardware via text commands.
Task: Add support for #ON and #OFF commands to turn the User LED (PA5) High or Low.
Hint: Add else if statements with strcmp to check rx_buffer and use HAL_GPIO_WritePin.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Deliverables
Demonstration

Temp Monitor: Show real-time temperature updates on LCD and Serial Terminal. Heat the sensor (touch it) to see changes.
Remote Control: Type a message on your PC and show it appearing on the LCD. Type #CLEAR to wipe it.

Submission

main.c: Fully commented.
lcd_i2c.c/.h: Your LCD driver files.
Short Video: Demonstrating both tasks.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Questions?
Open Floor

Any questions about ADC, I2C, or the logic?

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Lab 12: Analog Sensors & LCDs
text:
Thank You!
Next Steps

Complete the lab tasks.
Submit your deliverables by the deadline.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
section: Welcome to Lecture 12: Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Welcome to Lecture 12: Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
High-Speed Peripheral Communication

This week, we explore synchronous serial communication protocols.
We will focus on I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) and SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface).

Learning Objectives for Lecture 12

Understand the principles of synchronous serial communication.
Learn the I2C (2-wire) protocol: master/slave, addressing, data transfer.
Learn the SPI (4-wire) protocol: master/slave, clock polarity/phase, data transfer.
Compare and contrast I2C and SPI.
Explore common use cases for interfacing with modern sensors and peripherals.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Recap: Asynchronous vs. Synchronous Serial
Timing is Key

Asynchronous (e.g., UART): No shared clock. Relies on agreed-upon baud rate and start/stop bits.
Synchronous (e.g., I2C, SPI): Uses a shared clock signal to synchronize data transfer.

Typically faster and more reliable over short distances.






Asynchronous vs synchronous timing

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Synchronous Serial Communication
Shared Clock for Synchronization

In synchronous serial communication, a dedicated clock line is used.
The clock signal dictates when data bits are valid and should be read.
Ensures both sender and receiver are perfectly in sync.

Advantages

Higher data rates.
More reliable over short distances.
No need for start/stop bits or baud rate matching (just clock frequency).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit) Protocol
The 2-Wire Bus

Developed by Philips (now NXP).
A multi-master, multi-slave serial bus.
Uses only two bidirectional (half duplex) lines:

SDA (Serial Data Line): For data transfer.
SCL (Serial Clock Line): For clock synchronization.






I2C two-wire bus with pull-up resistors

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
I2C: Master and Slave
Roles on the Bus

Master: Initiates communication, generates the clock (SCL), and addresses slaves.

Can be a microcontroller.

Slave: Responds to the master’s address and performs actions as requested.

Can be sensors, EEPROMs, LCDs, etc.


Multi-Master Capability

Multiple masters can exist on the same bus.
Arbitration mechanism prevents conflicts if multiple masters try to communicate simultaneously.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text: I2C: Data Transfer

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Start and Stop Conditions

Start Condition: SDA goes from HIGH to LOW while SCL is HIGH.

Initiates communication.

Stop Condition: SDA goes from LOW to HIGH while SCL is HIGH.

Terminates communication.






Start and Stop Conditions

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Data Transfer

Data bits are transferred on SDA while SCL is LOW.
Data is sampled when SCL is HIGH.
Typically 8 data bits followed by an ACK/NACK bit.





Single Byte Data Transfer

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
I2C: Addressing
Unique Identification

Each slave device on the I2C bus has a unique 7-bit (or 10-bit) address.
The master sends the slave’s address to initiate communication.
The address is followed by a Read/Write bit (0 for Write, 1 for Read).

Acknowledge (ACK) / Not Acknowledge (NACK)

After each byte transferred (address or data), the receiver sends an ACK (LOW) or NACK (HIGH) signal.
ACK indicates successful reception.
NACK indicates an error or that the receiver is not ready.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
I2C: Write Sequence
Writing to a Slave


Sequence: Start → Address(W) → ACK → Register Address → ACK → Data → ACK → Stop.
Explanation: The master initiates communication with a start condition, sends the slave address with the write flag, receives an ACK, then transmits the register address to be written, receives an ACK, sends the data byte(s) each followed by an ACK, and finally issues a stop condition to release the bus.






I2C Write Sequence

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
I2C: Read Sequence
Reading from a Slave


Sequence: Start → Address(W) → ACK → Register Address → ACK → Repeated Start → Address(R) → ACK → Data → NACK → Stop.
Explanation: The master first writes the target register address using a write transaction, then issues a repeated start to switch to read mode, sends the slave address with the read flag, receives an ACK, reads the data byte(s) from the slave, sends a NACK after the final byte to signal the end of the read, and finally issues a stop condition.






I2C Read Sequence

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
I2C: Pull-up Resistors
Essential for Bus Operation

Both SDA and SCL lines are open-drain.
Require external pull-up resistors to pull the lines HIGH when no device is driving them LOW.
Typically 4.7kΩ or 10kΩ, depending on bus capacitance and speed.


I2C bus with pull-up resistors on SDA/SCL

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Open-Drain for Bidirectional Communication


How it Works


Logic 0 (Low): The transistor turns ON, connecting the line to Ground (GND).
Logic 1 (High): The transistor turns OFF. The external pull-up resistor pulls the line to VCC.
Bidirectional: Multiple devices can drive the line LOW without short circuits (Wired-AND).
Safety: If two devices drive opposite logic levels (one High, one Low), the result is Low, preventing damage.







Open-Drain Output Structure

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) Protocol
The 4-Wire Bus

A full-duplex, synchronous serial communication interface.
Uses four lines:

MOSI (Master Out Slave In): Data from Master to Slave.
MISO (Master In Slave Out): Data from Slave to Master.
SCK (Serial Clock): Clock signal generated by the Master.
SS/CS (Slave Select/Chip Select): Selects the active slave device.






SPI Schematic

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
SPI Connection: MOSI
Master Out Slave In

Function: Carries data from the Master to the Slave.
Direction: Driven by Master -> Read by Slave.
Alternative Names: SIMO, SDO (on Master), SDI (on Slave), DO, DI.





SPI MOSI Highlight

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
SPI Connection: MISO
Master In Slave Out

Function: Carries data from the Slave to the Master.
Direction: Driven by Slave -> Read by Master.
Alternative Names: SOMI, SDI (on Master), SDO (on Slave), DI, DO.
Note: High-Impedance (Hi-Z) when the slave is not selected.





SPI MISO Highlight

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
SPI Connection: SCK
Serial Clock

Function: Synchronizes data transmission.
Direction: Generated by Master -> Read by Slave.
Alternative Names: SCLK, CLK.
Operation: One bit is shifted out/in on each clock edge.





SPI SCK Highlight

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
SPI Connection: SS/CS
Slave Select / Chip Select

Function: Selects the target slave device for communication.
Direction: Driven by Master -> Read by Slave.
Alternative Names: nCS, CSB, CSN, EN.
Active Low: Typically, the line is pulled LOW to select the device.





SPI SS Highlight

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
SPI: Master and Slave
Dedicated Roles

Master: Always initiates communication, generates the clock (SCK), and selects the slave (SS/CS).

Typically a microcontroller.

Slave: Responds only when selected by the master.

Can be sensors, SD cards, displays, ADCs, DACs.


Single Master, Multiple Slaves

Only one master can exist on the bus.
Multiple slaves can be connected, each requiring a separate SS/CS line from the master.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
SPI: Data Transfer
Full-Duplex Operation

Data is transferred simultaneously in both directions (Master to Slave on MOSI, Slave to Master on MISO).
Data is shifted out and in on each clock edge.
No start/stop bits or addressing; relies on SS/CS for device selection.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
section: SPI: Clock Polarity (CPOL) and Clock Phase (CPHA)
text:
SPI: Clock Polarity (CPOL) and Clock Phase (CPHA)
Synchronizing Data

These two parameters define how data is sampled relative to the clock signal.
There are four SPI modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3) based on CPOL and CPHA combinations.



CPOL (Clock Polarity)

0: SCK is LOW when idle.
1: SCK is HIGH when idle.


CPHA (Clock Phase)

0: Data is sampled on the first clock edge.
1: Data is sampled on the second clock edge.


Importance

Master and slave must use the same SPI mode for successful communication.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
SPI Modes
Visualizing the 4 Modes

Mode 0 (CPOL=0, CPHA=0): Clock idle Low, sample on rising edge.
Mode 1 (CPOL=0, CPHA=1): Clock idle Low, sample on falling edge.
Mode 2 (CPOL=1, CPHA=0): Clock idle High, sample on falling edge.
Mode 3 (CPOL=1, CPHA=1): Clock idle High, sample on rising edge.





SPI Modes Timing Diagram

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Comparison: I2C vs. SPI
Key Differences








Feature
I2C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)
SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)




Wires
2 (SDA, SCL)
4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS/CS)


Speed
Up to ~3.4 Mbps (Standard: 100/400kbps)
Up to tens of Mbps (faster)


Duplex
Half-duplex (data on one line)
Full-duplex (simultaneous TX/RX)


Master/Slave
Multi-master, Multi-slave
Single master, Multi-slave


Addressing
Yes (7-bit/10-bit slave address)
No (uses SS/CS for device selection)


Complexity
More complex protocol (ACK/NACK)
Simpler protocol


Pull-ups
Required on SDA/SCL
Not required (push-pull drivers)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
When to Use I2C?
Best Use Cases

Multiple Low-Speed Devices: Ideal for connecting many sensors or small peripherals to a single microcontroller.
Short Distance: Typically used for on-board communication.
Simple Control: Reading sensor data, controlling LCDs, EEPROMs.
Limited Pins: When pin count is a constraint (only 2 wires).

Examples

Temperature/Humidity sensors (e.g., BME280)
Real-Time Clocks (RTCs)
EEPROM memory chips
OLED/LCD displays

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
When to Use SPI?
Best Use Cases

High-Speed Data Transfer: Ideal for applications requiring fast data streaming.
Dedicated Communication: Point-to-point communication between master and selected slave.
Full-Duplex Needs: When simultaneous sending and receiving is required.
Displays & Memory: Driving graphical displays, communicating with SD cards, Flash memory.

Examples



SD card readers
Flash memory chips
LCD/OLED displays (esp. graphical ones)



ADCs/DACs (high-speed)
MPU-9250 (IMU sensor)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
STM32 I2C Configuration (CubeMX)
Graphical Setup




Enable I2C Peripheral Clock: Select the desired I2C (e.g., I2C1).
Configure Mode: Set Mode to I2C.
Pin Configuration: CubeMX automatically assigns SDA/SCL pins (e.g., PB9 for I2C1_SDA, PB8 for I2C1_SCL).
Parameter Settings:

Speed Mode: Standard, Fast, Fast Mode Plus.
Addressing Mode: 7-bit or 10-bit.
General Call Mode: Enable/Disable.







STM32CubeMX I2C Configuration

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
STM32 SPI Configuration (CubeMX)
Graphical Setup



Enable SPI Peripheral Clock: Select the desired SPI (e.g., SPI1).
Configure Mode: Set Mode to Full-Duplex Master.
Pin Configuration: CubeMX automatically assigns MOSI, MISO, SCK, NSS (SS) pins.
Parameter Settings:

Frame Format: Motorola, TI.
Data Size: 8-bit, 16-bit.
Clock Polarity (CPOL): High/Low.
Clock Phase (CPHA): 1 Edge/2 Edge.
Prescaler: To set SPI clock speed.






STM32CubeMX SPI Configuration

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Use Cases: Interfacing Sensors


BME280 (I2C Example)

Sensor: Barometric pressure, ambient temperature, humidity sensor.
Communication: Typically I2C.
Usage: Read sensor registers to get environmental data.

MPU-9250 (SPI Example)

Sensor: 9-axis Motion Tracking device (Accelerometer, Gyroscope, Magnetometer).
Communication: Can use I2C or SPI (SPI often preferred for higher data rates).
Usage: Read sensor registers to get motion data.






BME280 and MPU-9250 Modules

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
BME280 Datasheet Insights
Key Interface Details

I2C Address: Determined by SDO pin.

SDO to GND \(\rightarrow\) Address 0x76 (Shifted: 0xEC).
SDO to VDD \(\rightarrow\) Address 0x77 (Shifted: 0xEE).

Important Registers:

0xD0: Chip ID (Value should be 0x60).
0xF4: ctrl_meas (Controls Mode, Oversampling).
0xF7 - 0xFC: Data Registers (Pressure & Temperature).
0x88 - 0xA1: Trimming Parameters (Required for calculation).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Understanding I2C Burst Read
Efficiency Matters

Auto-Increment: Most I2C sensors automatically increment their internal register pointer after each byte read.
Single Transaction: Instead of sending Start -> Address -> Register -> Stop for every byte, we send the start address once.
Efficiency: Reading 6 bytes in one go is much faster and reduces bus overhead compared to 6 separate 1-byte reads.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
MPU-9250 Datasheet Insights
Key Interface Details

SPI Protocol: Supports SPI Mode 0 or Mode 3.
Read/Write Logic: MSB of the address byte determines direction.

Read: Set bit 7 (Address | 0x80).
Write: Clear bit 7.

Important Registers:

0x75: WHO_AM_I (Value should be 0x71).
0x6B: PWR_MGMT_1 (Device starts in Sleep! Write 0x00 to wake).
0x3B - 0x40: Accelerometer Data (X, Y, Z High/Low bytes).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Understanding SPI Burst Read
Continuous Clocking

Keep CS Low: As long as Chip Select is held LOW and the clock is toggling, the sensor will continue to shift out data from subsequent registers.
Multi-Byte Read: We requested 6 bytes. The MPU-9250 sends ACCEL_X_H, then ACCEL_X_L, ACCEL_Y_H, etc., automatically incrementing the address.
16-bit Data: Sensor data is often 16-bit. We combine the High byte (MSB) and Low byte (LSB) into a single int16_t variable.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 12 - Synchronous Serial: I2C & SPI
text:
Conclusion: Lecture 12 Theory
Key Takeaways

Synchronous Serial: Uses a shared clock for data transfer.
I2C (2-wire): Multi-master, multi-slave, addressing, pull-ups required.
SPI (4-wire): Single master, multi-slave, full-duplex, faster, uses SS/CS.
CPOL/CPHA: Define SPI communication mode.
Applications: Interfacing with a wide range of sensors, memory, displays.

Looking Ahead: Lab 12

Talking to a Sensor: Interface with a sensor using a synchronous serial protocol (I2C or SPI).
Display data via UART.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
Source

Extracted from EE5203_Week10_Theory.pptx (DMA slides)
Visuals are extracted into images/week10_dma/

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text: Direct Memory Access (DMA)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
What is DMA?

Technique for efficient data transfer between peripherals and memory (or within memory) without heavy CPU usage.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
How does DMA work?

Initiated by CPU, executed by the DMA controller.
CPU is free to perform other tasks during the transfer.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
Benefits of DMA

Efficiency: Frees CPU for other tasks, enhancing system performance.
Enhanced throughput: Useful for both slow and fast peripherals; improves data transfer rates.
Reduced interrupts: Minimizes interrupts in high-speed peripherals, reducing performance overhead.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
AHB Bus Connectivity

Connected to a bus matrix that uses a priority-based round-robin scheduling algorithm.
Ensures no modules block data transmission for extended periods.
Arbitrates conflicts during simultaneous access attempts.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
section: Bridge Between AHB and APB Buses
text:
Bridge Between AHB and APB Buses

A bridge is necessary to connect AHB and APB due to different communication protocols.
Buffers addresses, controls, and data to prevent data loss between the high-speed AHB and lower-speed APB.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
DMA Controllers on AHB Bus

Reside on the AHB bus, functioning as both bus master and bus slave.
Two ports:

A slave port for receiving data/commands from the processor
A master port for initiating transfers

Manage multiple channels, allowing simultaneous DMA transfers with dedicated interfaces to peripherals.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
Interfacing a Peripheral without DMA

Without DMA, the processor is fully occupied during the data transfer.
Two main operations:

Load data to CPU registers from the peripheral
Store data from CPU registers to memory

Uses busy-waiting (polling) or interrupts → can be inefficient in high-frequency applications.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
DMA Implementation in Peripheral Communication

Processor sets up DMA transfers by programming the DMA controller.
DMA channels respond to specific events like USART1 receive buffer not empty (RXNE).
The “write to” and “read from” operations are offloaded from the processor core to the DMA controller.
DMA controller can generate an interrupt at the end of each DMA transfer to inform the processor.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
DMA Transactions

STM32F4 has two DMA controllers, with eight channels in each.
Priorities between DMA stream channels are software-programmable.
Each DMA transfer consists of three operations:

Load data from peripheral data register or memory (via DMA_SxPAR / DMA_SxM0AR)
Store data to peripheral data register or memory (via DMA_SxPAR / DMA_SxM0AR)
Post-decrement DMA_SxNDTR (remaining number of transactions)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
Channel Selection

8 requests from peripherals (TIM, ADC, SPI, I2C, etc.) are independently connected to each channel.
Their connection depends on the product implementation.
Selection is controlled by the CHSEL[2:0] bits in DMA_SxCR.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
Programming DMA (Registers)

Each DMA stream has four registers:

Channel memory address register: DMA_SxM0AR (x = 0..7)
Channel peripheral address register: DMA_SxPAR
Channel number of data register: DMA_SxNDTR
Channel configuration register: DMA_SxCR

SxCR specifies:

Transfer direction, increment mode (memory/peripheral), circular mode, channel priority, etc.
Interrupt enables (transfer complete, half-transfer, transfer error)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
Programming DMA (Example: ADC → Memory)

Software must specify total number of data to transfer in the SxNDTR register.
Increment mode determines whether peripheral or memory address increments after each DMA transfer.

void DMA2_Init(void){
  RCC->AHB1ENR |= RCC_AHB1ENR_DMA2EN;        // Enable DMA2 clock
  DMA2_Stream0->CR &= ~DMA_SxCR_EN;          // Disable DMA Stream0
  DMA2_Stream0->CR &= ~DMA_SxCR_PSIZE;       // Clear peripheral data size control bits
  DMA2_Stream0->CR |= DMA_SxCR_PSIZE_0;      // Peripheral data size 01 = 16 bits
  DMA2_Stream0->CR &= ~DMA_SxCR_MSIZE;       // Clear memory data size control bits
  DMA2_Stream0->CR |= DMA_SxCR_MSIZE_0;      // Memory data size 01 = 16 bits
  DMA2_Stream0->CR &= ~DMA_SxCR_PINC;        // Disable peripheral increment mode
  DMA2_Stream0->CR |= DMA_SxCR_MINC;         // Enable memory increment mode
  DMA2_Stream0->CR &= ~DMA_SxCR_DIR;         // Transfer direction: to memory
  DMA2_Stream0->NDTR = 10;                   // Number of data to transfer
  DMA2_Stream0->PAR = (uint32_t)&ADC1->DR;   // Peripheral address
  DMA2_Stream0->M0AR = (uint32_t)Buffer;     // Receive buffer (memory) address
  DMA2_Stream0->CR &= ~DMA_SxCR_CHSEL;       // Map stream0 to channel0 (ADC1)
  DMA2_Stream0->CR |= DMA_SxCR_EN;           // Enable DMA Stream0
}

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
text:
DMA Circular Mode

The DMA channel can run in circular mode or normal mode.

If CIRC bit in SxCR is cleared → normal mode

For each DMA transfer, hardware decrements SxNDTR by 1; DMA stream is disabled once SxNDTR reaches 0.

If CIRC bit is set → circular mode

Used to implement a circular buffer.
After SxNDTR reaches 0, SxPAR, SxMAR, and SxNDTR are automatically reset to the originally programmed value.
Useful for continuous data streams.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)
EE5203 Microprocessors — DMA (Lecture 10 deck extract)

STM32F4 reference manual (RM0368)

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Lab: Final Project Development & Demonstrations
section: Welcome to Lecture 14 Lab: Final Project Development & Demonstrations
text:
Welcome to Lecture 14 Lab: Final Project Development & Demonstrations
Bringing Your Vision to Life

This lab session is dedicated to the final stages of your embedded systems project.
It’s a crucial time for development, debugging, and preparing for your final demonstration.

Learning Objectives for Lab 14

Complete the development and integration of your final project components.
Troubleshoot and debug any remaining issues in your embedded system.
Prepare a compelling demonstration of your project’s functionality.
Refine your presentation skills for the final project showcase.

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Lab: Final Project Development & Demonstrations
text:
Conceptual Background: Project Lifecycle
From Idea to Implementation

Planning: Defining requirements, selecting components, outlining architecture.
Design: Detailed schematics, software flowcharts, module breakdown.
Implementation: Coding, circuit assembly, integration of hardware/software.
Testing & Debugging: Iterative process of finding and fixing errors.
Documentation & Presentation: Explaining your work and demonstrating functionality.

Visual Suggestion

Your Final Project Hardware: All components, sensors, actuators, custom PCBs.
Nucleo-F401RE Board: As the core microcontroller platform.
Solderless Breadboard / Custom PCB: Your chosen prototyping/final platform.
All relevant datasheets and reference manuals: For your chosen components.

Software

VS Code + STM32CubeMX: Your primary development environment.
Any specific libraries or tools: Relevant to your project (e.g., Python scripts for data visualization, custom drivers).

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Lab: Final Project Development & Demonstrations
text:
Final Project Development Checklist
Ensuring Readiness

Hardware Assembly: Is everything securely connected and powered?
Component Testing: Have all individual sensors/actuators been tested?
Software Modules: Is each software module (e.g., ADC read, motor control, communication) working independently?
Integration: Do all modules work together seamlessly?
Error Handling: Does your code gracefully handle unexpected inputs or conditions?
Power Management: Is your system stable under continuous operation?

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Lab: Final Project Development & Demonstrations
text:
Debugging Strategies for Complex Systems
Beyond Simple Blinking

Modular Debugging: Test each component/module in isolation before integrating.
Print Debugging (UART): Use printf (via UART) to output variable values, state changes, and error messages.
LED Indicators: Use LEDs to signal different states or errors in your program.
Logic Analyzer/Oscilloscope: Essential for debugging communication protocols (I2C, SPI) or timing issues.
Debugger Views: Utilize all VS Code debug panels (PERIPHERALS/SFR, MEMORY, Disassembly View, Watch).

Visual Suggestion

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Lab: Final Project Development & Demonstrations

Preparing for Your Demonstration
Making an Impact

Clear Objectives: What is your project supposed to do?
Concise Explanation: Explain the core concept and functionality simply.
Highlight Key Features: What makes your project unique or effective?
Prepare for Questions: Anticipate potential questions about your design, challenges, and future work.
Practice Your Demo: Ensure it runs smoothly and reliably.

Visual Suggestion

title: EE5203 Microprocessors Lecture 14 - Lab: Final Project Development & Demonstrations

Final Project Demonstration Guidelines
What to Show

Live Functionality: Demonstrate your project working as intended.
User Interaction: Show how a user would interact with your system.
Key Features: Highlight the most important aspects of your project.
Robustness: Show that it can handle various inputs or conditions.
Problem Solving: Briefly discuss a significant challenge you overcame.
